reed-mace: A plant of the genus Typha.
ra: as an inseparable prefix or preposition, is the Latin re, coming to us through the Italian and French, and primarily signifying again
rabate: In falconry, to recover a hawk to the fist.
rabbet: To pare down the edge of a board or other piece of timber, for the purpose of receiving the edge of another piece by lapping and thus uniting the two.
To lap and unite the edges of boardsIn ship carpentry, to let the edge of a plank into the keel.
A cut on the side of a boardto fit it to another by lapping; a joint made by lapping boards
rabbeted: Pared down the edge of a board; united by a rabbet joint.
rabbeting: Paring down the edge of a board; uniting by a rabbet joint.
rabbet-plane: A joiner’s plane for paring or cutting square down the edge of a board
rabbi: A title assumed by the Jewish doctors, signifying master or lord. This title is not conferred by authority, but assumed or allowed by courtesy to learned men.
rabbin: A title assumed by the Jewish doctors, signifying master or lord. This title is not conferred by authority, but assumed or allowed by courtesy to learned men.
rabbinic: Pertaining to the Rabbins, or to their opinions, learning and language.
The language or dialect of the Rabbins; the later Hebrew.
rabbinical: Pertaining to the Rabbins, or to their opinions, learning and language.
rabbinism: A Rabbinic expression or phraseology; a peculiarity of the language of the Rabbins.
rabbinist: Among the Jews, one who adhered to the Talmud and the traditions of the Rabbins, in opposition to the Caraites, who rejected the traditions.
rabbit: A small quadruped of the genus Lepus, which feeds on grass or other herbage, and burrows in the earth. The rabbit is said to be less sagacious than the hare. It is a very prolific animal, and is kept in warrens for the sake of its flesh.
rabble: A tumultuous crowd of vulgar, noisy people; the mob; a confused disorderly crowd.
The lower class of people, without reference to an assembly; the dregs of the people.
rabblement: A tumultuous crowd of low people.
rabdology: A method of performing mathematical operations by little square rods.
rabid: Furious; raging; mad; as a rabid dog or wolf. It is particularly applied to animals of the canine genus, affected with the distemper called rabies, and whose bite communicates hydrophobia.
raca: A Syriac word signifying empty, beggarly, foolish; a term of extreme contempt. Matthew 5:22.
race: The lineage of a family, or continued series of descendants from a parent who is called the stock. A race is the series of descendants indefinitely. Thus all mankind are called the race of Adam; the Israelites are of the race of Abraham and Jacob. Thus we speak of a race of kings, the race of Clovis or Charlemagne; a race of nobles. Hence the long race of Alban fathers come.
A generation; a family of descendants. A race of youthful and unhandled colts.
A particular breed; as a race of mules; a race of horses; a race of sheep. Of such a race no matter who is king.
A root; as race-ginger, ginger in the root or not pulverized.
A particular strength or taste of wine; a kind of tartness.
race-ginger: Ginger in the root or not pulverized.
race-horse: A horse bred or kept for running in contest; a horse that runs in competition.
racemation: The cultivation of cluster of grapes.
raceme: In botany a species of inflorescence, consisting of a peduncle with short lateral branches. It is simple or compound, naked or leafy.
racemiferous: Bearing racemes or clusters; as the racemiferous fig-tree.
racer: a runner; one that contends in a race. And bade the nimblest racer seize the prize.
rack: An engine of torture, used for extorting confessions from criminals or suspected persons. The rack is entirely unknown in free countries.
Torture; extreme pain; anguish. A fit of the stone puts a king to the rack and makes him as miserable as it does the meanest subject.
Any instrument for stretching or extending any thing; as a rack for bending a bow.
A grate on which bacon is laid.
A wooden frame of open work in which hay is laid for horses and cattle for feeding.
racked: Tortured; tormented; strained to the utmost.
racker: One that tortures or torments; one that racks.
racket: A confused, clattering noise, less loud than uproar; applied to the confused sounds of animal voices, or such voices mixed with other sound. We say, the children make a racket; the racket of a flock of fowls.
To make a confused noise or clamor; to frolic.
The instrument with which players at tennis strike the ball.
To strike as with a racket.
racking: Torturing; tormenting; straining; drawing off. 2 .a. Tormenting; excruciating; as a racking pain.
Torture; a stretching on the rack.
Torment of the mind; anguish; as the rackings of conscience.
The act of stretching cloth on a frame for drying.
The act of drawing from the sediment, as liquors.
racking-pace: The racking-pace of a horse is an amble, but with a quicker and shorter tread.
rack-rent: An annual rent of the full value of the tenement or near it.
rack-rented: Subjected to the payment of rack-rent.
rack-renter: One that is subjected to pay rack-rent.
racoon: An American quadruped of the genus Ursus. It is somewhat larger than a fox, and its fur is deemed valuable, next to that of the beaver. This animal lodges in a hollow tree, feeds on vegetables, and its flesh is palatable food. It inhabits North America from Canada to the tropics.
racy: Strong; flavorous; tasting of the soil; as racy cider; racy wine. Rich racy verses, in which we the soil from which they come, taste, smell and see.
rad: an initial or terminating syllable in names.
red: an initial or terminating syllable in names.

Of a bright color, resembling blood. Red is a simple or primary color, but of several different shades or hues, as scarlet, crimson, vermilion, orange redWe say red color, red cloth, red flame, red eyes, red cheeks, red lead. Red book of the exchequer, an ancient English record or manuscript containing various treatises relating to the times before the conquest. Red men, red people, red children, the aboriginals of America, as distinguished from the whites.
A red color; as a brighter color, the best of all the reds.
rod: an initial or terminating syllable in names.
The shoot or long twig of any woody plant; a branch, or the stem of a shrub; as a rod of hazle, of birch, of oak or hickory. Hence,
An instrument of punishment or correction; chastisement. I will chasten him with the rod of men. 2 Samuel 7:14; Proverbs 10:13.
A king of scepter. The rod and bird of peace.
raddle: A long stick used in hedging; also, a hedge formed by interweaving the shoots and branches of trees or shrubs. J .
radial: Pertaining to the radius or to the fore arm of the human body; as the radial artery or nerve. The radial muscles are two muscles of the fore arm, one of which bends the wrist, the other extends it. Radial curves, in geometry, curves of the spiral kind, whose ordinates all terminate in the center of the including circle, and appear like so many semidiameters.
radiance: Properly, brightness shooting in rays or beams; hence in general, brilliant or sparkling luster; vivid brightness; as the radiance of the sun. The Son girt with omnipotence, with radiance crown’d of majesty divine.
radiancy: Properly, brightness shooting in rays or beams; hence in general, brilliant or sparkling luster; vivid brightness; as the radiance of the sun. The Son girt with omnipotence, with radiance crown’d of majesty divine.
radiant: Shooting or darting rays of light; beaming with brightness; emitting a vivid light or splendor; as the radiant sun. Mark what radiant state she spreads. Radiant in glittering arms and beamy pride.
In optics, the luminous point or object from which light emanates, that falls on a mirror or lens.
radiantly: With beaming brightness; with glittering splendor.
radiate: To issue in rays, as light; to dart, as beams of brightness; to shine. Light radiates from luminous bodies directly to our eyes.
To issue and proceed in direct lines from a point.
To enlighten; to illuminate; to shed light or brightness on.

In botany, a rayed or radiate corol or flower, is a compound flower consisting of a disk, in which the corollets or florets are tubular and regular, and of a ray, in which the florets are irregular. Or a flower with several semiflosculous florets set round a disk in form of a radiant star.
radiated: Having crystals diverging from a center.
radiating: Darting rays of light; enlightening; as the radiating point in optics.
radiation: The emission and diffusion of rays of light; beamy brightness.
The shooting of any thing from a center, like the diverging rays of light.
radical: Pertaining to the root or origin; original; fundamental; as a radical truth or error; a radical evil; a radical difference of opinions or systems.
Implanted by nature; native; constitutional; as the radical moisture of a body.
Primitive; original; underived; uncompounded; as a radical word.
In botany, proceeding immediately from the root; as a radical leaf or peduncle.
radicality: A being radical; a quantity which has relation to a root.
radically: Originally; at the origin or root; fundamentally; as a scheme or system radically wrong or defective.
Primitively; essentially; originally; without derivation. These great orbs thus radically bright.
radicalness: The state of being radical or fundamental.
radicant: In botany, rooting; as a radicant stem or leaf.
radicate: To root; to plant deply and firmly; as radicated opinions; radicated knowledge. Meditation will radicate these seeds.
or a. Deeply planted. Prejudices of a whole race of people radicated by a succession of ages.
radication: The process of taking root deeply; as the radication of habits.
In botany, the disposition of the root of a plant with respect to the ascending and descending caudex and the radicles.
radicle: That part of the seed of a plant which upon vegetating becomes the root.
The fibrous part of a root, by which the stock or main body of it is terminated.
radiometer: The forestaff, an instrument for taking the altitudes of celestial bodies.
radish: A plant of the genus Raphanus, the root of which is eaten raw. Horse-radish is of the genus Cochlearia. Water-radish is of the genus Sisymbrium./ .
radius: In geometry, a right line drawn or extending from the center of a circle to the periphery, and hence the semidiameter of the circle. In trigonometry, the radius is the whole sine, or sine of degrees.
In anatomy, the exterior bone of the fore arm, descending along with the ulna from the elbow to the wrist.
In botany, a ray; the outer part or circumference of a compound radiate flower, or radiated discous flower.
radix: In etymology, a primitive word from which spring other words.

In logarithms, the base of any system of logarithms, or that number whose logarithm is unity. Thus in Briggs’, or the common system of logarithms, the radix is 10; in Napier’s, it is 2.7182818284. All other numbers are considered as some powers or roots of the radix, the exponents of which powers or roots, constitute the logarithms of those numbers respectively.
In algebra, radix sometimes denotes the root of a finite expression, from which a series is derived.
raff: To sweep; to snatch, draw or huddle together; to take by a promiscuous sweep. Obs. Their causes and effects I thus raff up together.
The sweepings of society; the rabble; the mob. This is used chiefly in the compound or duplicate, riff-raff.
A promiscuous heap or collection; a jumble.
raffle: To cast dice for a prize, for which each person concerned in the game lays down a stake, or hazards a part of the value; as, to raffle for a watch.

A game of chance, or lottery in which several persons deposit a part of the value of the thing, in consideration of the chance of gaining it. The successful thrower of the dice takes or sweeps the whole.
raffling: The act of throwing dice for a prize staked by a number.
raft: An assemblage of boards, planks or pieces of timber fastened together horizontally and floated down a stream; a float.
rafter: A roof timber; a piece of timber that extends from the plate of a building to the ridge and serves to support the covering of the roof.
rag: Any piece of cloth torn from the rest; a tattered cloth, torn or worn till its texture is destroyed. Linen and cotton rags are the chief materials of paper.
Garments worn out; proverbially, mean dress. Drowsiness shall clothe a man with rags. Proverbs 23:21. And virtue, though in rags, will Keep me warm.
ragamuffin: A paltry fellow; a mean wretch.
rag-bolt: An iron pin with barbs on its shank to retain it in its place.
rage: Violent anger accompanied with furious words, gestures or agitation; anger excited to fury. Passion sometimes rises to rage. Torment and loud lament and furious rage.
Vehemence or violent exacerbation of any thing painful; as the rage of pain; the rage of a fever; the rage of hunger or thirst.
Fury; extreme violence; as the rage of a tempest.
Enthusiasm; rapture. Who brought green poesy to her perfect age, and made that art which was a rage.
Extreme eagerness or passion directed to some object; as the rage for money. You purchase pain with all that joy can give, and die of nothing but a rage to live.
ragg: Rowley ragg, a species of silicious stone, of a dusky or dark gray color, with shining crystals, of a granular texture, and by exposure to the air acquiring an ochery crust.
ragged: Rent or worn into tatters, or till its texture is broken; as a ragged coat; a ragged sail.
Broken with rough edges; uneven; as a ragged rock.
Having the appearance of being broken or torn; jagged; rough with sharp or irregular points. The moon appears, when looked upon through a good glass, rude and ragged.
Wearing tattered clothes; as a ragged fellow.
Rough; rugged. What shepherd owns those ragged sheep?
raggedness: The state of being dressed in tattered clothes.
The state of being rough or broken irregularly; as the raggedness of a cliff. NM .
raging: Acting with violence or fury. 2.a. Furious; impetuous; vehemently driven or agitated; as the raging sea or tempest.
ragman: A man who collects or deals in rags, the materials of paper.
ragoo: A sauce or seasoning for exciting a languid appetite; or a high seasoned dish, prepared with fish, flesh, greens and the like, stewed with salt, pepper, cloves
ragout: A sauce or seasoning for exciting a languid appetite; or a high seasoned dish, prepared with fish, flesh, greens and the like, stewed with salt, pepper, cloves
ragstone: A stone of the silicious kind, so named from its rough fracture. It is of a gray color, the texture obscurely laminar or rather fibrous, the lamins consisting of a congeries of grains of a quartzy appearance, coarse and rough. It effervesces with acids, and gives fire with steel. It is used for a whetstone without oil or water, for sharpening coarse cutting tools.
ragwort: A plant of the genus Senecio.
rail: A cross beam fixed at the ends in two upright posts.

In the United States, a piece of timber cleft, hewed or sawed, rough or smooth, inserted in upright posts for fencing. The common rails among farmers, are rough, being used as they are split from the chestnut or other trees. The rails used in fences of boards or pickets round gentlemen’s houses and gardens, are usually sawed scantling and often dressed with the plane.
A bar of wood or iron used for inclosing any place; the piece into which ballusters are inserted.

A series of posts connected with cross beams, by which a place is inclosed. In New England we never call this series a rail, but by the general term railing. In a picket fence, the pales or pickets rise above the rails; in a ballustrade, or fence resembling it, the ballusters usually terminate in the rails.

In a ship, a narrow plank nailed for ornament or security on a ship’s upper works; also, a curved piece of timber extending from the bows of a ship to the continuation of its stern, to support the knee of the head.
rail-bird: A bird of the genus Cuculus.
railer: One who scoffs, insults, censures or reproaches with opprobrious language.
railing: Clamoring with insulting language; uttering reproachful words.
a. Expressing reproach; insulting; as a railing accusation. 2 Peter 2:11.
A series of rails; a fence.
raillery: Banter; jesting language; good humored pleasantry or slight satire; satirical merriment. Let raillery be without malice or heat. - Studies employed on low objects; the very naming of them is sufficient to turn them into raillery.
railleur: A banterer; a jester; a mocker.
raiment: Clothing in general; vestments; vesture; garments. Genesis 24:53; Deuteronomy 8:4. Living, both food and raiment she supplies.
rain: To fall in drops from the clouds, as water; used mostly with it for a nominative; as, it rains; it will rain; it rained, or it has rained.
To fall or drop like rain; as, tears rained at their eyes.

To pour or shower down from the upper regions, like rain from the clouds. Then said the Lord to Moses, behold I will rain bread from heaven for you. Exodus 16:4. God shall cast the fury of his wrath upon him, and shall rain it upon him while he is eating. Job 20:23. Upon the wicked he shall rain snares, fire and brimstone, and horrible tempest. Psalm 11:6.

The descent of water in drops from the clouds; or the water thus falling. Rain is distinguished from mist, by the size of the drops, which are distinctly visible. When water falls in very small drops or particles, we call it mist, and fog is composed of particles so fine as to be not only indistinguishable, but to float or be suspended in the air.
rainbat: Beaten or injured by the rain.
rainbow: A bow, or an arch of a circle, consisting of all the colors formed by the refraction and reflection of rays of light from drops of rain or vapor, appearing in the part of the hemisphere opposite to the sun. When the sun is at the horizon, the rainbow is a semicircle. The rainbow is called also iris. The moon sometimes forms a bow or arch of light, more faint than that formed by the sun, and called lunar rainbow. Similar bows at sea are called marine rainbows or sea bows.
rain-deer: The rane, a species of the cervine genus.
rain-water: Water that has fallen from the clouds.
rainy: Abounding with rain; wet; showery; as rainy weather; a rainy day or season.
raise: To lift; to take up; to heave; to lift from a low or reclining posture; as, to raise a stone or weight; to raise the body in bed. The angel smote Peter on the side and raised him up. Acis 12:7.
To set upright; as, to raise a mast.
To set up; to erect; to set on its foundations and put together; as, to raise the frame of a house.
To build; as, to raise a city, a fort, a wall. I will raise forts against thee. Isaiah 29:3; Amos 9:11.
raised: Lifted; elevated; exalted; promoted; set upright; built; made or enlarged; produced; enhanced; excited; restored to life; levied; collected; roused; invented and propagated; increased.
raiser: One who raises; that which raises; one that builds; one that levies or collects; one that begins, produces or propagates.
raisin: A dried grape. Grapes are suffered to remain on the vines till they are perfectly ripe, and then dried in an oven, or by exposure to the heat of the sun. Those dried in the sun are the sweetest.
raising: Lifting; elevating; setting upright; exalting; producing; enhancing; restoring to life; collecting; levying; propagating
The act of lifting, setting up, elevating, exalting, producing, or restoring to life.
In New England, the operation or work of setting up the frame of a building.
rajah: In India, a prince. some of the rajahs are said to be independent princes; others are tributary to the Mogul.
raja: In India, a prince. some of the rajahs are said to be independent princes; others are tributary to the Mogul.
rajahship: The dignity or principality of a rajah.
rake: An instrument consisting of a head-piece in which teeth are inserted, and a long handle; used for collecting hay or other light things which are spread over a large surface, or in gardens for breaking and smoothing the earth.
A loose, disorderly, vicious man; a man addicted to lewdness and other scandalous vices.

The projection of the upper parts of a ship, at the height of the stem and stern, beyond the extremities of the keel. The distance between a perpendicular line from the extremity of stem or stern to the end of the keel, is the length of the rake; one the fore-rake, the other the rake-aft.
The inclination of a mast from a perpendicular direction.
Properly, to scrape; to rub or scratch with something rough; as, to rake the ground.
raked: Scraped; gathered with a rake; cleaned with a rake; cannonaded fore and aft.
rakehell: A lewd, dissolute fellow; a debauchee; a rake.
raking: Scraping; gathering with a rake; cleaning and smoothing with a rake; Cannonading in the direction of the length; inclining. And raking chase-guns through our sterns they send.
a. That rakes; as a raking fire or shot.
The act of using a rake; the act or operation of collecting with a rake, or of cleaning and smoothing with a rake.
The space of ground raked at once; or the quantity of haycollected by once passing the rake.
rakish: Given to a dissolute life; lewd; debauched.
rally: To reunite; to collect and reduce to order troops dispersed or thrown into confusion.
To collect; to unite; as things scattered.

To treat with good humor and pleasantry, or with slight contempt or satire, according to the nature of the case. Honeycomb rallies me upon a country life. Strephon had long confess’d his am’rous pain, which gay Corinna rallied with disdain.
To assemble; to unite. Innumerable parts of matter chanced then to rally together and to form themselves into this new world.
To come back to order. The Grecians rally and their pow’rs unite.
ram: The male of the sheep or ovine genus; in some parts of England called a tup. In the United States, the word is applied, I believe, to no other male, except in the compound ram-cat.
In astronomy, Aries, the sign of the zodiac which the sun enters on the 21st of March, or a constellation of fixed stars in the figure of a ram. It is considered the first of the twelve signs.
An engine of war, used formerly for battering and demolishing the walls of cities; called a battering-ram.
To thrust or drive with violence; to force in; to drive down or together; as, to ram down a cartridge; to ram piles into the earth.
To drive, as with a battering ram.
ramadan: Among the Mohammedans, a solemn season of fasting.
ramage: The warbling of birds sitting on boughs.
ramble: To rove; to wander; to walk, ride or sail from place to place, without any determinate object in view; or to visit many places; to rove carelessly or irregularly; as, to ramble about the city; to ramble over the country. Never ask leave to go abroad, for you will be thought an idle rambling fellow.
To go at large without restraint and without direction.
To move without certain direction. O’re his ample sides, the rambling sprays luxuriant shoot.

a roving; a wandering; a going or moving from place to place without any determinate business or object; an irregular excursion. Coming home after a short Christmas ramble, I found a letter upon my table.
rambler: One that rambles; a rover; a wanderer.
rambling: Roving; wandering; moving or going irregularly.
rambooze: a drink made of wine, ale, eggs and sugar in winter, or of wine, milk, sugar and rose water in summer.
rambuse: a drink made of wine, ale, eggs and sugar in winter, or of wine, milk, sugar and rose water in summer.
ramekin: In cookery, small slices of bread covered with a farce of cheese and eggs.
ramequins: In cookery, small slices of bread covered with a farce of cheese and eggs.
raments: In botany, loose scales on the stems of plants.
rameous: In botany, belonging to a branch; growing on or shooting from a branch.
ramification: The process of branching or shooting branches from a stem.
A branch; a small division proceeding from a main stock or channel; as the ramifications of a family; the ramifications of an artery.
A division or subdivision; as the ramifications of a subject or scheme.
In botany, the manner in which a tree produces its branches or boughs.
The production of figures resembling branches.
ramify: To divide into branches or parts; as, to ramify an art, a subject or scheme.
To shoot into branches, as the stem of a plant. When the asparagus begins to ramify - .
To be divided or subdivided; as a main subject or scheme.
rammer: An instrument for driving any thing with force; as a rammer for driving stones or piles, or for beating the earth to more solidity: .
A gun-stick; a ramrod; a rod for forcing down the charge of a gun.
ramous: In botany, branched, as a stem or root; having lateral divisions.
Branchy; consisting of branches; full of branches.
ramp: To climb, as a plant; to creep up. Plants furnished with tendrils catch hold, and so ramping on trees, they mount to a great height.
To spring; to leap; to bound; to prance; to frolic. Their bridles they would champ - and trampling the fine element, would fiercely ramp. Sporting the lion ramp’d.
A leap; a spring; a bound.
rampancy: Excessive growth or practice; excessive prevalence; exuberance; extravagance; as the rampancy of vice.
rampant: Overgrowing the usual bounds; rank in growth; exuberant; as rampant weeds.

In heraldry, applied to the lion, leopard or other beast, rampant denotes the animal reared and standing on his hind legs, in the posture of climbing. It differs from saliant, which indicates the posture of springing or making a sally. The lion rampant shakes his brinded mane.
rampart: In fortification, an elevation or mound of earth round a place, capable of resisting cannon shot, and formed into bastions, curtains. No standards from the hostile ramparts torn.
That which fortifies and defends from assault; that which secures safety .
rampion: The name of several plants; as the common esculent rampion, a species of Campanula; the crested rampion, a species of Lobelia; the horned rampion, a species of Phyteuma.
rampire: The same as rampart; but obsolete.
ramsons: A plant, a species of Allium.
ran: the pret. of run. In old writers, open robbery.
ranch: To sprain; to injure by violent straining or contortion.
rancid: Having a rank smell; strong scented; sour; musty; as rancid oil.
rancidity: The quality of being rancid; a strong, sour scent, as of old oil. The rancidity of oils may be analogous to the oxidation of metals.
rancidness: The quality of being rancid; a strong, sour scent, as of old oil. The rancidity of oils may be analogous to the oxidation of metals.
rancor: The deepest malignity or spite; deep seated and implacable malice; inveterate enmity. It issues from the rancor of a villain.
rancorous: Deeply malignant; implacably spiteful or malicious; intensely virulent. So flam’d his eyes with rage and ranc’rous ire. Rancorous’ opposition to the gospel of Christ.
rancorously: With deep malignity or spiteful malice.
rand: A border; edge; margin; as the rand of a shoe.
random: A roving motion or course without direction; hence, want of direction, rule or method; hazard; chance; used in the phrase, at random, that is, without a settled point of direction; at hazard.
Course; motion; progression; distance of a body thrown; as the furthest random of a missile weapon.
Done at hazard or without settled aim or purpose; left to chance; as arandom blow.
Uttered or done without previous calculation; as a random guess.
random-shot: A shot not directed to a point, or a shot with the muzzle of the gun elevated above a horizontal line.
rane: A species of deer found in the northern parts of Europe and Asia. He has large branched palmated horns, and travels with great speed. Among the Laplanders, he is a substitute for the horse, the cow, the goat and the sheep, as he furnishes food, clothing and the means of conveyance. This animal will draw a sled on the snow more than a hundred miles in a day.
ranedeer: A species of deer found in the northern parts of Europe and Asia. He has large branched palmated horns, and travels with great speed. Among the Laplanders, he is a substitute for the horse, the cow, the goat and the sheep, as he furnishes food, clothing and the means of conveyance. This animal will draw a sled on the snow more than a hundred miles in a day.
ranforce: The ring of a gun next to the vent.
range: To set in a row or in rows; to place in a regular line, lines or ranks; to dispose in the proper order; as, to range troops in a body; to range men or ships in the order of battle.
To dispose in proper classes, orders or divisions; as, to range plants and animals in genera and species.
To dispose in a proper manner; to place in regular method; in a general sense. Range and arrange are used indifferently in the same sense.
To rove over; to pass over. Teach him to range the ditch and force the brake.
To sail or pass in a direction parallel to or near; as, to range the coast, that is, along the coast.
ranged: Disposed in a row or line; placed in order; passed in roving placed in a particular direction.
ranger: One that ranges; a rover; a robber.
In England, a sworn officer of a forest, appointed by the king’s letters patent, whose business is to walk through the forest, watch the deer, present trespasses.
rangership: The office of the keeper of a forest or park.
ranging: Placing in a row or line; disposing in order, method or classes; roving; passing near and in the direction of.
The act of placing in lines or in order; a roving
rank: A row or line, applied to troops; a line of men standing abreast or side by side, and as opposed to file, a line running the length of a company, battalion or regiment. Keep your ranks; dress your ranks. Fierce fiery warriors fight upon the clouds in ranks and squadrons and right form of war.
Ranks, in the plural, the order of common soldiers; as, to reduce an officer to the ranks.
A row; a line of things, or things in a line; as a rank of osiers.
Degree; grade; in military affairs; as the rank of captain, colonel or general; the rank of vice-admiral.

Degree of elevation in civil life or station; the order of elevation or of subordination. We say, all ranks and orders of men; every man’s dress and behavior should correspond with his rank; the highest and the lowest ranks of men or of other intelligent beings.
ranked: Placed in a line; disposed in an order or class; arranged methodically.
ranker: One that disposes in ranks; one that arranges.
ranking: Placing in ranks or lines; arranging; disposing in orders or classes; having a certain rank or grade.
rankle: To grow more rank or strong; to be inflamed; to fester; as a rankling wound. A malady that burns and rankles inward.
To become more violent; to be inflamed; to rage; as rankling malice; rankling envy. Jealousy rankles in the breast.
rankly: With vigorous growth; as, grass or weeks grow rankly.
rankness: Vigorous growth; luxuriance; exuberance; as the rankness of plants or herbage.
Exuberance; excess; extravagance; as the rankness of pride; the rankness of joy .
Extraordinary strength. The crane’s pride is in the rankness of her wing.
Strong taste; as the rankness of flesh or fish.
Rancidness; rank smell; as the rankness of oil.
ransack: To plunder; to pillage completely; to strip by plundering; as, to ransack a house or city. Their vow is made to ransack Troy.

To search thoroughly; to enter and search every place or part. It seems often to convey the sense of opening doors and parcels, and turning over things in search; as, to ransack files of papers. I ransack the several caverns.
To violate; to ravish; to deflower; as ransacked chastity.
ransom: The money or price paid for the redemption of a prisoner or slave, or for goods captured by an enemy; that which procures the release of a prisoner or captive, or of captured property, and restores the one to liberty and the other to the original owner. By his captivity in Austria, and the heavy ransom he paid for his liberty, Richard was hindered from pursuing the conquest of Ireland.
Release from captivity, bondage or the possession of an enemy. They were unable to procure the ransom of the prisoners.
In law, a sum paid for the pardon of some great offense and the discharge of the offender; or a fine paid in lieu of corporal punishment.
In Scripture, the price paid for a forfeited life, or for delivery or release from capital punishment. Then he shall give for the ransom of his life, whatever is laid upon him. Exodus 21:30.

The price paid for procuring the pardon of sins and the redemption of the sinner from punishment. Deliver him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom. Job 33:24. The Son of man came - to give his life a ransom for many. Matthew 20:28; Mark 10:45.
ransomed: Redeemed or rescued from captivity, bondage or punishment by the payment of an equivalent.
ransoming: Redeeming from captivity, bondage or punishment by giving satisfaction to the possessor; rescuing; liberating.
rant: To rave in violent, high sounding or extravagant language, without correspondent dignity of thought; to be noisy and boisterous in words or declamation; as a ranting preacher. Look where my ranking host of the garter comes.
High sounding language without dignity of thought; boisterous, empty declamation; as the rant of fanatics. This is stoical rant, without any foundation in the nature of man, or reason of things.
ranter: A noisy talker; a boisterous preacher.
ranting: Uttering high sounding words without solid sense; declaiming or preaching with boisterous empty words.
rantism: The practice or tenets of ranters.
ranula: A swelling under the tongue, similar to the encysted tumors in different parts of the body.
ranunculus: In botany, crowfoot, a genus of plants of many species, some of them beautiful flowering plants, particularly the Asiatic, or Turkey and Persian ranunculus, which is diversified with many rich colors.
rap: To strike with a quick sharp blow; to knock; as, to rap on the door.
To strike with a quick blow; to knock. With one great peal they rap the door. To rap out, to utter with sudden violence; as, to rap out an oath.

to seize and bear away, as the mind or thoughts; to transport out of one’s self; to affect with ecstasy or rapture; as rapt into admiration. I’m rapt with joy to see my Marcia’s tears. Rapt into future times the bar begun.
To snatch or hurry away. And rapt with whirling wheels. Rapt in a chariot drawn by fiery steeds.
rapacious: Given to plunder; disposed or accustomed to seize by violence; seizing by force; as a rapacious enemy. Well may thy lord, appeas’d redeem thee quite from death’s rapacious claim.
Accustomed to seize for food; subsisting on prey or animals seized by violence; as a rapacious tiger; a rapacious fowl.
rapaciously: By rapine; by violent robbery or seizure.
rapaciousness: The quality of being rapacious; disposition to plunder or to exact by oppression.
rapacity: Addictedness to plunder; the exercise of plunder; the act or practice of seizing by force; as the rapacity of a conquering army; the rapacity of pirates; the rapacity of a Turkish pashaw; the rapacity of extortioners.
Ravenousness; as the rapacity of animals.
The act or practice of extorting or exacting by oppressive injustice.
rape: In a general sense, a seizing by violence; also, a seizing and carrying away by force, as females.
In law, the carnal knowledge of a woman forcibly and against her will.
Privation; the act of seizing or taking away. And ruin’d orphans of thy rapes complain.
something taken or seized and carried away. Where now are all my hopes? oh, never more shall they revive, nor death her rapes restore.
rapeseed: The seed of the rape, from which oil is expressed.
rapid: Very swift or quick; moving with celerity; as a rapid stream; a rapid flight; a rapid motion. Part shun the goal with rapid wheels.
Advancing with haste or speed; speedy in progression; as rapid growth; rapid improvement.
Of quick utterance of words; as a rapid speaker.
rapidity: Swiftness; celerity; velocity; as the rapidity of a current; the rapidity of motion of any kind.
Haste in utterance; as the rapidity of speech or pronunciation.
Quickness of progression or advance; as rapidity of growth or improvement.
rapidly: With great speed, celerity or velocity; swiftly; with quick progression; as, to run rapidly; to grow or improve rapidly.
With quick utterance; as, to speak rapidly.
rapids: The part of a river where the current moves with more celerity than the common current. Rapids imply a considerable descent of the earth, but not sufficient to occasion a fall of the water, or what is called a cascade or cataract.
rapier: A small sword used only in thrusting.
rapine: The act of plundering; the seizing and carrying away of things by force.
rapparee: A wild Irish plunderer; so called from rapery, a half pike that he carries.
rapper: The knocker of a door. N/ .
rapture: Transport; ecstasy; violence of a pleasing passion; extreme joy or pleasure. Music when thus applied, raises in the mind of the hearer great conceptions; it strengthens devotion and advances praise into rapture.
Rapidity with violence; a hurrying along with velocity; as rolling with torrent rapture.
Enthusiasm; uncommon heat of imagination. You grow correct, that once with rapture writ.
rapturous: Ecstatic; transporting; ravishing; as rapturous joy, pleasure or delight.
rare: Uncommon; not frequent; as a rare event; a= rare phenomenon.
Unusually excellent; valuable to a degree seldom found. Rare work, all fill’d with terror and delight. Above the rest I judge one beauty rare.
Thin; porous; not dense; as a rare and attenuate substance. Water is nineteen times lighter and by consequence nineteen times rarer than gold.
Nearly raw; imperfectly roasted or boiled; as rare beef or mutton; eggs roasted rare.
rareeshow: A show carried in a box.
rarefaction: The act or process of expanding or distending bodies, by separating the parts and rendering the bodies more rare or porous, by which operation they appear under a larger bulk, or require more room, without an accession of new matter; opposed to condensation; as the rarefaction of air.
rarefy: To make thin and porous or less dense; to expand or enlarge a body without adding to it any new portion of its own matter; opposed to condense.
rarely: Seldom; not often; as things rarely seen.
rareness: The state of being uncommon; uncommonness; infrequency . And let the rareness the small gift commend.
Thinness; tenuity; as the rareness of air or vapor.
rareripe: Early ripe; ripe before others, or before the usual season.
An early fruit, particularly a kind of peach which ripens early.
rarity: Uncommonness; infrequency. Far from being fond of a flower for its rarity - .
A thing valued for its scarcity. I saw three rarities of different kinds, which pleased me more than any other shows in the place.
Thinness; tenuity; opposed to density; as the rarity of air.
rascal: A mean fellow; a scoundrel; in modern usage, a trickish dishonest fellow; a rogue; particularly applied to men and boys guilty of the lesser crimes, and indicating less enormity or guilt than villain. I have sense to serve my turn in store, and he’s a rascal who pretends to more.
rascality: Mean trickishness or dishonesty; base fraud.
rascally: Mean; vile; base; worthless; as a rascally porter.
rase: To pass along the surface of a thing, with striking or rubbing it at the same time; to graze. Might not the bullet which rased his cheek, have gone into his head? Obs.
To erase; to scratch or rub out; or to blot out; to cancel. J .
To level with the ground; to overthrow; to destroy; as, to rase a city.
rash: Hasty in council or action; precipitate; resolving or entering on a project or measure without due deliberation and caution, and thus encountering unnecessary hazard; applied to persons; as a rash statesman or minister; a rash commander.
Uttered or undertaken with too much haste or too little reflection; as rash words; rash measures.
Requiring haste; urgent. I have scarce leisure to salute you, my matter is so rash.
Corn so dry as to fall out with handling.
rasher: A thin slice of bacon; a thin cut.
rashly: With precipitation; hastily; without due deliberation. He that doth any thing rashly, must do it willingly; . So rashly brave, to dare the sword of Theseus.
rashness: To much haste in resolving or in undertaking a measure; precipitation; inconsiderate readiness or promptness to decide or act, implying disregard of consequences or contempt of danger; applied to persons. The failure of enterprises is often owing to rashness. We offend by rashness, which is an affirming or denying before we have sufficiently informed ourselves.
The quality of being uttered or done without due deliberation; as the rashness of words or of undertakings.
rasp: A large rough file; a grater.
To rub or file with a rasp; to rub or grate with a rough file; as, to rasp wood to make it smooth; to rasp bones to powder.
raspberry: The fruit of a bramble or species of rubus; a berry growing on a prickly plant; as the black raspberry; the red and the white raspberry .
rasure: The act of scraping or shaving; the act of erasing.
The mark by which a letter, word or any part of a writing is erased, effaced or obliterated; an erasure.
rat: A small quadruped of the genus Mus, which infests houses, stores and ships; a troublesome race of animals. To smell a rat, to be suspicious, to be on the watch from suspicion; as a Cat by the scent or noise of a rat.
ratable: That may be rated, or set at a certain value; as a Danish ore ratable at two marks./ .
Liable or subjected by law to taxation; as ratable estate.
ratafia: ratafee’. A fine spirituous liquor, prepared from the kernels of several kinds of fruits, particularly of cherries, apricots and peaches.
ratan: A small cane, the growth of India.
rat-catcher: One who makes it his business to catch rats.
ratch: In clock work, a sort of wheel having twelve fangs, which serve to lift the detents every hour and thereby cause the clock to strike.
ratchet: In a watch, a small tooth at the bottom of the fusee or barrel, which stops it in winding up.
rate: The proportion or standard by which quantity or value is adjusted; as silver valued at the rate of six shillings and eight pence the ounce. The rate and standard of wit was different then from what it is in these days.
Price or amount stated or fixed on any thing. A king may purchase territory at too dear a rate. The rate of interest is prescribed by law.
Settled allowance; as a daily rate of provisions. 2 Kings 25:30.
Degree; comparative height or value. I am a spirit of no common rate. In this did his holiness and godliness apear above the rate and pitch of other men’s, in that he was so infintely merciful.
Degree in which any thing is done. the ship sails at the rate of seven knots an hour. Many of the horse could not march at that rate, nor come lup soon enough.
rated: Set at a certain value; estimated: set in a certain order or rank.
rater: One who sets a value on or makes an estimate.
rath: Early; coming before others, or before the usual time. Bring the rath primrose, that forsaken dies. We sometimes see the word rath-ripe, early ripe; but it is obsolete or nearly so. In the United States, I believe it is not used at all.
rather: More readily or willingly; with better liking; with preference or choice. My soul chooseth strangling and death rather than life. Job 7:15. Light is come into the world, and men loved darkness rather than light, because their deeds were evil. John 3:19; Psalm 84:10.
In preference; preferably; with better reason. Good is rather to be chosen than evil. See Acts 5:29.
In a greater degree than otherwise. He sought throughout the world, but sought in vain, and no where finding, rather fear’d her slain.
More properly; more correctly speaking. This is an art which does mend nature, change it rather; but the art itself is nature.

Noting some degree of contrariety in fact. She was nothing better, but rather grew worse. Mark 5:26; Matthew 27:24. The rather, especially; for better reason; for particular cause. You are come to me in a happy time, the rather for I have some sport in hand. Had rather, is supposed to be a corruption of would rather. I had rather speak five words with my understanding. 7 Corinthians 14:19. This phrase may have been originally, “I’d rather,” for I would rather, and the contraction afterwards mistaken for had. Correct speakers and writers generally use would in all such phrases; I would rather, I prefer; I desire in preference.
rathoffite: A mineral brought from Sweden, of the garnet kind. Its color is a dingy brownish black, and it is accompanied with calcarious spar and small crystals of hornblend.
ratification: The act of giving sanction and validity to something done by another; as the ratification of a treaty by the senate of the United States.
ratifier: He or that which ratifies or sanctions.
ratify: To confirm; to establish; to settle. We have ratified to them the borders of Judea.
To approve and sanction; to make valid; as, to ratify an agreement or treaty.
rating: Setting at a certain value; assigning rant to; estimating.
ratio: ra’sho. Proportion, or the relation of homogeneous things which determines the quantity of one from the quantity of another, without the intervention of a third. The relation which one quantity has to another of the same kind, as expressed by the quotient of the one divided by the other. Thus the ratio of 4 to 2 is 4/2, or 2; and the ratio of 5 to 6 is 5/6. This is geometrical ratio, which is that signified when the term is used without distinction; but arithmetical ratio is the difference between two quantities. Thus the arithmetical ratio of 2 to 6 is 4. Ratio respects magnitudes of the same kind only. One line may be compared with another line, but a line cannot be compared with a superficies, and hence between a line and a superficies there can be no ratio.
ratiocination: The act or process of reasoning, or of deducing consequences from premises.
ratiocinative: Argumentative; consisting in the comparison of propositions or facts, and the deduction of inferences from the comparison; as a ratiocinative process.
ration: A portion or fixed allowance of provisions, drink and forage, assigned to each soldier in an army for his daily subsistence and for the subsistence of horses. Officers have several rations according to their rank or number of attendants. Seamen in the navy also have rations of certain articles.
rational: Having reason or the faculty of reasoning; endowed with reason; opposed to irrational; as, man is a rational being; brutes are not rational animals. It is our glory and happiness to have a rational nature.
Agreeable to reason; opposed to absurd; as a rational conclusion or inference; rational conduct.
Acting in conformity to reason; wise; judicious; as a rational man.
rationale: A detail with reasons; a series of reasons assigned; as Dr. Sparrow’s rationale of the Common Prayer.
An account or solution of the principles of some opinion, action, hypothesis, phenomenon.
rationalist: One who proceeds in his disquisitions and practice wholly upon reason.
rationality: The power of reasoning. God has made rationality the common portion of mankind.
Reasonableness. Well directed intentions, whose rationalities will not bear a rigid examination.
rationally: In consistency with reason; reasonably. We rationally expect every man will pursue his own happiness.
rationalness: The state of being rational or consistent with reason.
ratlin: A small line traversing the shrouds of a ship, making the step of a ladder for ascending to the mast-heads.
ratline: A small line traversing the shrouds of a ship, making the step of a ladder for ascending to the mast-heads.
ratoon: A sprout from the root of the sugar cane, which has been cut.
rat-tail: In farriery, an excrescence growing from the pastern to the middle of the shank of a horse.
ratteen: A thick woolen stuff quilled or twilled.
rattinet: A woolen stuff thinner than ratteen.
rattle: To make a quick sharp noise rapidly repeated, by the collision of bodies not very sonorous. When bodies are sonorous, it is called jingling. We say, the wheels rattle over the pavement. \ . And the rude hail in rattling tempest forms. He fagoted his notions as they fell, and if they rhym’d and rattl’d, all was well.
To speak eagerly and noisily; to utter words in a clattering manner. Thus turbulent in rattling tone she spoke. He rattles it out against popery.
To cause to make a rattling sound or a rapid succession of sharp sounds; as, to rattle a chain.
To stun with noise; to drive with sharp sounds rapidly repeated. Sound but another, and another shall, as loud as thine, rattle the welkin’s ear.
To scold; to rail at clamorously; as, to rattle off servants sharply.
rattlesnake: A snake that has rattles at the tail, of the genus Crotalus. The rattles consist of articulated horny cells, which the animal vibrates in such a manner as to make a rattling sound. The poison of the rattlesnake is deadly.
rattlesnake-root: A plant or root of the genus Polygala, and another of the genus Prenanthes.
rattlesnake-weed: A plant of the genus Eryngium.
rattling: Making a quick succession of sharp sounds.
A rapid succession of sharp sounds. Nahum 3:2.
raucity: Hoarseness; a loud rough sound; as the raucity of a trumpet.
Among physicians, hoarseness of the human voice.
raught: the old participle of reach. Obs.
ravage: Spoil; ruin; waste; destruction by violence, wither by men, beasts or physical causes; as the ravage of a lion; the ravages of fire or tempest; the ravages of an army. Would one think ‘twene possible for love to make such ravage in a noble soul.
Waste; ruin; destruction by decay; as the ravages of time.
To spoil; to plunder; to pillage; to sack. Already Cesar has ravag’d more than half the globel .
To lay waste by any violent force; as, a flood or inundation ravages the meadows. The shatter’d forest and the ravag’d vale.
to waste or destroy by eating; as fields ravaged by swarms of locusts.
ravager: a plunderer a spoiler; he or that which lays waste.
rave: To wander in mind or intellect; to be delirious; to talk irrationally; to be wild. When men thus rave, we may conclude their brains are turned.
To utter furious exclamations; to be furious or raging; as a madman. Have I not cause to rave and beat my breast?
To dote; to be unreasonably fond; followed by upon; as, to rave upon antiquity.
the upper side-piece of timber of the body of a cart.
ravel: To entangle; to entwist together; to make intricate; to involve; to perplex. What glory’s due to him that could divide such ravel’d inte’rests, has the knot unty’>d?
To untwist; to unweave or unknot; to disentangle; as, to ravel out a twist; to ravel out a stocking. Sleep, that knits up the ravel’d sleeve of care.
to hurry or run over in confusion.
ravelin: In fortification, a detached work with two faces which make a salient angle, without any flanks, and raised before the counterscarp, of the place. In this it differs from a half moon, which is placed before an angle.
raven: ra’ven. A large fowl of a black color, of the genus Corvus.

To devour with great eagerness; to eat with voracity. Our natures do pursue, like rats that raven down their proper bane, a thirsty evil, and when we drink, we die. Like a roaring lion, ravening the prey. Ezekiel 22:25.
rav’n. To prey with rapacity. Benjamin shall raven as a wolf. Genesis 49:27.
ravening: Preying with rapacity; voraciously devouring; as a ravening wolf.
ravenous: Furiously voracious; hungry even to rage; devouring with rapacious eagerness; as a ravenous wolf, lion or vulture.
Eager for prey or gratification; as ravenous appetite or desire.
ravenousness: Extreme voracity; rage for prey; as the ravenousness of a lion.
raver: One that raves or is furious.
ravet: An insect shaped like a cock-chaffer, which infests the West Indies.
ravin: A long deep hollow worn by a stream or torrent of water; hence, any long deep hollow or pass through mountains
ravine: A long deep hollow worn by a stream or torrent of water; hence, any long deep hollow or pass through mountains
raving: or a. Furious with delirium; mad; distracted.
ravingly: With furious wildness or frenzy; with distraction.
ravish: To seize and carry away by violence. These hairs which thou dost ravish from my chin, will quicken and accuse thee. This hand shall ravish thy pretended right.
To have carnal knowledge of a woman by force and against her consent. Isaiah 13:16; Zechariah 14:2.
To bear away with joy or delight; to delight to ecstasy; to transport. Thou hast ravished my heart.Song of Solomon 4:9.
ravished: Snatched away by violence; forced to submit to carnal embrace; delighted to ecstasy.
ravisher: One that forces a woman to his carnal embrace.
ravishing: Snatching or taking by violence; compelling to submit to carnal intercourse; delighting to ecstasy.
A seizing and carrying away by violence.
Carnal knowledge by force against consent.
ravishment: The act of forcing a woman to carnal connection; forcible violation of chastity.
Rapture; transport of delight; ecstasy; pleasing violence of the mind or senses. All things joy with ravishment attracted by thy beauty still to gaze.
The act of carrying away; abduction; as the ravishment of children from their parents, of a ward from his guardian, or of a wife from her husband.
raw: Not altered from its natural state; not roasted, boiled or cooked; not subdued by heat; as raw meat.
Not covered with skin; bare, as flesh. If there is quick raw flesh in the risings, it is an old leprosy. Leviticus 13:10, 11.
sore. And all his sinews waxen weak and raw through long imprisonment.
Not altered by heat; not cooked or dressed; being in its natural state; as raw fruit.
raw-boned: Having little flesh on the bones.
rawhead: The name of a specter, mentioned to frighten children; as rawhead and bloody bones.
rawness: The state of being raw; uncooked; unaltered by heat; as the rawness of flesh.
Unskillfulness; state of being inexperienced; as the rawness of seamen or troops.
ray: a line of light, or the right line supposed to be described by a particle of light. a collection of parallel rays constitutes a beam; a collection of diverging or converging rays, a pencil. The mixed solar beam contains, 1st. calorific rays, producing heat and expansion, but not vision and color; 2d. colorific rays, producing vision and color, but not heat nor expansion; 3d. chimical rays, producing certain effects on the composition of bodies, but neither heat, expansion, vision or color; 4th. a power producing magnetism, but whether a distinct or associated power, is not determined. It seems to be associated with the violet, more than with the other rays.
Figuratively, a beam of intellectual light.
Light; luster. The air sharpen’d his visual ray.
In botany, the outer part or circumference of a compound radiate flower.
In ichthyology, a bony or cartilaginous ossicle in the fins of fishes, serving to support the membrane.
rayless: Destitute of light; dark; not illuminated.
raze: To subvert from the foundation; to overthrow; to destroy; to demolish; as, to raze a city to the ground. The royal hand that raz’d unhappy Troy.
To erase; to efface; to obliterate. Razing the characters of your renown. [In this sense, rase and erase are now used.I .
To extirpate. And raze their factions and their family.
razed: Subverted; overthrown; wholly ruined; erased; extirpated.
razee: A ship of war cut down to a smaller size.
razor: An instrument for shaving off beard or hair. Razors of a boar, a boar’s tusks.
razor-bill: An aquatic fowl, the Alca torda; also, the Rhynchops nigra or cut-water.
razor-fish: A species of fish with a compressed body.
razure: The act of erasing or effacing; obliteration.
re: a prefix or inseparable particle in the composition of words, denotes return, repetitionIt is contracted from red, which the Latins retained in words beginning with a vowel, as in redamo, redeoIn a few English words, all or most of which, I believe, we receive from the French, it has lost its appropriate signification, as in rejoice, recommend
A small Portuguese coin or money of account, value about one mill and a fourth, American money.
reabsorb: To draw in or inbibe again what has been effused, extravasated or thrown off; used of fluids; as, to reabsorb chyle, lymph, blood, gas.
reabsorption: The act or process of inbibing what has been previously thrown off, effused or extravasated; the swallowing a second time.
reaccess: A second access or approach; a visit renewed.
reach: To extend; to stretch; in a general sense; sometimes followed by out and forth; as, to reach out the arm. Hence,

To extend to; to touch by extending either the arm alone, or with an instrument in the hand; as, to reach a book on the shelf; I cannot reach the object with my cane; the seaman reaches the bottom of the river with a pole or a line.
To strike from a distance. O patron power, thy present aid afford, that I may reach the beast.
To deliver with the hand by extending the arm; to hand. He reached me an orange. He reached me a full cup.
reached: Stretched out; extended; touched by extending the arm; attained to; obtained.
reacher: One that reaches or extends; one that delivers by extending the arm.
reaching: Stretching out; extending; touching by extension of the arm; attaining to; gaining; making efforts to vomit.
react: To act or perform a second time; as, to react a play. The same scenes were reacted at Rome.
To return an impulse or impression; to resist the action of another body by an opposite force. Every elastic body reacts on the body that impels it from its natural state.
To act in opposition; to resist any influence or power.
reacted: Acted or performed a second time.
reacting: Acting again; in physics, resisting the impulse of another body.
reaction: In physics, counteraction; the resistance made by a body to the action or impulse of another body, which endeavors to change its state, either of motion or rest. Action and reaction are equal.
Any action in resisting other action or power.
read: The preterit and pp. read, is pronounced red.

To utter or pronounce written or printed words, letters or characters in the proper order; to repeat the names or utter the sounds customarily annexed to words, letters or characters; as, to read a written or printed discourse; to read the letters of an alphabet; to read figures; to read the notes of music, or to read music.
To inspect and understand words or characters; to peruse silently; as, to read a paper or letter without uttering the words; to read to one’s self.
readable: That may be read; fit to be read.
readeption: A regaining; recovery of something lost.
reader: One that reads; any person who pronounces written words; particularly, one whose office is to read prayers in a church.
By way of distinction, one that reads much; one studious in books.
readership: the office of reading prayers in a church.
readily: Quickly; promptly; easily. I readily perceive the distinction you make.
Cheerfully; without delay or objection; without reluctance. He readily granted my request.
readiness: Quickness; promptness; promptitude; facility; freedom from hinderance or obstruction; as readiness of speech; readiness of thought; readiness of mind in suggesting an answer; readiness of reply.
Promptitude; cheerfulness; willingness; alacrity; freedom from reluctance; as, to grant a request or assistance’ with readiness. They received the word with all readiness of mind. Acis 17:11.
A state of preparation; fitness of condition. The troops are in readiness.
reading: Pronouncing or perusing written or printed words or characters of a book or writing.
Study of books; as a man of extensive reading.
readjust: To settle again; to put in order again what had been discomposed.
readmission: The act of admitting again what had been excluded; as the readmission of fresh air into an exhausted receiver; the readmission of a student into a seminary.
readmit: To admit again. Whose ear is ever open and his eye gracious to readmit the suppliant.
readmittance: A second admittance; allowance to enter again.
readorn: To adorn anew; to decorate a second time.
ready: Quick; prompt; not hesitating; as ready wit; a ready consent.
Quick to receive or comprehend; not slow or dull; as a ready apprehension.
Quick in action or execution; dextrous; as an artist ready in his business; a ready writer. Psalm 45:1.
Prompt; not delayed present in hand. He makes ready payment; he pays ready money for every thing he buys.
Prepared; fitted; furnished with what is necessary, or disposed in a manner suited to the purpose; as a ship ready for sea. My oxen and fatlings are killed, and all things are ready. Matthew 22:4.
reaffirm: zé. To affirm a second time.
reagent: In chimistry, a substance employed to precipitate another in solution, or to detect the ingredients of a mixture. Bergman reckons barytic muriate to be one of the most sensible reagents.
reaggravation: In the Romish ecclesiastical law, the last monitory, published after three admonitions and before the last excommunication. Before they proceed to fulminate the last excommunication, they publish an aggravation and a reaggravation.
real: Actually being or existing; not fictitious or imaginary; as a description of real life. The author describes a real scene or transaction.
True; genuine; not artificial, counterfeit or factitious; as real Madeira wine; real ginger.
True; genuine; not affected; not assumed. The woman appears in her real character.
Relating to things, not to persons; not personal. Many are perfect in men’s humors, that are not greatly capable of the real part of business.

In law, pertaining to things fixed, permanent or immovable, as to lands and tenements; as real estate, opposed to personal or movable property. Real action, in law, is an action which concerns real property. Real assets, assets consisting in real estate, or lands and tenements descending to an heir, sufficient to answer the charges upon the estate created by the ancestor. Chattels real, are such chattels as concern or savor of the reality; as a term for years of land, wardships in chivalry, the next presentation to a church, estate by statue-merchant, elegit. Real composition, is when an agreement is made between the owner of lands and the parson or vicar, with consent of the ordinary, that such lands shall be discharged from payment of tithes, in consequence of other land or recompense given to the parson in lieu and satisfaction thereof. Real presence, in the Romish church, the actual
realist: A scholastic philosopher, who maintains that things and not words, are the objects of dialectics; opposed to nominal or nominalist.
realgar: A combination of sulphur and arsenic; red sulphuret of arsenic. Realgar differs from orpiment in having undergone a greater degree of heat.
reality: Actual being or existence of any thing; truth; fact; in distinction from mere appearance. A man may fancy he understands a critic, when in reality he does not comprehend his meaning.
Something intrinsically important, not merely matter of show. And to realities yield all her shows.
In the schools, that may exist of itself, or which has a full and absolute being of itself, and is not considered as a part of any thing else.
In law, immobility, or the fixed, permanent nature of property; as chattels which savor of the reality. [This word is so written in law, for reality.I .
realization: The act of realizing or making real.
The act of converting money into land.
The act of believing or considering as real.
The act of bringing into being or act.
realize: To bring into being or act; as, to realize a scheme or project. We realize what Archimedes had only in hypothesis, weighing a single grain of sand against the globe of earth.
To convert money into land, or personal into real estate.

To impress on the mind as a reality; to believe, consider or treat as real. How little do men in full health realize their frailty and mortality. Let the sincere christian realize the closing sentiment.

To bring home to one’s own case or experience; to consider as one’s own; to feel in all its force. Who, at his fire side, can realize the distress of shipwrecked mariners? This allusion must have had enhanced strength and beauty to the eye of a nation extensively devoted to a pastoral life, and therefore realizing all its fine scenes and the tender emotions to which they gave birth.
To bring into actual existence and possession; to render tangible or effective. He never realized much profit from his trade or speculations.
realized: Brought into actual being; converted into real estate; impressed, received or treated as a reality; felt in its true force; rendered actual, tangible or effective.
realizing: Bringing into actual being; converting into real estate; impressing as a reality; feeling as one’s own or in its real force; rendering tangible or effective. 2.a. That makes real, or that brings home as a reality; as a realizing view of eternity: .
really: In truth; in fact; not in appearance only; as things really evil. The anger of the people is really a short fit of madness. In this sense, it is used familiarly as a slight corroboration of an opinion or declaration. Why really, sixty five is somewhat old. REALL Y.
realm: A royal jurisdiction or extent of government; a kingdom; a king’s dominions; as the realm of England.
Kingly government; as the realm of bees.
ream: A bundle or package of paper, consisting of twenty quires.
reanimate: To revive; to resuscitate; to restore to life; as a person dead or apparently dead; as, to reanimate a drowned person.
To revive the spirits when dull or languid; to invigorate; to infuse new life or courage into; as, to reanimate disheartened troops; to reanimate drowsy senses or languid spirits.
reanimating: Restoring life to; invigorating with new life and courage.
reanimation: The act or operation of reviving from apparent death; the act or operation of giving fresh spirits, courage or vigor.
reannex: To annex again; to reunite; to annex what has been separated.
reap: To cut grain with a sickle; as, to reap wheat or rye. When ye reap the harvest, thou shalt not wholly reap the corners of thy field. Leviticus 19:9.
To clear of a crop by reaping; as, to reap a field.

To gather; to obtain; to receive as a reward, or as the fruit of labor or of works; in a good or bad sense; as, to reap a benefit from exertions. He that soweth to the flesh, shall of the flesh reap corruption. Galatians 6:8. Ye have plowed wickedness; ye have reaped iniquity. Hosea 10:13.
To perform the act or operation of reaping. In New England, farmers reap in July and August.
To receive the fruit of labor or works. They that sow in tears, shall reap in joy. Psalm 126:5.
reaped: Cut with a sickle; received as the fruit of labor or works.
reaper: One that cuts grain with a sickle.
reaping: Cutting grain with a sickle; receiving as the fruit of labor or the reward of works.
reaping-hook: An instrument used in reaping; a sickle.
rear: In a general sense, that which is behind or backwards; appropriately, the part of an army which is behind the other, either when standing on parade or when marching; also, the part of a fleet which is behind the other. It is opposed to front or van. Bring up the rear.
The last class; the last in order. Coins I place in the rear. In the rear, behind the rest; backward, or in the last class. In this phrase, rear signifies the part or place behind.
Raw; rare; not well roasted or boiled.
To raise. Who now shall rear you to the sun, or rank your tribes?
reared: Raised; lifted; brought up; educated; elevated.
rear-guard: The body of an army that marches in the rear of the main body to protect it.
rear-line: The line in the rear of an army.
rear-rank: The rank of a body of troops which is in the rear.
rearward: The end; the tail; the train behind.
reascend: To rise, mount or climb again.
To mount or ascend again. He mounts aloft and reascends the skies.
reascension: The act of reascending; a remounting.
reason: That which is thought or which is alleged in words, as the ground or cause of opinion, conclusion or determination. I have reasons which I may choose not to disclose. You ask me my reasons. I freely give my reasons. The judge assigns good reasons for his opinion, reasons which justify his decision. Hence in general,

The cause, ground, principle or motive of any thing said or done; that which supports or justifies a determination, plan or measure. Virtue and vice are not arbitrary things; but there is a natural and eternal reason for that goodness and virtue, and against vice and wickedness. 7 Peter 3:15.

Efficient cause. He is detained by reason of sickness.WV . Spain in thin sown of people, partly by reason of its sterility of soiI . The reason of the motion of the balance in a wheel-watch is by motion of the next wheel.
Final cause. Reason, in the English language, is sometimes taken for true and clear principles; sometimes for clear and fair deductions; sometimes for the cause, particularly the final cause.
reasonable: Having the faculty of reason; endued with reason; as a reasonable being.
Governed by reason; being under the influence of reason; thinking, speaking or acting rationally or according to the dictates of reason; as, the measure must satisfy all reasonable men.

Conformable or agreeable to reason; just; rational. By indubitable certainty, I mean that which does not admit of any reasonable cause of doubting. A law may be reasonable in itself, though a man does not allow it.
Not immoderate. Let all things be thought upon, that may with reasonable swiftness add more feathers to our wings.
Tolerable; being in mediocrity; moderate; as a reasonable quantity.
reasonableness: Agreeableness to reason; that state or quality of a thing which reason supports or justifies; as the reasonableness of our wishes, demands or expectations. The reasonableness and excellency of — charity.
Conformity to rational principles. The whole frame and contexture of a watch carries in it a reasonableness - the passive impression of the reason or intellectual idea that was in the artist.
Moderation; as the reasonableness of a demand.
reasonably: In a manner or degree agreeable to reason; in consistency with reason. We may reasonably suppose self interest to be the governing principle of men.
Moderately; in a moderate degree; not fully; in a degree reaching to mediocrity. If we can be industry make our deaf and dumb persons reasonably perfect in the language - .
reasoner: One who reasons or argues; as a fiar reasoner; a close reasoner; a logical reasoner.
reasoning: arguing; deducing inferences from premises; debating; discussing.

The act or process of exercising the faculty of reason; that act or operation of the mind by which new or unknown propositions are deduced from previous ones which are known and evident, or which are admitted or supposed for the sake of argument; argumentation; ratiocination; as fair reasoning; false reasoning; absurd reasoning; strong or weak reasoning. The reasonings of the advocate appeared to the court conclusive.
reasonless: Destitute of reason; as a reasonless man or mind.
Void of reason; not warranted or supported by reason. This proffer is absurd and reasonless.
reassert: To assert again; to maintain after suspension or cessation. Let us hope - we may have a body of authors who will reassert our claim to respectability in literature.
reassign: To assign back; to transfer back what has been assigned.
reassimilate: To assimilate or cause to resemble anew; to change again into a like or suitable substance.
reassimilated: Assimilated anew; changed again to a like substance.
reassurance: A second assurance against loss; or the assurance of property by an underwriter, to relieve himself from a risk he has taken.
reassure: To restore courage to; to free from fear or terror. They rose with fear, till dauntless Pallas reassur’d the rest.
To insure a second time against loss, or rather to insure by another what one has already insured; to insure against loss that may be incurred by taking a risk.
reassured: Insured against loss by risk taken, as an underwriter.
reassurer: One who insures the first underwriter.
reassuring: Restoring from fear, terror or depression of courage.
reasty: Covered with a kind of rust and having a rancid taste; applied to dried meat.
reate: A kind of long small grass that grows in water and complicates itself.
reave: To take away by stealth or violence; to bereave. Obs.
rebate: To blunt; to beat to obtuseness; to deprive of keenness. He doth rebate and blunt his natural edge. The keener edge of battle to rebate.
In commerce, abatement in price; deduction.
In heraldry, a diminution or abatement of the bearings in a coat of arms.
rebatement: In commerce, abatement in price; deduction.
In heraldry, a diminution or abatement of the bearings in a coat of arms.
rebel: One who revolts from the government to which he owes allegiance, either by openly renouncing the authority of that government, or by taking arms and openly opposing it. A rebel differs from an enemy, as the latter is one who does not owe allegiance to the government which he attacks. Numbers 17:10.
One who willfully violates a law.
One who disobeys the king’s proclamation; a contemner of the king’s laws.
A villain who disobeys his lord.
rebelled: or a. Rebellious; guilty of rebellion.
rebelling: Renouncing the authority of the government to which one owes allegiance; rising in opposition to lawful authority.
rebellion: An open and avowed renunciation of the authority of the government to which one owes allegiance; or the taking of arms traitorously to resist the authority of lawful government; revolt. Rebellion differs from insurrection and from mutiny. Insurrection may be a rising in opposition to a particular act or law, without a design to renounce wholly all subjection to the government. Insurrection may be, but is not necessarilyMutiny is an insurrection of soldiers or seamen against the authority of their officers. No sooner is the standard of rebellion displayed, than men of desperate principles resort to it.

Open resistance to lawful authority: . Commission of rebellion, in law, a commission awarded against a person who treats the king’s authority with contempt, in not obeying his proclamation according to his allegiance, and refusing to attend his sovereign when required; in which case, four commissioners are ordered to attach him wherever he may be found.
rebellious: Engaged in rebellion; renouncing the authority and dominion of the government to which allegiance is due; traitorously resisting government or lawful authority. Deuteronomy 9:7, 24; Deuteronomy 21:18, 20.
rebelliously: With design to throw off the authority of legitimate government; in opposition to the government, to which one is bound by allegiance; with violent or obstinate disobedience to lawful authority.
rebelliousness: The quality or state of being rebellious.
rebellow: To bellow in return; to echo back a loud roaring noise. The cave rebellow’d and the temple shook.
rebellowing: Bellowing in return or in echo.
reboation: The return of a loud bellowing sound.
reboil: To take fire; to be hot.
rebound: To spring back; to start back; to be reverberated by an elastic power resisting force or impulse impressed; as a rebounding echo. Bodies absolutely hard, or so soft as to be void of elasticity, will not rebound from one another.
To drive back; to reverberate. Silenus sung; the vales his voice rebound.
The act of flying back in resistance of the impulse of another body; resilience. Put back as from a rock with swift rebound.
rebuff: Repercussion, or beating back; a quick and sudden resistance. The strong rebuff of some tumultuous cloud.
To beat back; to offer sudden resistance to; to check.
rebuild: To build again; to renew a structure; to build or construct what has been demolished; as, to rebuild a house, a wall, a wharf or a city.
rebild: To build again; to renew a structure; to build or construct what has been demolished; as, to rebuild a house, a wall, a wharf or a city.
rebuke: To chide; to reprove; to reprehend for a fault; to check by reproof. The proud he tam’d, the penitent he cheer’d, not to rebuke the rich offender fear’d. Thou shalt in any wise rebuke thy neighbor. Leviticus 19:17.
To check or restrain. The Lord rebuke thee, O Satan. Zechariah 3:2; Isaiah 17:13.
To chasten; to punish; to afflict for correction. O Lord, rebuke me not in thine anger. Psalm 6:7.&
To check; to silence. Master, rebuke thy disciples. Luke 19:39.
To check; to heal. And he stood over her and rebuked the fever. Luke 4:39.
rebuked: Reproved; reprehended; checked; restrained; punished for faults.
rebuker: One that rebukes; a chider; one that chastises or restrains.
rebus: An enigmatical representation of some nameby using figures or pictures instead of words. A gallant in love with a woman named Rose Hill, painted on the border of his gown, a rose, a hill, an eye, a loaf and a well, which reads, Rose Hill I love well .
In some chimical writers, sour milk; sometimes, the ultimate matter of which all bodies are composed.
In heraldry, a coat of arms which bears an allusion to the name of the person; as three cups, for Butler.
rebut: To repel; to oppose by argument, plea or countervailing proof.
To answer, as a plaintiff's sur-rejoinder. The plaintiff may answer the rejoinder by a sur-rejoinder; on which the defendant may rebut.
rebutter: In law pleadings, the answer of a defendant to a plaintiffs sur-rejoinder.

In law pleadings, the answer of a defendant to a plaintiff's sur-rejoinder. If I grant to a tenant to hold without impeachment of waste, and afterward implead him for waste done, he may debar me of this action by showing my grant, which is a rebutter.
rebutting: Repelling; opposing by argument, countervailing allegation or evidence.
recall: To call back; to take back; as, to recall words or declarations.
To revoke; to annul by a subsequent act; as, to recall a decree.
To call back; to revive in memory; as, to recall to mind what has been forgotten.
To call back from a place or mission; as, to recall a minister from a foreign court; to recall troops from India.
recallable: That may be recalled. Delegates recallable at pleasure.
recant: To retract; to recall; to contradict a former declaration. How soon would ease recant vows made in pain, as violent as void.
To recall words; to revoke a declaration or proposition; to unsay what has been said. Convince me I am wrong, and I will recant.
recantation: The act of recalling; retraction; a declaration that contradicts a former one.
recapacitate: To qualify again; to confer capacity on again.
recapitulate: To repeat the principal things mentioned in a preceding discourse, argument or essay; to give a summary of the principal facts, points or arguments.
recapitulating: Repeating the principal things in a discourse or argument.
recapitulation: Asummary or concise statement or enumeration of the principal points or facts in a preceding discourse, argument or essay.
recaption: The act of retaking; reprisal; the retaking of one’s own goods, chattels, wife or children from one who has taken them and wrongfully detains them. Writ of recaption, a writ to recover property taken by a second distress, pending a replevin for a former distress for the same rent or service.
recaptor: One who retakes; one that takes a prize which had been previously taken.
recapture: The act of retaking; particularly, the retaking of a prize or goods from a captor.
To retake; particularly, to retake a prize which had been previously taken.
