recapturing: Retaking, as a prize from the captor.
recast: To cast again; as, to recast cannon.
recede: To move back; to retreat; to withdraw. Like the hollow roar of tides receding from th’ insulted shore. All bodies moved circularly, endeavor to recede from the center.
To withdraw a claim or pretension; to desist from; to relinquish what had been proposed or asserted; as, to recede from a demand; to recede from terms or propositions.
To cede back; to grant or yield to a former possessor; as, to recede conquered territory.
receipt: The act of receiving; as the receit of a letter.
The place of receiving; as the receit of custom. Matthew 9:9.
Reception; as the receit of blessings or mercies.
Reception; welcome; as the kind receit of a friend. Obs.
Recipe; prescription of ingredients for any composition, as of medicines.
receit: The act of receiving; as the receit of a letter.
The place of receiving; as the receit of custom. Matthew 9:9.
Reception; as the receit of blessings or mercies.
Reception; welcome; as the kind receit of a friend. Obs.
Recipe; prescription of ingredients for any composition, as of medicines.
receive: To take, as a thing offered or sent; to accept. He had the offer of a donation, but he would not receive it.
To take as due or as a reward. He received the money on the day it was payable. He received ample compensation.
To take or obtain from another in any manner, and either good or evil. Shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil? Job 2:10.
To take, as a thing communicated; as, to receive a wound by a shot; to receive a disease by contagion. The idea of a solidity we receive by our touch.
To take or obtain intellectually; as, to receive an opinion or notion from others.
received: Taken; accepted; admitted; embraced; entertained; believed.
receivedness: General allowance or belief; as the receivedness of an opinion.
receiver: One who takes or receives in any manner.
An officer appointed to receive public money; a treasurer.
One who takes stolen goods from a thief, knowing them to be stolen, and incurs the guilt of partaking in the crime.
A vessel for receiving and containing the product of distillation.
The vessel of an air pump, for containing the thing on which an experiment is to be made.
receiving: Taking; accepting; admitting; embracing; believing; entertaining.
recency: Newness; new state; late origin; as the recency of a wound or tumor.
Lateness in time; freshness; as the recency of a transaction.
recent: New; being of late origin or existence. The ancients believed some parts of Egypt to be recent, and formed by the mud discharged into the sea by the Nile.
Late; modern; as great and worthy men ancient or recent.
Fresh; lately received; as recent news or intelligence.
Late; of late occurrence; as a recent event or transaction.
Fresh; not long dismissed, released or parted from; as Ulysses, recent from the storms.
recently: Newly; lately; freshly; not long since; as advices recently received; a torn recently built or repaired; an isle recently discovered.
recentness: Newness; freshness; lateness of origin or occurrence; as the recentness of alluvial land; the recentness of news or of events.
receptacle: A place or vessel into which something is received or in which it is contained, as a vat, a tun, a hollow in the earthThe grave is the common receptacle of the dead.

In botany, one of the parts of the fructification; the base by which the other parts of the fructification are connected. A proper receptacle belongs to one fructification only; a common receptacle connects several florets or distinct fructifications. The receptacle of the fructification is common both to the flower and the fruit, or it embraces the corol and germ. The receptacle of the flower, is the base to which the parts of the flower, exclusive of the germ, are fixed. The receptacle of the fruit, is the base of the fruit only. The receptacle of the seeds, is the base to which the seeds are fixed.
In anatomy, the receptacle of the chyle is situated on the left side of the upper verteber of the loins, under the aorta and the vessels of the left kidney.
receptacular: In botany, pertaining to the receptacle or growing on it, as the nectary.
reception: The act of receiving; in a general sense; as the reception of food into the stomach, or of air into the lungs.
Admission of any thing sent or communicated; as the reception of a letter; the reception of sensation or ideas.
Readmission. All hope is lost of my reception into grace.
Admission of entrance for holding or containing; as a sheath fitted for the reception of a sword; a channel for the reception of water.
receptive: Having the quality of receiving or admitting what is communicated. Imaginary space is receptive of all bodies.
receptivity: The state or quality of being receptive.
receptory: Generally or popularly admitted or received.
recess: A withdrawing or retiring; a moving back; as the recess of the tides.
A withdrawing from public business or notice; retreat; retirement. My recess hath given them confidence that I may be conquered. And every neighboring grove sacred to soft recess and gentle love.
Place of retirement or secrecy; private abode. This happy place, our sweet recess.
State of retirement; as lords in close recess. In the recess of the jury, they are to consider their evidence.
recession: The act of withdrawing, retiring or retreating.
The act of receding from a claim, or of relaxing a demand.
A cession or granting back; as the recession of conquered territory to its former sovereign.
recharge: To charge or accuse in return.
To attack again; to attack anew.
recheat: Among hunters, a lesson which the huntsman winds on the horn when the hounds have lost the game, to call them back from pursuing a counter scent.
rechoose: rechooz’. To choose a second time.
rechosen: or a. recho’zn. Re-elected; chosen again.
recipe: res’ipy. A medical prescription; a direction of medicines to be taken by a patient.
recipient: A receiver; the person or thing that receives; he or that to which any thing is communicated.
reciprocal: Acting in vicissitude or return; alternate. Corruption is reciprocal to generation.
Mutual; done by each to the other; as reciprocal love; reciprocal benefits or favors; reciprocal duties; reciprocal aid.

Mutually interchangeable. These two rules will render a definition reciprocal with the thing defined. Reciprocal terms, in logic, those terms that have the same signification, and consequently are convertible and may be used for each other. Reciprocal quantities, in mathematics, are those which, multiplied together, produce unity. Reciprocal figures, in geometry, are those which have the antecedents and consequents of the same ratio in both figures. Reciprocal ratio, is the ratio between the reciprocals of two quantities; as, the reciprocal ratio of 4 to 9, is that of 1/4 to 1/9.
The reciprocal of any quantity, is unity divided by that quantity. Thus the reciprocal of 4 is 1/4.
reciprocally: Mutually; interchangeably; in such a manner that each affects the other and is equally affected by it. These two particles do reciprocally affect each other with the same force.
reciprocate: To act interchangeably; to alternate. One brawny smith the puffing bellows plies, and draws and blows reciprocating air.
To exchange; to interchange; to give and return mutually; as, to reciprocate favors.
reciprocating: Interchanging; each giving or doing to the other the same thing.
reciprocation: Interchange of acts; a mutual giving and returning; as the reciprocation of kindnesses.
Alternation; as the reciprocation of the sea in the flow and ebb of tides.
Regular return or alternation of two symptoms or diseases.
reciprocity: Reciprocal obligation or right; equal mutual rights or benefits to be yielded or enjoyed. The commissioners offered to negotiate a treaty on principles of reciprocity.
recision: s as z. The act of cutting off.
recital: Rehearsal; the repetition of the words of another or of a writing; as the recital of a deed; the recital of testimony.
Narration; a telling of the particulars of an adventure or of a series of events.
recitation: In colleges and schools, the rehersal of a lesson by pupils before their instructor.
recitative: Reciting; rehearsing; pertaining to musical pronunciation.

a kind of musical pronunciation, such as that in which the several parts of the liturgy are rehearsed in churches, or that of actors on the stage, when they express some action or passion, relate some event or reveal some design. In recitative, the composer and the performer endeavor to imitate the inflections, accent and emphasis of natural speech.
recite: To rehearse a lesson. The class will recite at eleven o'clock.
To rehearse; to repeat the words of another or of a writing; as, to recite the words of an author or of a deed or covenant.
In writing, to copy; as, the words of a deed are recited in the pleading.
To tell over; to relate; to narrate; as to recite past events; to recite the particulars of a voyage.
To rehearse, as a lesson to an instructor.
reciter: One that recites or rehearses; a narrator.
reck: To care; to mind; to rate at much; as we say, to reckon much of; followed by of. Obs. Thou’s but a lazy loorde, and recks much of thy swinke. I reck as little what betideth me, as much I wish all good befortune you. Of night or loneliness it recks me not.
To heed; to regard; to care for. This son of mine not recking danger.
reckless: Careless; heedless; mindless. I made the king reckless, as them diligent.
reckon: To count; to number; that is, to tell the particulars. The priest shall reckon to him the money, according to the years that remain, even to the year of jubilee, and it shall be abated. Leviticus 27:18. I reckoned above two hundred and fifty on the outside of the church.
To esteem; to account; to repute. Romans 8:18. For him I reckon not in high estate.
To repute; to set in the number or rank of. He was reckoned among the transgressors. Luke 22:37.
To assign in an account. Romans 4:4.
reckoned: rek’nd. Counted; numbered; esteemed; reputed; computed; set or assigned to in account.
reckoner: rek’ner. One who reckons or computes. Reckoners without their host must reckon twice.
reckoning: rek’ning. Counting; computing; esteeming; reputing; stating an account mutually.
The act of counting or computing; calculation.

A statement of accounts with another; a statement and comparison of accounts mutually for adjustment; as in the proverb, “short reckonings make long friends.” . The way to make reckonings even, is to make them often.
The charges or account made by a host. A coin would have a nobler use than to pay a reckoning.
reckoning-book: a book in which money received and expended is entered.
reclaim: To claim back; to demand to have returned. The vender may reclaim the goods.

To call back from error, wandering or transgression, to the observance of moral rectitude; to reform; to bring back to correct deportment or course of life. It is the intention of Providence in its various expressions of goodness, to reclaim mankind.
To reduce to the state desired. Much labor is requir’d in trees, to tame their wild disorder, and in ranks reclaim.
To call back; to restrain. Or is her tow’ring flight reclaim’d by seas from Icarus’ downfall nam’d?
To recall; to cry out against. The headstrong horses hurried Octavius along, and were deaf to his reclaiming them.
reclaimable: That may be reclaimed, reformed or tamed.
reclaimant: One that opposes, contradicts or remonstrates against.
reclaimed: Recalled from a vicious life; reformed; tamed; domesticated; recovered.
reclaiming: Recalling to a regular course of life; reforming; recovering; taking; demanding.
reclamation: Demand; challenge of something to be restored; claim made.
reclinate: In botany, reclined, as a leaf; bend downwards, so that the point of the leaf is lower than the base. A reclinate stem is one that bends in an arch towards the earth.
reclination: That act of leaning or reclining.
recline: To lean back; to lean to one side or sideways; as, to recline the head on a pillow, or on the bosom of another, or on the arm. The mother reclin’d her dying head upon his breast.
To lean; to rest or repose; as, to recline on a couch.
Leaning; being in a leaning posture. They sat recline on the soft downy bank damask’d with flowers.
reclining: Leaning back or sideways; resting; lying.
reclose: s as z. To close or shut again.
recluse: Shut up; sequestered; retired from the world or from public notice; solitary; as a recluse monk or hermit; a recluse life. I all the live-long day consume in meditation deep, recluse from human converse.
A person who live in retirement or seclusion from intercourse with the world; as a hermit or monk.
A person who confines himself to a cell in a monastery.
reclusely: In retirement or seclusion from society.
reclusion: s as z. A state of retirement from the world; seclusion.
recognition: Acknowledgment; formal avowal; as the recognition of a final concord on a writ of covenant.

Acknowledgment; solemn avowal by which a thing is owned or declared to belong to, or by which the remembrance of it is revived. The lives of such saints had, at the time of their yearly memorials, solemn recognition in the church of God.
Knowledge confessed or avowed; as the recognition of a thing present; memory of it as passed.
recognitor: recon’itor. One of a jury upon assize.
recognizable: recon’izable. That may be recognized or acknowledged.
recognizance: Acknowledgment of a person or thing; avowal; profession; as the recognizance of christians, by which they avow their belief in their religion.

In law, an obligation of record which a man enters into before some court of record or magistrate duly authorized, with condition to do some particular act, as to appear at the assizes, to keep the peace or pay a debt. This recognizance differs from a bond, as it does not create a new debt, but it is the acknowledgment of a former debt or record. This is witnessed by the record only, and not by the party’s seal. There is also a recognizance in the nature of a statute staple, acknowledged before either of the chief justices or their substitutes, the mayor of the staple at Westminster and the recorder of London, which is to be enrolled and certified into chancery.
The verdict of a jury impaneled upon assize.
recognize: To recollect or recover the knowledge of, either with an avowal of that knowledge or not. We recognize a person at a distance, when we recollect that we have seen him before, or that we have formerly known him. We recognize his features or his voice. Speak, vassal; recognize thy sovereign queen.
To enter an obligation of record before a proper tribunal. A B recognized in the sum of twenty pounds.
recognized: Acknowledged; recollected as known; bound by recognizance.
recognizee: reconizee’. The person to whom a recognizance is made.
recognizing: Acknowledging; recollecting as known; entering a recognizance.
recognizor: reconizor’. One who enters into a recognizance.
recoil: To move or start back; to roll back; as, a cannon recoils when fired; waves recoil from the shore.
To rebound; as, the blow recoils.
To retire; to flow back; as, the blood recoils with horror at the sight.
To start back; to shrink. Nature recoils at the bloody deed.
recoiling: Starting or falling back; retiring; shrinking.
The act of starting or falling back; a shrinking; revolt.
recoin: To coin again; as, to recoin gold or silver.
recollect: To collect again; applied to ideas that have escaped from the memory; to recover or call back ideas to the memory. I recollect what was said at a former interview; or I cannot recollect what was said.

To recover or recall the knowledge of; to bring back to the mind or memory. I met a man whom I thought I had seen before, but I could not recollect his name or the place where I had seen him. I do not recollect you.
To recover resolution or composure of mind. The Tyrian queen admir’d his fortunes, more admir’d the man, then recollected stood.
re-collect: To gather again; to collect what has been scattered; as, to re-collect routed troops.
recollection: The act of recalling to the memory, as ideas that have escaped; or the operation by which ideas that have escaped; or the operation by which ideas are recalled to the memory or revived in the mind. Recollection differs from remembrance, as it is the consequence of volition, or an effort of the mind to revive ideas; whereas remembrance implies no such volition. We often remember things without any voluntary effort. Recollection is called also reminiscence.
The power of recalling ideas to the mind, or the period within which things can be recollected; remembrance. The events mentioned are not within my recollection.
In popular language, recollection is used as synonymous with remembrance.
recollet: A monk of a reformed order of Franciscans.
recombine: To combine again. If we recombine these two elastic fluids.
recomfort: To comfort again; to console anew.
recommence: recommens’. To commence again; to begin anew.
recommend: To praise to another; to offer or commend to another’s notice, confidence or kindness by favorable representations. Maecenas recommended Virgil and Horace to Augustus.
To make acceptable. A decent boldness ever meets with friends, succeeds, and ev’n a stranger recommends.
To commit with prayers. Paul chose Silas and departed, being recommended by the brethren to the grace of God. Acts 15:40.
recommendable: That may be recommended; worthy of recommendation or praise.
recommendation: The act of recommending or of commending; the act of representing in a favorable manner for the purpose of procuring the notice, confidence or civilities of another. We introduce a friend to a stranger by a recommendation of his virtues or accomplishments.
That which procures a kind or favorable reception. The best recommendation of a man to favor is politeness. Misfortune is a recommendation to our pity: .
recommendatory: That commends to another; that recommends.
recommission: To commission again. Officers whose time of service had expired, were to be recommissioned.
recommit: To commit again; as, to recommit persons to prison.
To refer again to a committee; as, to recommit a bill to the same committee.
recommitment: A second or renewed commitment; a renewed reference to a committee.
recommitting: Committing again; referring again to a committee.
recompact: To join anew. Repair and recompact my scatter’d body.
recompense: To compensate; to make return of an equivalent for any thing given, done or suffered; as, to recompense a person for services, for fidelity or for sacrifices of time, for loss or damages. The word is followed by the person or the service. We recompense a person for his services, or we recompense his kindness. It is usually found more easy to neglect than to recompense a favor.
To require; to repay; to return an equivalent; in a bad sense. Recompense to no man evil for evil. Romans 12:17.
To make an equivalent return in profit or produce. The labor of man is recompensed by the fruits of the earth.
To compensate; to make amends by any thing equivalent. Solyman - said he would find occasion for them to recompense that disgrace.
To make restitution or an equivalent return for. Numbers 5:7.
recompilement: New compilation or digest; as a recompilement of laws.
recompose: To quiet anew; to compose or tranquilize that which is ruffled or disturbed; as, to recompose the mind.
To compose anew; to form or adjust again. We produced a lovely purple which we can destroy or recompose at pleasure.
recomposed: Quieted again after agitation; formed anew; composed a second time.
recomposing: Rendering tranquil after agitation; forming or adjusting anew.
reconcilable: Capable of being reconciled; capable of renewed friendship. The parties are not reconcilable.
That may be made to agree to be consistent; consistent. The different accounts of the numbers of ships are reconcilable.
Capable of being adjusted; as, the difference between the parties is reconcilable.
reconcilableness: The quality of being reconcilable; consistency; as the reconcilableness of parts of Scripture which apparently disagree.
Possibility of being restored to friendship and harmony.
reconcile: To conciliate anew; to call back into union and friendship the affections which have been alienated; to restore to friendship or favor after estrangement; as, to reconcile men or parties that have been at variance. Go thy way; first be reconciled to thy brother - Matthew 5:24. We pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God. Corinthians 5:20; Ephesians 2:16; Colossians 1:20-21.
To bring to acquiescence, content or quiet submission; with to; as, to reconcile one’s self to afflictions. It is our duty to be reconciled to the dispensations of Providence.

To make consistent or congruous; to bring to agreement or suitableness; followed by with or to. The great men among the ancients understood how to reconcile manual labor with affairs of state. Some figures monstrous and misshap’d appear, considered singly, or beheld too near; which but proportion’d to their light and place, due distance reconciles to form and grace.
To adjust; to settle; as, to reconcile differences or quarrels.
reconciled: Brought into friendship from a state of disagreement or enmity; made consistent; adjusted.
reconcilement: Reconciliation; renewal of friendship. Animosities sometimes make reconcilement impracticable.
Friendship renewed. RECONCIILEMENT. No cloud of anger shall remain, but peace assured and reconcilement.
reconciler: One who reconciles; one who brings parties at variance into renewed friendship.
One who discovers the consistence of proposition.
reconciliation: The act of reconciling parties at variance; renewal of friendship after disagreement or enmity. Reconciliation and friendship with God, really form the basis of all rational and true enjoyment.

In Scripture, the means by which sinners are reconciled and brought into a state of favor with God, after natural estrangement or enmity; the atonement; expiation. Seventy weeks are determined upon thy people and upon thy holy city, to finish the transgression and to make an end of sin, and to make reconciliation for iniquity. Danie! 9:24; Hebrews 2:17.
Agreement of things seemingly opposite, different or inconsistent.
reconciling: Bringing into favor and friendship after variance; bringing to content or satisfaction; showing to be consistent; adjusting; making to agree.
recondite: Secret; hidden from the view or intellect; abstruse; as recondite causes of things.
Profound; dealing in things abstruse; as recondite studies.
reconditory: A repository; a store-house or magazine.
reconnoiter: To view; to survey; to examine by the eye; particularly in military affairs, to examine the state of an enemy’s army or camp, or the ground for military operations.
reconquer: To conquer again; to recover by conquest.
reconsider: To consider again; to turn in the mind again; to review.
To annul; to take into consideration a second time and rescind; as, to reconsider a motion in a legislative body; to reconsider a vote. The vote has been reconsidered, that is.
reconsideration: A renewed consideration or review in the mind.
reconvene: To convene or call together again.
To assemble or come together again.
reconvey: To convey back or to its former place; as, to reconvey goods.
To transfer back to a former owner; as, to reconvey an estate.
reconveyed: Conveyed back; transferred to a former owner.
reconveying: Conveying back; transferring to a former owner.
record: To register; to enroll; to write or enter in a book or on parchment, for the purpose of preserving authentic or correct evidence of a thing; as, to record the proceedings of a court; to record a deed or lease; to record historical events.
To imprint deeply on the mind or memory; as, to record the sayings of another in the heart.
To cause to be remembered. So evn and morn recorded the third day.
recorded: Registered; officially entered in a book or on parchment; imprinted on the memory.
recorder: A person whose official duty is to register writings or transactions; one who enrolls or records.
An officer of a city who is keeper of the rolls or records, or who is invested with judicial powers.
Formerly, a kind of flute, flageolet or wind instrument. The figures of recorders, flutes and pipes are straight; but the recorder hath a less bore and a greater above and below.
recording: Registering; enrolling; imprinting on the memory.
recouch: To retire again to a lodge, as lions.
recount: To relate in detail; to recite; to tell or narrate the particulars; to rehearse. Say from these glorious seeds what harvest flows, recount our blessings, and compare our woes./ .
recounted: Related or told in detail; recited.
recourse: Literally, a running back; a return.
A going to with a request or application, as for aid or protection. Children have recourse to their parents for assistance.
Application of efforts, art or labor. The general had recourse to stratagem to effect his purpose. Our last recourse is therefore to our art.
recover: To regain; to get or obtain that which was lost; as, to recover stolen goods; to recover a town or territory which an enemy had taken; to recover sight or senses; to recover health or strength after sickness. David recovered all that the Amalekites had carried away. 7 Samuel 30:18.
To restore from sickness; as, to recover one from leprosy. Kings 5:3.
To revive from apparent death; as, to recover a drowned man.
To regain by reparation; to repair the loss of, or to repair an injury done by neglect; as, to recover lost time. Good men have lapses and failings to lament and recover.
To regain a former state by liberation from capture or possession. That they may recover themselves out of the snare of the devil. Timothy 2:26.
recoverable: That may be regained or recovered. Goods lost or sunk in the ocean are not recoverable.
That may be restored from sickness.
That may be brought back to a former condition. A prodigal course is like the sun’s, but not like his recoverable.
That may be obtained from a debtor or possessor. The debt is recoverable.
recovered: Regained; restored obtained by judicial decision.
recoveree: In law, the tenant or person against whom a judgment is obtained in common recovery.
recovering: Regaining; obtaining in return or by judgment in law; regaining health.
recoveror: In law, the demandant or person who obtains a judgment in his favor in common recovery.
recovery: The act of regaining, retaking or obtaining possession of anything lost. The crusades were intended for the recovery of the holy land from the Saracens. We offer a reward for the recovery of stolen goods.

Restoration from sickness or apparent death. The patient has a slow recovery from a fever. Recovery from a pulmonary affection is seldom to be expected. Directions are given for the recovery of drowned persons.
The capacity of being restored to health. The patient is past recovery.

The obtaining of right to something by a verdict and judgment of court from an opposing party in a suit; as the recovery of debt, damages and costs by a plaintiff; the recovery of cost by a defendant; the recovery of land in ejectment. Common recovery, in law, is a species of assurance by matter of record, or a suit or action, actual or fictitious, by which lands are recovered against the tenant of the freehold; which recovery binds all persons, and vests an absolute fee simple in the recoveror.
recreant: Crying for mercy, as a combatant in the trial by battle; yielding; hence, cowardly; mean spirited.
Apostate; false. Who for so many benefits receiv’d, turn’d recreant to God, ingrate and false.
One who yields in combat and cries craven; one who begs for mercy; hence, a mean spirited, cowardly wretch.
recreate: To refresh after toil; to reanimate, as languid spirits or exhausted strength; to amuse or divert in weariness. Painters when they work on white grounds, place before them colors mixed with blue and green, to recreate their eyes. St. John is said to have recreated himself with sporting with a tame partridge.
To gratify; to delight. These ripe fruits recreate the nostrils with their aromatic scent.
To relieve; to revive; as, to recreate the lungs with fresh air.
re-create: To create or form anew. An opening the campaign of 1776, instead of reinforcing, it was necessary to re-create the army.
recreating: Refreshing after toil; reanimating the spirits or strength; diverting; amusing.
recreation: Refreshment of the strength and spirits after toil; amusement; diversion.
Relief from toil or pain; amusement in sorrow or distress.
recreative: Refreshing; giving new vigor or animation; giving relief after labor or pain; amusing; diverting. Choose such sports as are recreative and healthful. Let the music be recreative.
recreativeness: The quality of being refreshing or diverting.
recrement: Superfluous matter separated from that which is useful; dross; scoria; spume; as the recrement of ore or of the blood.
recremental: Drossy; consisting of superfluous matter separated from that which is valuable.
recrementitial: Drossy; consisting of superfluous matter separated from that which is valuable.
recrementitious: Drossy; consisting of superfluous matter separated from that which is valuable.
recriminate: To return one accusation with another. It is not my business to recriminate.
To charge an accuser with the like crime.
recrimination: The return of one accusation with another.
In law, an accusation brought by the accused against the accuser upon the same fact.
recriminator: He that accuses the accuser of a like crime.
recrudescence: The state of becoming sore again.
recrudescency: The state of becoming sore again.
recrudescent: Growing raw, sore or painful again.
recruit: To repair by fresh supplies any thing wasted. We say, food recruits the flesh; fresh air and exercise recruit the spirits. Her cheeks glow the bright, recruiting their color.
To supply with new men any deficiency of troops; as, to recruit an army.
To gain new supplies of any thing wasted; to gain flesh, health, spirits; as, lean cattle recruit in fresh pastures.
To gain new supplies of men; to raise new soldiers.
The supply of any thing wasted; chiefly, a new raised soldier to supply the deficiency of an army.
recruited: Furnished with new supplies of what is wasted.
recruiting: Furnishing with fresh supplies; raising new soldiers for an army.
The business of raising new soldiers to supply the loss of men in an army.
recruitment: The act or business of raising new supplies of men for an army.
rectangle: In arithmetic, the product of two lines multiplied into each other.
rectangled: Having right angles, or angles of ninety degrees.
rectangular: Right angled; having angles of ninety degrees.
rectifiable: that may be rectified; capable of being corrected or set right; as a rectifiable mistake.
rectification: The act or operation of correcting, amending or setting right that which is wrong or erroneous; as the retification of errors, mistakes or abuses.
In chimistry, the process of refining or purifying any substance by repeated distiliation, which separates the grosser parts; as the rectification of spirits or sulphuric acid.
rectified: Corrected; set or made right; refined by repeated distiliation or sublimation.
rectifier: One who refines a substance by repeated distiliation.
An instrument that shows the variations of the compass, and rectifies the course of a ship.
rectify: To make right; to correct that which is wrong, erroneous or false; to amend; as, to rectify errors, mistakes or abuses; to rectify the will, the judgment, opinions; to rectify disorders.
In chimistry, to refine by repeated distiliation or sublimation, by which the fine parts of a substance are separated from the grosser; as, to rectify spirit or wine.
To rectify the globe, is to bring the sun’s place in the ecliptic on the globe to the brass meridian.
rectifying: Correcting; amending; refining by repeated distiliation or sublimation.
rectilineal: Right lined; consisting of a right line or of right lines; straight; as a rectilinear figure or course; a rectilinear side or way.
rectilinear: Right lined; consisting of a right line or of right lines; straight; as a rectilinear figure or course; a rectilinear side or way.
rectitude: In morality, rightness of principle or practice; uprightness of mind; exact conformity to truth, or to the rules prescribed for moral conduct, either by divine or human laws. Rectitude of mind is the disposition to act in conformity to any known standard of right, truth or justice; rectitude of conduct is the actual conformity to such standard. Perfect rectitude belongs only to the Supreme Being. The more nearly the rectitude of men approaches to the standard of the divine law, the more exalted and dignified is their character. Want of rectitude is not only sinful, but debasing. There is a sublimity in conscious rectitude - in comparison with which the treasures of earth are not worth naming.
rector: Aruler or governor. God is the supreme rector of the world.
A clergyman who has the charge and cure of a parish, and has the tithes; or the parson of an unimpropriated parish.

The chief elective officer of some universities, as in France and Scotland. The same title was formerly given to the president of a college in New England, but it is now in disuse. In Scotland, it is still the title of the head master of a principal school.
The superior officer or chief of a convent or religious house; and among the Jesuits, the superior of a house that is a seminary or college.
rectorship: The office or rank of a rector.
rectory: A parish church, parsonage or spiritual living, with all its rights, tithes and glebes.
rectum: In anatomy, the third and last of the large intestines.
recubation: The act of lying or leaning.
recumb: To lean; to recline; to repose.
recumbence: The act of reposing or resting in confidence.
recumbency: The posture of leaning, reclining or lying.
recumbent: Leaning; reclining; as the recumbent posture of the Romans at their meals.
recuperation: Recovery, as of any thing lost.
recuperative: Tending to recovery; pertaining to recovery.
recuperatory: Tending to recovery; pertaining to recovery.
recur: To return to the thought or mind. When any word has been used to signify an idea, the old idea will recur in the mind, when the word is heard.
To resort; to have recourse. If to avoid succession in eternal existence, they recur to the punctum stans of the schools, they will very little help us to a more positive idea of infinite duration.
recurrence: Return; as the recurrence of error.
recurrency: Return; as the recurrence of error.
recurrent: Returning from time to time; as recurrent pains of a disease.

In crystallography, a recurrent crystal is one whose faces, being counted in annular ranges from one extremity to the other, furnish two different numbers which succeed each other times, as 4, 8, 4, 8, 4.
In anatomy, the recurrent nerve is a branch of the par vagum, given off in the upper part of the thorax, which is reflected and runs up along the trachea to the larynx.
recurvate: In botany, bent, bowed or curved downwards; as a recurvate leaf.
Bent outward; as a recurvate prickle, awn, petiole, calyx or corol.
recurved: Bent back or downwards; as a recurved leaf.
recurviroster: A fowl whose beak or bill bends upwards, as the avoset.
recusant: s as z. Refusing to acknowledge the supremacy of the king, or to conform to the established rites of the church; as a recusant lord.
In English history, a person who refuses to acknowledge the supremacy of the king in matters of religion; as a popish recusant, who acknowledges the supremacy of the pope.
One who refuses communion with the church of England; a non- conformist. All that are recusants of holy rites.
recusation: In law, the act of refusing a judge, or challenging that he shall not try the cause, on account of his supposed partiality. [This practice is now obsolete.; .
recuse: s as z. To refuse or reject, as a judge; to challenge that the judge shall not try the cause.
redact: To force; to reduce to form.
redan: In fortification, a work indented, or formed with salient and re- entering angles, so that one part may flank and defend another.
red-berried: Having or bearing red berries; as red-berried shrub cassia.
red-bird: The popular name of several birds in the United States, as the Tanagra aestiva or summer red-bird, the Tanagra rubra, and the Baltimore oriole or hangnest.
redbreast: A bird so called from the color of its breast, a species of Motacilla. In America, this name is given to the robin, so called, a species of Turdus.
redbud: A plant or tree of the genus Cercis.
red-chalk: A kind of clay ironstone; reddle.
red-coat: A name given to a soldier who wears a red coat.
redden: To grow or become red. - The coral redden and the ruby glow.
To blush. Appius reddens at each word you speak.
reddendum: In law, the clause by which rent is reserved in a lease.
reddition: A returning of any thing; restitution; surrender.
redditive: Returning; answering to an interrogative; a term of grammar.
reddle: Red chalk, commonly used as a pigment. It is a mineral of a florid color, but not of a deep red.
redeem: To purchase back; to ransom; to liberate or rescue from captivity or bondage, or from any obligation or liability to suffer or to be forfeited, by paying an equivalent; as, to redeem prisoners or captured goods; to redeem a pledge.

To repurchase what has been sold; to regain possession of a thing alienated, by repaying the value of it to the possessor. If a man sell a dwelling house in a walled city, then he may redeem it within a whole year after it is sold. Leviticus 25:29.

To rescue; to recover; to deliver from. Th’ Almighty from the grave hath me redeem’d. Redeem Israel, O God, out of all his troubles.PsaIm 25:22, Deuteronomy 7:8. The mass of earth not yet redeemed from chaos.
To compensate; to make amends for. It is a chance which does redeem all sorrows. By lesser ills the greater to redeem.
To free by making atonement. Thou hast one daughter who redeems nature from the general curse.
redeemable: That may be redeemed; capable of redemption.

That may be purchased or paid for in gold and silver, and brought into the possession of government or the original promiser. The capital of the debt of the United States may be considered in the light of an annuity redeemable at the pleasure of the government.
redeemed: Ransomed; delivered from bondage, distress, penalty, liability, or from the possession of another, by paying an equivalent.
redeemer: The Savior of the world, Jesus Christ.
redeeming: Ransoming; procuring deliverance from captivity, capture, bondage, sin, distress or liability to suffer, by the payment of an equivalent.
redeliver: To deliver again; to liberate a second time.
redelivery: The act of delivering back; also, a second delivery or liberation.
redemand: To demand back; to demand again.
redemise: s as z. To convey or transfer back, as an estate in fee simple, fee tail, for life or a term of years.
Reconveyance; the transfer of an estate back to the person who has demised it; as the demise and redemise of an estate in fee simple, fee tail, or for life or years, by mutual leases.
redemption: Repurchase of captured goods or prisoners; the act of procuring the deliverance of persons or things from the possession and power of captors by the payment of an equivalent; ransom; release; as the redemption of prisoners taken in war; the redemption of a ship and cargo.
Deliverance from bondage, distress, or from liability to any evil or forfeiture, either by money, labor or other means.
Repurchase, as of lands alienated. Leviticus 25:24; Jeremiah 32:7-8.

The liberation of an estate from a mortgage; or the purchase of the right to re-enter upon it by paying the principal sum for which it was mortgaged with interest and cost; also, the right of redeeming and re-entering.
Repurchase of notes, bills or other evidence of debt by paying their value in specie to their holders.
redemptioner: One who redeems himself, or purchases his release from debt or obligation to the master of a ship by his services; or one whose services are sold to pay the expenses of his passage to America.
redemptory: Paid for ransom; as Hector’s redemptory price.
redented: Formed like the teeth of a saw; indented.
redeye: A fish of a red color, particularly the iris.
redgum: A disease of new born infants; an eruption of red pimples in early infancy.
red-haired: Having hair of a red or sandy color.
red-hot: Red with heat; heated to redness; as red-hot iron; red-hot balls.
redigest: To digest or reduce to form a second time.
redigesting: Digesting a second time; reducing again to order.
redintegrate: To make whole again; to renew; to restore to a perfect state.
Renewed; restored to wholeness or a perfect state.
redintegration: Renovation; restoration to a whole or sound state.
In chimistry, the restoration of any mixed body or matter to its former nature and constitution.
redispose: s as z. To dispose or adjust again.
redisseizin: In law, a writ of redisseizin, is a writ to recover seizin of lands or tenements against a redisseizor.
redisseizor: A person who disseizes lands or tenements a second time, or after a recovery of the same from him in an action of novel disseizin.
redistribute: To distribute again; to deal back again.
redistribution: A dealing back, or a second distribution.
red-led: red-led. Minium, or red oxyd of lead, composed of 88 parts of lead and 12 of oxygen.
redness: The quality of being red; red color.
redolent: Having or diffusing a sweet scent.
redouble: To repeat often; as, to redouble blows.
To increase by repeated or continued additions. And AEtna rages with redoubl’d heat.
redub’l. To become twice as much. The argument redoubles upon us.
redoubled: redub’Ild. Repeated in return; repeated over and over; increased by repeated or continued additions.
redoubling: redub’ling. Repeating in return; repeating again and again; increasing by repeated or continued additions.
redound: To be sent, rolled or driven back. The evil, soon driven back, redounded as a flood on those from whom it sprung.
To conduce in the consequence; to contribute; to result. The honor done to our religion ultimately redounds to God the author of it.
To proceed in the consequence or effect; to result. There will no small use redound from them to that manufactures.
redout: In fortification, an outwork; a small square fort without any defense, except in front; used in trenches, lines of circumvallation, contravallation and approach to defend passages.
redoutable: Formidable; that is to be dreaded; terrible to foes; as a redoubtable hero. Hence the implied sense is valiant.
redpole: A bird with a red head or poll, of the genus Fringilla.
redraft: t To draw or draft anew.

In the French commercial code, a new bill of exchange which the holder of a protested bill draws on the drawer or indorsers, by which he reimburses to himself the amount of the protested bill with costs and charges.
redrafted: Drafted again; transcribed into a new copy.
redraw: To draw again. In commerce, to draw a new bill of exchange, as the holder of a protested bill, on the drawer or indorsers.
To draw a second draft or copy.
redress: To set right; to amend. In yonder spring of roses, find what to redress till noon.

To remedy; to repair; to relieve from, and sometimes to indemnify for; as, to redress wrongs; to redress injuries; to redress grievances. Sovereigns are bound to protect their subjects, and redress their grievances.
To ease; to relieve; as, she labored to redress my pain.
Reformation; amendment. For us the more necessary is a speedy redress of ourselves.

Relief; remedy; deliverance from wrong, injury or oppression; as the redress of grievances. We applied to government, but could obtain no redress. There is occasion for redress when the cry is universal.
redsear: To break or crack when too hot, as iron under the hammer; a term of workmen.
redshank: A bird of the genus Scolopax.
A contemptuous appellation for bare legged persons.
redshort: Brittle, or breaking short when red hot, as a metal; a term of workmen.
redstart: A bird of the genus Motacilla.
redtail: A bird of the genus Motacilla.
redstreak: A sort of apple, so called from its red streaks.
Cider pressed from the red streak apples.
reduce: Literally, to bring back; as, to reduce these bloody days again.
To bring to a former state. It were but just and equal to reduce me to my dust.

To bring to any state or condition, good or bad; as, to reduce civil or ecclesiastical affairs to order; to reduce a man to poverty; to reduce a state to distress; to reduce a substance to powder; to reduce a sum to fractions; to reduce on to despair.

To diminish in length, breadth, thickness, size, quantity or value; as, to reduce expenses; to reduce the quantity of any thing; to reduce the intensity of heat; to reduce the brightness of color light; to reduce a sum or amount; to reduce the price of goods.
To lower; to degrade; to impair in dignity or excellence. Nothing so excellent but a man may fasten on something belonging to it, to reduce it.
reduced: Brought back; brought to a former state; brought into any state or condition; diminished; subdued; impoverished.
reducement: The act of bringing back; the act of diminishing; the act of subduing; reduction.
reducible: That may be reduced. All the parts of painting are reducible into these mentioned by the author.
reducing: Bringing back; bringing to a former state, or to a different state or form; diminishing; subduing; impoverishing.
reduct: In building, a little place taken out of a larger to make it more regular and uniform, or for some other convenience.
reduction: The act of reducing, or state of being reduced; as the reduction of a body to powder; the reduction of things to order.
Diminution; as the reduction of the expenses of government; the reduction of the national debt.
Conquest; subjugation; as the reduction of a province to the power of a foreign nation.

In arithmetic, the bringing of numbers of different denominations into one denomination; as the reduction of pounds, ounces, pennyweights and grains to grains, or the reduction of grains to pounds; the reduction of days and hours to minutes, or of minutes to hours and days. The change of numbers of a higher denomination into a lower, as of pounds into pence or farthings, is called reduction descending; the change of numbers of a lower denomination into a higher, as of cents into dimes, dollars or eagles, is called reduction ascending. Hence the rule for bringing sums of different denominations into one denomination, is called reduction.

In algebra, reduction of equations is the clearing of them of all superfluous quantities, bringing them to their lowest terms and separating the known from the unknown, till the unknown quantity alone is found on one side, and the known ones of the other.
reductive: That which has the power of reducing.
redundance: Excess or superfluous quantity; superfluity; superabundance; as a redundancy of bile: . Labor throws off redundancies.
redundancy: Excess or superfluous quantity; superfluity; superabundance; as a redundancy of bile: . Labor throws off redundancies.
redundant: Superfluous; exceeding what is natural or necessary; superabundant; exuberant; as a redundant quantity of bile or food. Notwithstanding the redundant oil in fishes, they do not increase fat so much as flesh. Redundant words, in writing or discourse, are such as are synonymous with others used, or such as add nothing to the sense or force of the expression.
Using more words or images than are necessary or useful. Where an author is redundant, mark those paragraphs to be retrenched.

In music, a redundant chord is one which contains a greater number of tones, semitones or lesser intervals, than it does in its natural state, as from fa to sol sharp. It is called by some authors, a chord extremely sharp.
redundantly: With superfluity or excess; superfluously; superabundantly.
ree: A small Portuguese coin or money of account, value about one mill and a fourth, American money.
To riddle; to sift; that is, to separate or throw off. [Not in use or local.
re-echo: To echo back; to reverberate again; as, the hills re-echo the roar of cannon.
To echo back; to return back or be reverberated; as an echo. And a loud groan re-echoes from the main.
reechy: Tarnished with smoke; sooty; foul; as a reechy neck.
reed: The common name of many aquatic plants; most of them large grasses, with hollow jointed stems, such as the common reed of the genus Arundo, the bambooThe bur-reed is of the genus Sparganium; the Indian Flowering reed of the genus Canna .
A musical pipe; reeds being anciently used for instruments of music.
A little tube through which a hautboy, bassoon or clarinet is blown.
An arrow, as made of a reed headed.
reeded: Formed with channels and ridges like reeds.
reeden: ree’dn. Consisting of a reed or reeds’ as reeden pipes.
reedgrass: A plant, bur-reed, of the genus Sparganium.
re-edification: Act or operation of rebuilding; state of being rebuilt.
re-edify: To rebuild; to build again after destruction.
reedless: Destitute of reeds; as reedless banks.
reedmace: A plant of the genus Typha.
reedy: Abounding with reeds; as a reedy pool.
reef: A certain portion of a sail between the top or bottom and a row of eyelet holes, which is folded or rolled up to contract the sail, when the violence of the wind renders it necessary.
A chain or range of rocks lying at or near the surface of the water.
To contract or reduce the extent of a sail by rolling or folding a certain portion of it and making it fast to the yard.
reef-bank: A piece of canvas sewed across a sail, to strengthen it in the part where the eyelet holes are formed.
reefed: Having a portion of the top or bottom folded and made fast to the yard.
reefing: Folding and making fast to the yard, as a portion of a sail.
reef-line: A small rope formerly used to reef the courses by being passed through the holes of the reef spirally.
reef-tackle: A tackle upon deck, communicating with its pendant, and passing through a block at the top-mast head, and through a hole in the top-sail-yard-arm, is attached to a cringle below the lowest reef; used to pull the skirts of the top-sails close to the extremities of the yards to lighten the labor of reefing.
reek: To steam; to exhale; to emit vapor; applied especially to the vapor of certain moist substances, rather than to the smoke of burning bodies. I found me laid in balmy sweat, which with his beams the sun soon dry’d, and on the reeking moisture fed. Whose blood yet reeks on my avenging sword.
reeky: Smoky; soiled with smoke or steam; foul.
reel: A frame or machine turning on an axis, and on which yarn is extended for winding, either into skeins, or from skeins on to spools and quills. On a reel also seamen wind their log-lines.
To gather yarn from the spindle.

To stagger; to incline or move in walking, first to one side and then to the other; to vacillate. He with heavy fumes opprest, reel’d from the palace and retir’d to rest. They reel to and fro, and stagger like a drunken man. Psalm 107:27.
re-elect: To elect again; as, to re-elect the former governor.
re-election: Election a second time, or repeated election; as the re-election of a former representative.
re-eligibility: The capacity of being re-elected to the same office.
re-eligible: Capable of being elected again to the same office.
re-embark: To embark or put on board again.
re-embarkation: A putting on board or a going on board again.
re-embattle: To array again for battle; to arrange again in the order of battle.
re-enacting: Enacting anew; passing again into a law.
re-enaction: The passing into a law again.
re-enactment: The enacting or passing of a law a second time; the renewal of a law.
re-enforce: To strengthen with new force, assistance or support, as to re-enforce an argument; but particularly, to strengthen an army or a fort with additional troops, or a navy with additional ships.
re-enforced: Strengthened by additional force, troops or ships.
re-enforcement: Additional force; fresh assistance; particularly, additional troops or force to augment the strength of an army or of ships.
Any augmentation of strength or force by something added.
re-engage: To engage again; to enlist a second time; to covenant again.
re-enjoy: To enjoy anew or a second time.
re-entering: Entering in return; as salient and re-entering angles.
re-enthrone: To enthrone again; to replace ona throne.
re-establish: To establish anew; to fix or confirm again; as, to re-establish a covenant; to re-establish health.
re-establishment: The act of establishing again; the state of being re-established; renewed confirmation; restoration.
reeve: A bird, the female of the ruff.
In seamen’s language, to pass the end of a rope through any hole in a block, thimble, cleat, ring-bolt, cringle
re-exchange: In commerce, the exchange chargeable on the redraft of a bill of exchange. The rate of re-exchange is regulated with respect to the drawer, at the course of exchange between the place where the bill of exchange was payable, and the place where it was drawn. Re- exchanges cannot be emulated.
re-export: To export again; to export what has been imported. In the United States, a drawback is allowed on commodities re-exported.
re-exportation: The act of exporting what has been imported.
refect: To refresh; to restore after hunger or fatigue.
refection: Refreshment after hunger or fatigue. WWV”AD .
refectory: A room of refreshment; properly, a hall or apartment in convents and monasteries, where a moderate repast is taken.
refel: To refute; to disprove; to repress; as, to refel the tricks of a sophister.
refer: To direct, leave or deliver over to another person or tribunal for information or decision; as when parties to a suit refer their cause to another court; or the court refers a cause to individuals for examination and report. A person whose opinion is requested, sometimes refers the inquirer to another person or other source of information.
To reduce as to the ultimate end. You profess and practice to refer all things to yourself.

To reduce; to assign; as to an order, genus or class. Naturalists are sometimes at a loss to know to what class or genus an animal or plant is to be referred. To refer one’s self, to betake; to apply. NM .
To respect; to have relation. Many passages of Scripture refer to the peculiar customs of the orientals.
To appeal; to have recourse; to apply. In suits it is good to refer to some friend of trust.
referable: That may be referred; capable of being considered in relation to something else.

That may be assigned; that may be considered as belonging to or related to. It is a question among philosophers, whether all the attractions which obtain between bodies, are referable to one general cause.
referee: One to whom a thing is referred; particularly, a person appointed by a court to hear, examine and decide a cause between parties, pending before the court, and make report to the court. In New England, a referee differs from a arbitrator, in being appointed by the court to decide in a cause which is depending before that court. An arbitrator is chosen by parties to decide a cause between them.
reference: A sending, dismission or direction to another for information.
Relation; respect; view towards. The christian religion commands sobriety, temperance and moderation, in reference to our appetites and passions.
Allusion to. In his observations he had no reference to the case which has been stated.
In law, the process of assigning a cause depending in court, for a hearing and decision, to persons appointed by the court.
referendary: One to whose decision a cause is referred.
An officer who delivered the royal answer to petitions.
referred: Dismissed or directed to another; assigned, as to a class, order or cause; assigned by a court to persons appointed to decide.
referring: Dismissing or directing to another for information; alluding; assigning, as to a class, order, cause; or assigning to private persons for decision.
re-find: To find again; to experience anew.
refine: To purify; in a general sense; applied to liquors, to depurate; to defecate; to clarify; to separate, as liquor, from all extraneous matter. In this sense, the verb is used with propriety, but it is customary to use fine.

Applied to metals, to separate the metallic substance from all other matter, whether another metal or alloy, or any earthy substance; in short, to detach the pure metal from all extraneous matter. I will bring the third part through the fire, and will refine them as silver is refined. Zechariah 13:9.
To purify, as manners, from what is gross, clownish or vulgar; to polish; to make elegant. We expect to see refined manners in courts.
To purify, as language, by removing vulgar words and barbarisms.
To purify, as taste; to give a nice and delicate perception of beauty and propriety in literature and the arts.
refined: Purified; separated from extraneous matter; assayed, as metals; clarified, as liquors; polished; separated from what is coarse, rude or improper.
refinedness: State of being refined; purity; refinement; also, affected purity.
refinement: The act of purifying by separating from a substance all extraneous matter; a clearing from dross, dregs or recrement; as the refinement of metals or liquors.
The state of being pure. The more bodies are of a kin to spirit in subtilty and refinement, the more diffusive are they.
Polish of language; elegance; purity. From the civil war to this time, I doubt whether the corruptions in our language have not equaled its refinements.
Polish of manners; elegance; nice observance of the civilities of social intercourse and of graceful decorum. Refinement of manners is often found in persons of corrupt morals.
Purity of taste; nice perception of beauty and propriety in literature and the arts.
refiner: One that refines metals or other things.
An improver in purity and elegance; as a refiner of language.
An inventor of superfluous subtilities; one is who over nice in discrimination, in argument, reasoning, philosophy.
refinery: The place and apparatus for refining metals.
refining: Purifying; separating from alloy or any extraneous matter; polishing; improving in accuracy, delicacy or purity.
refit: t To fit or prepare again; to repair; to restore after damage or decay; as, to refit ships of war.
reflect: To throw back; to return. In the rainbow, the rays of light are reflected as well as refracted. Bodies close together reflect their own color.
To throw back light; to return rays or beams; as a reflecting mirror or gem.

To throw or turn back the thoughts upon the past operations of the mind or upon past events. We reflect with pleasure on a generous or heroic action; we reflect with pain on our follies and vices; we reflect on our former thoughts, meditations and opinions.
To consider attentively; to revolve in the mind; to contemplate; as, I will reflect on this subject. And as I much reflected, much I mourn’d. In every action, reflect upon the end.
reflected: Thrown back; returned; as reflected light.
reflectent: Bending or flying back; as the ray descendent, and ray reflectent.
reflectible: That may be reflected or thrown back.
reflecting: Turning back, as thoughts upon themselves or upon past events.
Reflecting on, casting censure or reproach.
reflection: The act of throwing back; as the reflection of light or colors. The angle of incidence and the angle of reflection are always equal.
That which is reflected. As the sun in water we can bear, yet not the sun, but his reflection there.
The operation of the mind by which it turns its views back upon itself and its operations; the review or consideration of past thoughts, opinions or decisions of the mind, or of past events.

Thought thrown back on itself, on the past or on the absent; as melancholy reflections; delightful reflections. Job’s reflections on his once flourishing estate, at the same time afflicted and encouraged him.
reflective: Throwing back images; as a reflective mirror. In the reflective stream the sighing bride, viewing her charms impair’'d - .
Considering the operation of the mind, or things past; as reflective reason.
reflex: Directed back; as a reflex act of the soul, the turning of the intellectual eye inward upon its own actions.
Designating the parts of a painting illuminated by light reflected from another part of the same picture.
reflexibility: The quality of being reflexible or capable of being reflected; as the reflexibility of the rays of light.
reflexible: Capable of being reflected or thrown back. The light of the sun consists of rays differently refrangible and reflexible.
reflexive: Having respect to something past. Assurance reflexive cannot be a divine faith.
refluent: Flowing back; ebbing; as the refluent tide.
Flowing back; returning, as a fluid; as refluent blood.
reflux: A flowing back; the returning of a fluid; as the flux and reflux of the tides; the flux and reflux of Euripus.
refocillate: To refresh; to revive; to give new vigor to.
refocillation: The act of refreshing or giving new vigor; restoration of strength by refreshment.
refoment: To foment anew; to warm or cherish again.
reform: To change from worse to better; to amend; to correct; to restore to a former good state, or to bring from a bad to a good state; as, to reform a profligate man; to reform corrupt manners’ or morals. The example alone of a vicious prince will corrupt an age, but that of a good one will not reform it.
To change from bad to good; to remove that which is bad or corrupt; as, to reform abuses; to reform the vices of the age.
To abandon that which is evil or corrupt, and return to a good state; to be amended or corrected. A man of settled habits of vice will seldom reform.
Reformation; amendment of what is defective, vicious, corrupt or depraved; as the reform of parliamentary elections; reform of government.
re-form: To form again; to create or shape anew.
reformation: The act of reforming; correction or amendment of life, manners, or of any thing vicious or corrupt; as the reformation of manners; reformation of the age; reformation of abuses. Satire lashes vice into reformation.
By way of eminence, the change of religion from the corruptions of popery to its primitive purity, begun by Luther, A.D. 1517.
re-formation: The act of forming anew; a second forming in order; as the re- formation of a column of troops into a hollow square.
reformed: Corrected; amended; restored to a good state; as a reformed profligate; the reformed church.
reformer: One who effects a reformation or amendment; as a reformer of manners or of abuses.
One of those who commenced the reformation of religion from popish corruption; as Luther, Melancthon, Zuinglius and Calvin.
reforming: Correcting what is wrong; amending; restoring to a good state.
reformist: One who is of the reformed religion.
One who proposes or favors a political reform.
refract: t To break the natural course of the rays of light; to cause to deviate from a direct course. A dense medium refracts the rays of light, as they pass into it from a rare medium.
refracted: Turned from aé direct course, as rays of light. 2.a. In botany, bent back at an acute angle; as a refracted corol.
refracting: Turning from a direct course. 2.a. That turns rays from a direct course; as a refracting medium.
refraction: The deviation of a moving body, chiefly rays of light, from a direct course. This is occasioned by the different densities of the mediums through which light passes. Refraction out of a rarer medium into a denser, is made towards the perpendicular. Refraction may be caused by a body’s falling obliquely out of one medium into another. Refraction double, the separation of a ray of light into two separate parts, by passing through certain transparent mediums, as the Iceland crystal. All crystals, except those whose primitive form is either a cube or a regular octahedron, exhibit double refraction.
refractive: That refracts or has power to refract or turn from a direct course; as refractive densities.
refractoriness: Perverse or sullen obstinacy in opposition or disobedience. I never allowed any man’s refractoriness against the privileges and orders of the house.
refractory: Sullen or perverse in opposition or disobedience; obstinate in non-compliance; as a refractory child; a refractory servant. Raging appetites that are most disobedient and refractory.
Unmanageable; obstinately unyielding; as a refractory beast.
Applied to metals, difficult of fusion; not easily yielding to the force of heat.
A person obstinate in opposition or disobedience.
refragable: That may be refuted that is.
refrain: To hold back; to restrain; to keep from action. My son - refrain thy foot from their path. Proverbs 1:15. Then Joseph could not refrain himself before all them that stood by. Genesis 45:1.
To forbear; to abstain; to keep one’s self from action or interference. Refrain from these men and let them alone. Acis 5:38.
The burden of a song; a kind of musical repetition.
refrangibility: The disposition of rays of light to be refracted or turned out of a direct course, in passing out of one transparent body or medium into another.
refrangible: Capable of being refracted or turned out of a direct course in passing from one medium to another; as rays of light.
refresh: To cool; to allay heat. A dew coming after a heat refresheth.
To give new strength to; to invigorate; to relieve after fatigue; as, to refresh the body. A man or a beast is refreshed by food and rest. Exodus 23:12.
To revive; to reanimate after depression; to cheer; to enliven. For they have refreshed my spirit and yours. 7 Corinthians 16:18.
To improve by new touches any thing impaired. The rest refresh the scaly snakes.
To revive what is drooping; as, rain refreshes the plants.
refresher: He or that which refreshes, revives or invigorates.
refreshing: or a. Cooling; invigorating; reviving; reanimating.
Refreshment; relief after fatigue or suffering.
refreshment: Act of refreshing; or new strength or vigor received after fatigue; relief after suffering; applied to the body.
New life or animation after depression; applied to the mind or spirits.
That which gives fresh strength or vigor, as food or rest.
refrigerant: Among physicians, a medicine which abates heat and refreshes the patient.
refrigerate: To cool; to allay the heat of; to refresh.
refrigeration: The act of cooling; the abatement of heat; state of being cooled.
refrigeratory: In distillation, a vessel filled with cold water, through which the worm passes; by which means the vapors are condensed as they pass through the worm.
refuge: Shelter or protection from danger or distress. - Rocks, dens and caves, but I in none of these find place or refuge. We have made lies our refuge. Isaiah 28:17. - We might have strong consolation, who have fled for refuge to lay hold on the hope set before us. Hebrews 6:18.

That which shelters or protects from danger, distress or calamity; a strong hold which protects by its strength, or a sanctuary which secures safety by its sacredness; any place inaccessible to an enemy. The high hills are a refuge for the wild goats. Psalm 104:18. The Lord also will be a refuge for the oppressed. Psalm 9:9.
An expedient to secure protection or defense. This last old man - their latest refuge was to send to him.

Expedient, in general. Light must be supplied, among graceful refuges, by terracing any story in danger of darkness. Cities of refuge, among the Israelites, certain cities appointed to secure the safety of such persons as might commit homicide without design. Of these there were three on each side of Jordan. Joshua 20:2-3.
refugee: One who flies to a shelter or place of safety.

One who, in times of persecution or political commotion, flees to a foreign country for safety; as the French refugees, who left France after the revocation of the edict of Nantz, and settled in Flanders and America; the refugees from Hispaniola, in 1792; and the American refugees, who left their country at the revolution.
refulgent: Casting a bright light; shining; splendid; as refulgent beams; refulgent light; refulgent arms. A conspicuous and refulgent truth.
refulgently: With a flood of light; with great brightness.
refund: To pour back. Were the humors of the eye tinctured with any color, they would refund that color upon the object. J .
To repay; to return in payment or compensation for what has been taken; to restore; as, to refund money taken wrongfully; to refund money advanced with interest; to refund the amount advanced.
refunding: Pouring back; returning by payment or compensation.
refusable: 2. s as z. That may be refused.
refusal: The act of refusing; denial of any thing demanded, solicited or offered for acceptance. The first refusal is not always proof that the request will not be ultimately granted.
The right of taking in preference to others; the choice of taking or refusing; option; pre-emption. We say, a man has the refusal of a farm or a horse, or the refusal of an employment.
refuse: To deny a request, demand, invitation or command; to decline to do or grant what is solicited, claimed or commanded. Thus Edom refused to give Israel passage through his border. Numbers 20:21.
To decline to accept what is offered; as, to refuse an office; to refuse an offer. If they refuse to take the cup at thy hand - Jeremiah 25:28.
To reject; as, to refuse instruction or reproof. Proverbs 10:17. The stone which the builders refused is become the head of the corner. Psalm 118:22.
s as z. To decline to accept; not to comply. Too proud to ask, to humble too refuse.
Literally, refused; rejected; hence, worthless; of no value; left as unworthy of reception; as the refuse parts of stone or timber. Please to bestow on him the refuse letters.
refutable: That may be refuted or disproved; that may be proved false or erroneous.
refutation: The act or process of refuting or disproving; the act of proving to be false or erroneous; the overthrowing of an argument, opinion, testimony, doctrine or theory, by argument or countervailing proof.
refute: To disprove and overthrow by argument, evidence or countervailing proof; to prove to be false or erroneous; to confute. We say, to refute arguments, to refute testimony, to refute opinions or theories, to refute a disputant. There were so many witnesses to these two miracles, that it is impossible to refute such multitudes.
refuted: Disproved; proved to be false or erroneous.
refuting: Proving to be false or erroneous; confuting.
regain: To gain anew; to recover what has escaped or been lost.
regal: Pertaining to a king; kingly; royal; as a regal title; regal authority; regal state, pomp or splendor; regal power or sway. But we say, a royal or kingly government, not a regal one. We never say, a regal territory, regal dominions, regal army, or regal navy. Regal expresses what is more personal.
regale: A magnificent entertainment or treat given to embassadors and other persons of distinction.
To refresh; to entertain with something that delights; to gratify, as the senses; as, to regale the taste, the eye or the ear. The birds of the forest regale us with their songs.
regalia: Ensigns of royalty; the apparatus of a coronation; as the crown, scepter.
In law, the rights and prerogatives of a king.
regality: Royalty; sovereignty; kingship. He came partly in by the sword and had high courage in all points of regality: .
regard: To look towards; to point or be directed. It is a peninsula which regardeth the main land.
To observe; to notice with some particularity. If much you note him, you offend him; feed and regard him not.
To attend to with respect and estimation; to value. This aspect of mine, the best regarded virgins of your clime have lov'd.

To attend to as a thing that affects our interest or happiness; to fix the mind on as a matter of importance. He does not regard the pain he feels. He does not regard the loss he has suffered. He regards only the interest of the community.
To esteem; to hold in respect and affection. The people regard their pastor, and treat him with great kindness. 2 Kings 3:14.
regardant: In law, a villain regardant is one annexed to the manor or land.
In heraldry, looking behind, as a lion or other beast.
regarder: In law, the regarder of the forest is an officer whose business is to view the forest, inspect the officers, and inquire of all offenses and defaults.
regardful: Taking notice; heedful; observing with care; attentive. Let a man be very tender and regardful of every pious motion made by the Spirit of God on his heart.
regarding: Noticing; considering with care; attending to; observing; esteeming; caring for.
regardless: Not looking or attending to; heedless; negligent; careless; as regardless of life or of health; regardless of danger; regardless of consequences. Regardless of the bliss wherein he sat.
regata: In Venice, a grand rowing match in which many boats are rowed for a prize.
regatta: In Venice, a grand rowing match in which many boats are rowed for a prize.
regather: To gather or collect a second time.
regel: A fixed star of the first magnitude in Orion’s left foot.
regil: A fixed star of the first magnitude in Orion’s left foot.
regency: The district under the jurisdiction of a vicegerent.
The body of men entrusted with vicarious government; as a regency constituted during a king’s minority, insanity, or absence from the kingdom.
regenerate: To generate or produce anew; to reproduce. Through all the soil a genial ferment spreads, regenerates the plants and new adorns the meads.
In theology, to renew the heart by a change of affections; to change the heart and affections from natural enmity to the love of God; to implant holy affections in the heart.
Born anew; renovated in heart; changed from a natural to a spiritual state.
regenerating: Renovating the nature by the implantation of holy affections in the heart.
regeneration: Reproduction; the act of producing anew.

In theology, new birth by the grace of God; that change by which the will and natural enmity of man to God and his law are subdued, and a principle of supreme love to God and his law, or holy affections, are implanted in the heart. He saved us by the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit. Titus 3:5.
regeneratory: Renewing; having the power to renew; tending to reproduce or renovate.
regent: Ruling; governing; as a regent principle.
Exercising vicarious authority. Queen regent, a queen who governs; opposed to queen consort.
A governor; a ruler; in a general sense; as Uriel, regent of the sun.
One invested with vicarious authority; one who governs a kingdom in the minority, absence or disability of the king.
In colleges, a teacher of arts and sciences, having pupils under his care, generally of the lower classes; those who instruct the higher classes being called professors.
regentship: The power of governing, or the office of a regent.
regerminate: To germinate again. Perennial plants regerminate several years successively.
regicide: A king-killer; one who murders a king.
The killing or murder of a king.
regimen: In medicine, the regulation of diet with a view to the preservation or restoration of health; or in a more general sense, the regulation of all the non-naturals for the same purposes.
Any regulation or remedy which is intended to produce beneficial effects by gradual operation.

In grammar, government; that part of syntax or construction, which regulates the dependency of words, and the alterations which one occasions or requires in another in connection with it; the words governed.
regiment: In military affairs, a body of men, either horse, foot or artillery, commanded by a colonel or lieutenant colonel and major, and consisting of a number of companies, usually from eight to ten.
Government; mode of ruling; rule; authority; as used by Hooker, Hale and others.
To form into a regiment or into regiments with proper officers.
regimental: Belonging to a regiment; as regimental officers; regimental clothing.
regimentals: The uniform worn by the troops of a regiment.
regimented: Formed into a regiment; incorporated with a regiment.
region: A tract of land or space of indefinite extent, usually a tract of considerable extent. It is sometimes nearly synonymous with country; as all the region of Argob. Deuteronomy 3:4. He had dominion over all the region on this side the river. 7 Kings 4:24. So we speak of the airy region, the etherial regions, the upper regions, the lower regions.
The inhabitants of a region or district of country. Matthew 3:5.
A part of the body; as the region of the heart or liver.
Place; rank. He is of too high a region.
register: A written account or entry of acts, judgments or proceedings, for preserving and conveying to future times an exact knowledge of transactions. The word appropriately denotes an official account of the proceedings of a public body, a prince, a legislature, a court an incorporated company and the like, and in this use it is synonymous with record. But in a lax sense, it signifies any account entered on paper to preserve the remembrance of what is done.
The book in which a register or record is kept, as a parish register; also, a list, as the register of seamen. REGIISTER.

The officer or person whose business is to write or enter in a book accounts of transactions, particularly of the acts and proceedings of courts or other public bodies; as the register of a court of probate; a register of deeds.
In chimistry and the arts, an aperture with a lid, stopper or sliding plate, in a furnace, stovefor regulating the admission of air and the heat of the fire.
The inner part of the mold in which types are cast.
registrar: An officer in the English universities, who has the keeping of all the public records.
registration: The act of inserting in a register.
registry: The act of recording or writing in a register.
The place where a register is kept.
reglet: A ledge of wood exactly planed, used by printers to separate lines and make the work more open.
regnant: Reigning; exercising regal authority; as a queen regnant. The modern phrase is queen regent.
Ruling; predominant; prevalent; having the chief power; as vices regnant. We now say, reigning vices.
regorge: To vomit up; to eject from the stomach; to throw back or out again.
regrant: The act of granting back to a former proprietor.
regrate: To buy provisions and sell them again in the same market or fair; a practice which, by raising the price is a public offense and punishable. Regrating differs from engrossing and monopolizing, which signify the buying the whole of certain articles, or large quantities, and from forestalling, which signifies the purchase of provisions on the way, before they reach the market.
regrater: One who buys provisions and sells them in the same market or fair.
regrating: Purchasing provisions and selling them in the same market.
regreet: A return or exchange of salutation.
regress: Passage back; return; as ingress and regress.
The power of returning or passing back.
To go back; to return to a former place or state.
regression: The act of passing back or returning.
regressively: In a backward way or manner; by return.
regret: Grief; sorrow; pain of mind. We feel regret at the loss of friends, regret for our own misfortunes, or for the misfortunes of others. Never any prince expressed a more lively regret for the loss of a servant. Her piety itself would blame, if her regrets should waken thine.
