Pain of conscience; remorse; as a passionate regret at sin.
To grieve at; to lament; to be sorry for; to repent. Calmly he look’d on either life, and here saw nothing to regret, or there to fear.
regular: Conformed to a rule; agreeable to an established rule, law or principle, to a prescribed mode or to established customary forms; as a regular epic poem; a regular verse in poetry; a regular piece of music; regular practice of law or medicine; a regular plan; a regular building.
Governed by rule or rules; steady or uniform in a course or practice; as regular in diet; regular in attending on divine worship.

In geometry, a regular figure is one whose sides and angles are equal, as a square, a cube, or an equilateral triangle. Regular figures of more than three or four sides are usually called regular polygons.
Instituted or initiated according to established forms or discipline; as a regular physician.
Methodical; orderly; as a regular kind of sensuality or indulgence.
regularity: Agreeableness to a rule or to established order; as the regularity of legal proceedings.
Method; certain order. Regularity is the life of business.
Conformity to certain principles; as the regularity of a figure.
Steadiness or uniformity in a course; as the regularity of the motion of a heavenly body. There is no regularity in the vicissitudes of the weather.
regularly: In a manner accordant to a rule or established mode; as a physician or lawyer regularly admitted to practice; a verse regularly formed.
In uniform order; at certain intervals or periods; as day and night regularly returning.
Methodically; in due order; as affairs regularly performed.
regulate: To adjust by rule, method or established mode; as, to regulate weights and measures; to regulate the assize of bread; to regulate our moral conduct by the laws of God and of society; to regulate our manners by the customary forms.
To put in good order; as, to regulate the disordered state of a nation or its finances.
To subject to rules or restrictions; as, to regulate trade; to regulate diet.
regulated: Adjusted by rule, method or forms; put in good order; subjected to rules or restrictions.
regulating: Adjusting by rule, method or forms; reducing to order; subjecting to rules or restrictions.
regulation: The act of regulating or reducing to order.
A rule or order prescribed by a superior for the management of some business, or for the government of a company or society.
regulator: The small spring of a watch, which regulates its motions by retarding or accelerating them.
Any part of a machine which regulates its movements.
reguline: Pertaining to regulus or pure metal. Bodies which we can reduce to the metallic or reguline state.
regulize: To reduce to regulus or pure metal; to separate pure metal from extraneous matter.
regulus: In chimistry, the finer or pure part of a metallic substance, which, in the melting of ores, falls to the bottom of the crucible.
regurgitate: To throw or pour back as from a deep or hollow place; to pour or throw back in great quantity: .
To be thrown or poured back.
regurgitation: The act of swallowing § again; reabsorption.
rehabilitate: To restore to a former capacity; to reinstate; to qualify again; to restore, as a delinquent to a former right, rank or privilege lost or forfeited; a term of the civil and canon law.
rehabilitated: Restored to a former rank, right privilege or capacity; reinstated.
rehabilitating: Restoring to a former right, rank, privilege or capacity; reinstating.
rehabilitation: The act of reinstating in a former rank or capacity; restoration to former rights.
rehear: pret. and pp. reheard. To hear again; to try a second time; as, to rehear a cause in the court of king’s bench.
rehearing: In law, a second hearing or trial.
rehearsal: Recital; repetition of the words of another or of a written work; as the rehearsal of the Lord’s prayer.
Narration; a telling or recounting, as of particulars in detail; as the rehearsal of a soldier’s adventures.
The recital of a place before the public exhibition of it; as the rehearsal of a comedy.
rehearse: To recite; to repeat the words of a passage or composition; to repeat the words of another. When the words were heard which David spoke, they rehearsed them before Saul. 7 Samuel 17:31.
To narrate or recount events or transactions. There shall they rehearse the righteous acts of the Lord. Judges 5:11; Acts 11:4.
To recite or repeat in private for experiment and improvement, before a public representation; as, to rehearse a tragedy.
rehearsed: rehers’ed. Recited; repeated; as words; narrated.
rehearser: rehers’er. One who recites or narrates.
rehearsing: rehers’ing. Reciting; repeating words; recounting; telling; narrating.
reigle: A hollow cut or channel for guiding any thing; as the reigle of a side post for a flood gate.
reign: To possess or exercise sovereign power or authority; to rule; to exercise government, as a king or emperor; or to hold the supreme power. George the third reigned over Great Britain more than fifty years. Behold, a king shall reign in righteousness. Isaiah 32:1.
To be predominant; to prevail. Pestilent diseases which commonly reign in summer or autumn.
To rule; to have superior or uncontrolled dominion. Romans 6:12.
Royal authority; supreme power; sovereignty. He who like a father held his reign.
reigning: Holding or exercising supreme power; ruling; governing as king, queen or emperor.
a. Predominating; prevailing; as a reigning vice or disease.
reimbody: To imbody again; to be formed into a body anew.
reimbursable: That may be repaid. A loan has been made of two million of dollars, reimbursable in ten years.
reimburse: reimburs’. To refund; to replace in a treasury or in a private coffer, an equivalent to the sum taken from it, lost or expended; as, to reimburse the expenses of a war or a canal. The word is used before the person expending, or the treasury from which the advances are made, or before the expenses. We say, to reimburse the individual, to reimburse the treasury, or to reimburse the expenses. To reimburse the person, is to repay to him his losses, expenses or advances; to reimburse the treasury, is to refund to it the sum drawn from it; to reimburse losses or expenses, is to repay them or make them good.
reimbursed: Repaid; refunded; made good, as loss or expense.
reimbursement: reimburs’ment. The act of repaying or refunding; repayment; as the reimbursement of principal and interest.
reimburser: One who repays or refunds what has been lost or expended.
reimbursing: Repaying; refunding; making good, as loss or expense.
rein: The strap of a bridle, fastened to the curb or snaffle on each side, by which the rider of a horse restrains and governs him.
The instrument of curbing, restraining or governing; government. To give the reins, to give license; to leave without restraint. To take the reins, to take the guidance or government.
reindeer: A species of the cervine genus; more correctly written ranedeer, or rather rane, which is the true name.
reinforce: To give new force to; to strengthen by new assistance or support.
reinforcement: New force added; fresh supplies of strength; particularly, additional troops or ships.
reingratiate: To ingratiate again; to recommend again to favor.
reinlistment: The act of inlisting anew; the act of engaging again in military service.
reins: The kidneys; the lower part of the back.
In Scripture, the inward parts; the heart, or seat of the affections and passions. Psalm 73:21.
reinspection: The act of inspecting a second time.
reinstall: To install again; to seat anew.
reinstate: To place again in possession or in a former state; to restore to a state from which one has been removed; as, to reinstate a king in the possession of the kingdom; to reinstate one in the affections of his family.
reinstated: Replaced in possession or in a former state.
reinstatement: The act of putting in a former state; re-establishment.
reinstating: Replacing in a former state; putting again in possession.
reinsurance: An insurance of property already insured; a second insurance of the same property. Such reinsurance is permitted by the French commercial code; but in England is prohibited by statute, except when the first underwriter is insolvent.
reinsure: To insure the same property a second time by other underwriters. The insurer may cause the property insured to be reinsured by other persons.
reinsured: Insured a second time by other persons.
reinsuring: Insuring a second time by other persons.
reintegrate: To renew with regard to any state or quality; to restore.
reinterrogate: To interrogate again; to question repeatedly.
reinvestment: The act of investing anew; a second or repeated investment.
reinvigorate: To revive vigor in; to reanimate.
reiterate: To repeat; to repeat again and again; as reiterated crimes; to reiterate requests.
reject: To throw away, as any thing useless or vile.
To cast off. Have I rejected those that me ador'd?
To cast off; to forsake. Jeremiah 7:29.
To refuse to receive; to slight; to despise. Because thou has rejected knowledge, I will reject thee. Hosea 4:6; 1 Samuel 15:23.
To refuse to grant; as, to reject a prayer or request.
rejected: Thrown away; cast off; refused; slighted.
rejecting: Throwing away; casting off; refusing to grant or accept; slighting.
rejection: The act of throwing away; the act of casting off or forsaking; refusal to accept or grant.
rejective: That rejects, or tends to cast off.
rejoice: rejois’. To experience joy and gladness in a high degree; to be exhilarated with lively and pleasurable sensations; to exult. When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice; but when the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn. Proverbs 29:2. I will rejoice in thy salvation. Psalm 9:14.
rejois’. To make joyful; to gladden; to animate with lively pleasurable sensations; to exhilarate. Whoso loveth wisdom rejoiceth his father. Proverbs 29:3. While she, great saint, rejoices heaven.
rejoicing: Animating with gladness; exhilarating; feeling joy.
The act of expressing joy and gladness. The voice of rejoicing and salvation is in the tabernacles of the righteous. Psalm 118:15.
The subject of joy. Thy testimonies have I taken as an heritage forever, for they are the rejoicing of my heart. Psalm 119:117.
rejoin: To join again; to unite after separation.
In law pleadings, to answer, as the defendant to the plantif’s replication.
rejoinder: An answer to a reply; or in general, an answer.
In law pleadings, the defendants’s answer to the plantif’s replication.
rejoining: Joining again; answering a plaintiff's replication.
rejourn: rejurn’. To adjourn to another hearing or inquiry.
rejudge: rejuj’. To judge again; to re-examine; to review; to call to a new trial and decision.
rejuj’. To judge again; to re- examine; to review; to call to a new trial and decision. Rejudge his acts, and dignify disgrace.
rejuvenescence: A renewing of youth; the state of being young again.
rejuvenescency: A renewing of youth; the state of being young again.
rekindle: To kindle again; to set on fire anew.
To inflame again; to rouse anew.
reland: To land again; to put on land what had been shipped or embarked.
To go on shore after having embarked.
relapse: To slip or slide back; to return.
To fall back; to return to a former state or practice; as, to relapse into vice or error after amendment.
To fall back or return from recovery or a convalescent state; as, to relapse into a fever.
relaps’. A sliding or falling back, particularly into a former bad state, either of body or of morals; as a relapse into a disease from a convalescent state; a relapse into a vicious course of life.
relapser: One that relapses into vice or error.
relapsing: Sliding or falling back, as into disease or vice.
relate: To tell; to recite; to narrate the particulars of an event; as, to relate the story of Priam; to relate the adventures of Don Quixote.
To ally by connection or kindred. To relate one’s self, to vent thoughts in words.
To have reference or respect; to regard. All negative words relate to positive ideas.
related: a. Allied by kindred; connected by blood or alliance, particularly by consanguinity; as a person related in the first or second degree.
relater: One who tells, recites or narrates; a historian.
relating: a. Having relation or reference; concerning.
relation: The act of telling; recital; account; narration; narrative of facts; as a historical relation. We listened to the relation of his adventures.
Respect; reference; regard. I have been importuned to make some observations on this art, in relation to its agreement with poetry.

Connection between things; mutual respect, or what one thing is with regard to another; as the relation of a citizen to the state; the relation of a subject to the supreme authority; the relation of husband and wife, or of master and servant; the relation of a state of probation to a state of retribution.
Kindred; alliance; as the relation of parents and children. Relations dear, and all the charities of father, son and brother, first were known.
A person connected by consanguinity or affinity; a kinsman or kinswoman. He passed a month with his relations in the country.
relational: Having relation or kindred. We might be tempted to take these two nations for relational stems.
relationship: The state of being related by kindred, affinity or other alliance.
relative: Having relation; respecting. The arguments may be good, but they are not relative to the subject.

Not absolute or existing by itself; considered as belonging to or respecting something else. Every thing sustains both an absolute and a relative capacity; an absolute, as it is such a thing, endued with such a nature; and a relative, as it is a part of the universe, and so stands in such a relation to the whole.
Incident to man in society; as relative rights and duties.

Particular; positive. Relative made, in music, the mode which the composer interweaves with the principal mode in the flow of the harmony. Relative terms, in logic, terms which imply relation, as guardian and ward; master and servant; husband and wife. Relative word, in grammar, a word which relates to another word, called its antecedent, or to a sentence or member of a sentence, or to a series of sentences.
A person connected by blood or affinity; strictly, one allied by blood; a relation; a kinsman or kinswoman.' . Confining our care either to ourselves and relatives.
relatively: In relation or respect to something else; not absolutely. Consider the absolute affections of any being as it is in itself, before you consider it relatively.
relator: In law, one who brings an information in the nature of a quo warranto.
relax: To slacken; to make less tense or rigid; as, to relax a rope or cord; to relax the muscles or sinews; to relax the reins in riding.
To loosen; to make less close or firm; as, to relax the joints.
To make less severe or rigorous; to remit or abate in strictness; as, to relax a law or rule of justice; to relax a demand.
To remit or abate in attention, assiduity or labor; as, to relax study; to relax exertions or efforts.
To unbend; to ease; to relieve from close attention; as, conversation relaxes the student or the mind.
relaxation: The act of slackening or remitting tension; as a relaxation of the muscles, fibers or nerves; a relaxation of the whole system.
Remission or abatement of rigor; as a relaxation of the law.
Remission of attention or application; as a relaxation of mind, study or business.
relaxed: Slackened; loosened; remitted or abated in rigor or in closeness; made less vigorous; languid.
relaxing: Slackening; loosening; remitting or abating in rigor, severity or attention; rendering languid.
relay: A supply of horses placed on the road to be in readiness to relieve others, that a traveler may proceed without delay.
Hunting dogs kept in readiness at certain places to pursue the game, when the dogs that have been in pursuit are weary.
To lay again; to lay a second time; as, to relay a pavement.
release: To set free from restraint of any kind, either physical or moral; to liberate from prison, confinement or servitude. Matthew 27:15; Mark 15:6.
To free from pain, care, trouble, grief.
To free from obligation or penalty; as, to release one from debt, from a promise or covenant.
To quit; to let go, as a legal claim; as, to release a debt or forfeiture. Deuteronomy 15:1-3.

To discharge or relinquish a right to lands or tenements, by conveying it to another that has some right or estate in possession, as when the person in remainder releases his right to the tenant in possession; when one co-parcener releases his right to the other; or the mortgagee releases his claim to the mortgager.
released: Set free from confinement; freed from obligation or liability; freed from pain; quitclaimed.
releasement: The act of releasing from confinement or obligation.
releasing: Liberating from confinement or restraint; freeing from obligation or responsibility, or from pain or other evil; quitclaiming.
relegate: To banish; to send into exile.
relent: To soften; to become less rigid or hard; to give. In some houses, sweetmeats will relent more than in others. When op’ning buds salute the welcome day, and earth relenting feels the genial ray.
To grow moist; to deliquesce; applied to salts; as the relenting of the air. Salt of tartar - placed in a cellar, will begin to relent.
To soften in temper; to become more mild and tender; to feel compassion. Can you behold my tears, and not once relent?
To slacken. And oftentimes he would relent his pace. Obs.
relenting: Softening in temper; becoming more mild or compassionate.
The act of becoming more mild or compassionate.
relentless: Unmoved by pity unpitying; insensible to the distress of others; destitute of tenderness; as a pray to relentless despotism. For this th’ avenging powr employs his’ darts, Thus will persist relentless in his ire. Relentless thoughts, in Milton, may signify unremitted, intently fixed on disquieting objects.
relessee: The person to whom a release is executed.
relessor: The person who executes a release. There must be a privity of estate between the relessor and release.
relevance: The state of being relevant, or of affording relief or aid.
In Scots law, sufficiency to infer the conclusion.
relevancy: The state of being relevant, or of affording relief or aid.
In Scots law, sufficiency to infer the conclusion.
relevant: Pertinent; applicable. The testimony is not relevant to the case. The argument is not relevant to the question.
reliance: Rest or repose of mind, resulting from a full belief of the veracity or integrity of a person, or of the certainty of a fact; trust; confidence; dependence. We may have perfect reliance on the promises of God; we have reliance on the testimony of witnesses; we place reliance on men of known integrity, or on the strength and stability of government.
relic: That which remains; that which is left after the loss or decay of the rest; as the relics of a town; the relics of magnificence; the relics of antiquity. The relics of saints, real or pretended, are held in great veneration by the catholics.
The body of a deceased person; a corpse.
relict: A widow; a woman whose husband is dead.
relief: The removal, in whole or in part, of any evil that afflicts the body of mind; the removal or alleviation of pain, grief, want, care, anxiety, toil or distress, or of any thing oppressive or burdensome, by which some ease is obtained. Rest gives relief to the body when weary; an anodyne gives relief from pain; the sympathy of friends affords some relief to the distressed; a loan of money to a man embarrassed may afford him a temporary relief; medicines which will not cure a disease, sometimes give a partial relief. A complete relief from the troubles of life is never to be expected.
That which mitigates or removes pain, grief or other evil.
The dismission of a sentinel from his post, whose place is supplied by another soldier; also, the person who takes his place.

In sculpturethe projecture or prominence of a figure above or beyond the ground or plane on which it is formed. Relief is of three kinds; high relief low relief and demi relief The difference is in the degree of projecture. High relief is formed from nature, as when a figure projects as much as the life. Low relief is when the figure projects but little, as in medals, festoons, foliages and other ornaments. Demi relief is when one half of the figure rises from the plane.
In painting, the appearance of projection, or the degree of boldness which a figure exhibits to the eye at a distance.
relier: One who relies, or places full confidence in.
relievable: Capable of being relieved; that may receive relief.
relieve: To free, wholly or partially, from pain, grief, want, anxiety, care, toil, trouble, burden, oppression or any thing that is considered to be an evil; to ease of any thing that pains the body or distresses the mind. Repose relieves the wearied body; a supply of provisions relieves a family in want; medicines may relieve the sick man, even when they do not cure him. We all desire to be relieved from anxiety and from heavy taxes. Law or duty, or both, require that we should relieve the poor and destitute.
To alleviate or remove; as when we say, to relieve pain or distress; to relieve the wants of the poor.

To dismiss from a post or station, as sentinels, a guard or ships, and station others in their place. Sentinels are generally relieved every two hours; a guard is usually relieved once in twenty four hours.
To right; to ease of any burden, wrong or oppression by judicial or legislative interposition, by the removal of a grievance, by indemnification for losses and the like.

To abate the inconvenience of any thing by change, or by the interposition of something dissimilar. The moon relieves the luster of the sun with a milder light. The poet must not encumber his poem with, too much business, but sometimes relieve the subject with a moral reflection.
relieved: Freed from pain or other evil; eased or cured; aided; succored; dismissed from watching.
Alleviated or removed; as pain or distress.
reliever: One that relieves; he or that which gives ease.
relieving: Removing pain or distress, or abating the violence of it; easing; curing; assisting; dismissing from a post, as a sentinel; supporting.
relievo: Relief; prominence of figures in statuary, architecture; apparent prominence of figures in painting.
relight: To light anew; to illuminate again.
To rekindle; to set on fire again.
religion: Religion, in its most comprehensive sense, includes a belief in the being and perfections of God, in the revelation of his will to man, in man’s obligation to obey his commands, in a state of reward and punishment, and in man’s accountableness to God; and also true godliness or piety of life, with the practice of all moral duties. It therefore comprehends theology, as a system of doctrines or principles, as well as practical piety; for the practice of moral duties without a belief in a divine lawgiver, and without reference to his will or commands, is not religion.

Religion, as distinct from theology, is godliness or real piety in practice, consisting in the performance of all known duties to God and our fellow men, in obedience to divine command, or from love to God and his law. James 1:27.

Religion, as distinct from virtue, or morality, consists in the performance of the duties we owe directly to God, from a principle of obedience to his will. Hence we often speak of religion and virtue, as different branches of one system, or the duties of the first and second tables of the law. Let us with caution indulge the supposition, that morality can be maintained without religion.

Any system of faith and worship. In this sense, religion comprehends the belief and worship of pagans and Mohammedans, as well as of christians; any religion consisting in the belief of a superior power or powers governing the world, and in the worship of such power or powers. Thus we speak of the religion of the Turks, of the Hindoos, of the Indiansas well as of the christian religion. We speak of false religion, as well as of true religion.
religionist: A bigot to any religious persuasion.
religious: Pertaining or relating to religion; as a religious society; a religious sect; a religious place; religious subjects.
Pious; godly; loving and reverencing the Supreme Being and obeying his precepts; as a religious man.
Devoted to the practice of religion; as a religious life.
Teaching religion; containing religious subject or the doctrines and precepts of religion, or the discussion of topics of religion; as a religious book.
Exact; strict; such as religion requires; as a religious observance of vows or promises.
religiously: Piously; with love and reverence to the Supreme Being; in obedience to the divine commands.
According to the rites of religion.
Exactly; strictly; conscientiously; as a vow or promise religiously observed.
religiousness: The quality or state of being religious.
relinquish: To withdraw from; to leave; to quit. It may be to forsake or abandon, but it does not necessarily express the sense of the latter. A man may relinquish an enterprise for a time, or with a design never to resume it. In general, to relinquish is to leave without the intention of resuming, and equivalent to forsake, but is less emphatical than abandon and desert. They placed Irish tenants on the lands relinquished by the English.
To forbear; to withdraw from; as, to relinquish the practice of intemperance; to relinquish the rites of a church.
To give up; to renounce a claim to; as, to relinquish a debt. To relinquish back, or to, to give up; to release; to surrender; as, to relinquish a claim to another.
relinquishment: The act of leaving or quitting; a forsaking; the renouncing a claim to.
reliquary: A depository for relics; a casket in which relics are kept.
reliquidate: To liquidate anew; to adjust a second time.
reliquidation: A second or renewed liquidation; a renewed adjustment.
relish: Taste; or rather, a pleasing taste; that sensation of the organs which is experienced when we take food or drink of an agreeable flavor. Different persons have different relishes. Relish is often natural, and often the effect of habit.
Liking; delight; appetite. We have such a relish for faction, as to have lost that of wit.
Sense; the faculty of perceiving excellence; taste; as a relish for fine writing, or a relish of fine writing. Addison uses both of and for after relish.
That which gives pleasure; the power of pleasing. When liberty is gone, life grows insipid and has lost its relish.
Cast; manner. It preserves some relish of old writing.
relished: Giving an agreeable taste; received with pleasure.
relive: reliv’. To live again; to revive.
reloan: To loan again; to lend what has been lent and repaid.
A second lending of the same money.
relucent: Shining; transparent; clear; pellucid; as a relucent stream.
reluctance: Unwillingness; great opposition of mind; repugnance; with to or against; as, to undertake a war with reluctance. He has a great reluctance to this measure. Bear witness, heav’n with what reluctancy her helpless innocence I doom to die.
reluctancy: Unwillingness; great opposition of mind; repugnance; with to or against; as, to undertake a war with reluctance. He has a great reluctance to this measure. Bear witness, heav’n with what reluctancy her helpless innocence I doom to die.
reluctant: Striving against; unwilling; much opposed in heart. Reluctant now I touch’d the trembling string.
Unwilling; acting with slight repugnance; coy.
Proceeding from an unwilling mind; granted with reluctance; as reluctant obedience.
reluctantly: With opposition of heart; unwillingly. What is undertaken reluctantly is seldom well performed.
rely: To rest on something, as the mind when satisfied of the veracity, integrity or ability of persons, or of the certainty of facts or of evidence; to have confidence in; to trust in; to depend; with on. We rely on the promise of a man who is known to be upright; we rely on the veracity or fidelity of a tried friend; a prince relies on the affections of his subjects for support, and on the strength of his army for success in war; above all things, we rely on the mercy and promises of God. That which is the ground of confidence, is a certainty or full conviction that satisfies the mind and leaves it at rest, or undisturbed by doubt. Because thou has relied on the king of Syria, and not relied on the Lord thy God - 2 Chronicles 16:8.
relying: Reposing on something, as the mind; confiding in; trusting in; depending.
remain: To continue; to rest or abide in a place for a time indefinite. They remained a month in Rome. We remain at an inn for a night, for a week, or a longer time. Remain a widow at thy father’s house, till Shelah my son be grown. Genesis 38:11.

To be left after others have withdrawn; to rest or abide in the same place when others remove, or are lost, destroyed or taken away. Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark. Genesis 7:23.
To be left after a part or others have past. Let our remaining time or years be employed in active duties.
To continue unchanged, or in a particular state. He remains stupid; he remains in a low state of health.
Not to be lost; not to escape; not to be forgotten. All my wisdom remained with me.
remainder: Any thing left after the separation and removal of a part. If these decoctions be repeated till the water comes off clear, the remainder yields no salt. The last remainders of unhappy Troy.
Relics; remains; the corpse of a human being.
That which is left after a part is past; as the remainder of the day or week; the remainder of the year; the remainder of life.
The sum that is left after subtraction or after any deduction.

In law, an estate limited to take effect and be enjoyed after another estate is determined. A grants land to B for twenty years; remainder to D in fee. If a man by deed or will limits his books or furniture to A for life, with remainder to B, this remainder is good. A writ of formedon in remainder, is a writ which lies where a man gives lands to another for life or in tail, with remainder to a third person in tail or in fee, and he who has the particular estate dies without issue heritable, and a stranger intrudes upon him in remainder and keeps him out of possession; in this case, the remainder-man shall have his writ of formedon in the remainder.
remainder-man: In law, he who has an estate after a particular estate is determined.
remaining: Continuing; resting; abiding for an indefinite time; being left after separation and removal of a part, or after loss or destruction, or after a part is passed, as of time.
remains: That which is left after a part is separated, taken away or destroyed; as the remains of a city or house demolished.
A dead body; a corpse. The singular, remain, in the like sense, and in the sense of abode, is entirely obsolete.
remake: pret. and pp. remade. To make anew.
remand: To call or send back him or that which is ordered to a place; as, to remand an officer from a distant place; to remand an envoy from a foreign court.
remark: Notice or observation, particularly notice or observation expressed in words or writing; as the remarks of an advocate; the remarks made in conversation; the judicious or the uncandid remarks of a critic. A remark is not always expressed, for we say, aman makes his remarks on a preacher’s sermon while he is listening to it. In this case the notice is silent, a mere act of the mind.
To observe; to note in the mind; to take notice of without expression. I remarked the manner of the speaker; I remarked his elegant expressions.
To express in words or writing what one thinks or sees; to express observations; as, it is necessary to repeat what has been before remarked.
To mark; to point out; to distinguish. His manacles remark him.
remarkable: Observable; worthy of notice. ‘Tis remarkable that they talk most, who have the least to say.

Extraordinary; unusual; that deserves particular notice, or that may excite admiration or wonder; as the remarkable preservation of lives in shipwreck. The dark day in May, 1780, was a remarkable phenomenon.N .
remarkableness: Observableness; worthiness of remark; the quality of deserving particular notice.
remarkably: In a manner or degree worthy of notice; as, the winters of 1825, 1826 and 1828 were remarkably free from snow. The winter of was remarkable for a great quantity of snow.
remarked: Noticed; observed; expressed in words or writing.
remarker: An observer; one who makes remarks.
remarking: Observing; taking notice of; expressing in words or writing.
remarried: Married again or a second time.
remarry: To marry again or a second time.
remarrying: Marrying again or a second time.
remasticate: To chew or masticate again; to chew over and over, as in chewing the cud.
remasticating: Chewing again or over and over.
remastication: The act of masticating again or repeatedly.
remediable: That may be remedied or cured. The evil is believed to be remediable.
remedial: Affording a remedy; intended for a remedy, or for the removal of an evil. The remedial part of law is so necessary a consequence of the declaratory and directory, that laws without it must be very vague and imperfect. Statutes are declaratory of remedial.
remediate: in the sense of remedial, is not in use.
remediless: Not admitting a remedy; incurable; desperate; as a remediless disease.
lrreparable; as, a loss or damage is remediless.
Not admitting change or reversal; as a remediless doom.
Not admitting recovery; as a remediless delusion.
remedilessly: In a manner or degree that precludes a remedy.
remedy: That which cures a disease; any medicine or application which puts an end to disease and restores health; with for; as a remedy for the gout.

That which counteracts an evil of any kind; with for, to or against; usually with for. Civil government is the remedy for the evils of natural liberty. What remedy can be provided for extravagance in dress? The man who shall invent an effectual remedy for intemperance, will deserve every thing from his fellow men.
That which cures uneasiness. Our griefs how swift, our remedies how slow.
That which repairs loss or disaster; reparation. In the death of a man there is no remedy.
To cure; to heal; as, to remedy a disease.
remedying: Curing; healing; removing; restoring from a bad to a good state.
remember: To have in the mind an idea which had been in the mind before, and which recurs to the mind without effort. We are said to remember any thing, when the idea of it arises in the mind with the consciousness that we have had this idea before.

When we use effort to recall an idea, we are said to recollect it. This distinction is not always observed. Hence remember is often used as synonymous with recollect, that is, to call to mind. We say, we cannot remember a fact, when we mean, we cannot recollect it . Remember the days’ of old. Deuteronomy 32:7.
To bear or keep in mind; to attend to. Remember what I warn thee; shun to taste.
To preserve the memory of; to preserve from being forgotten. Let them have their wages duly paid, and something over to remember me.
remembrance: The retaining or having in mind an idea which had been present before, or an idea which had been previously received from an object when present, and which recurs to the mind afterwards without the presence of its object. Technically, remembrance differs from reminiscence and recollection, as the former implies that an idea occurs to the mind spontaneously, or without much mental exertion. The latter imply the power or the act of recalling ideas which do not spontaneously recur to the mind. The righteous shall be in everlasting remembrance. Psalm 112:6. Remembrance is when the same idea recurs, without the operation of the like object on the’ external sensory.
Transmission of a fact from one to another. Titan among the heav’ns th’ immortal fact display’d, lest the remembrance of his grief should fall.
Account preserved; something to assist the memory. Those proceedings and remembrances are in the Tower.
Memorial. But in remembrance of so brave a deed, a tomb and funeral honors I decreed.
A token by which one is kept in the memory. Keep this remembrance for thy Julia’s sake .
remembrancer: One that reminds, or revives the remembrance of any thing. God is present in the consciences of good and bad; he is there a remembrancer to call our’ actions to mind.

An officer in the exchequer of England, whose business is to record certain papers and proceedings, make out processes; a recorder. The officers bearing this name were formerly called clerks of the remembrance.
rememorate: To remember; to revive in the memory.
remorselessness: remors’lessness. Savage cruelty; insensibility to distress.
remote: Distant in place; not near; as a remote country; a remote people. Give me a life remote from guilty courts.
Distant in time, past or future; as remote antiquity. Every man is apt to think the time of his dissolution to be remote.
Distant; not immediate. It is not all remote and even apparent good that affects us.
Distant; primary; not proximate; as the remote causes of a disease.
Alien; foreign; not agreeing with; as a proposition remote from reason.
remotely: At a distance in space or time; not nearly: .
At a distance in consanguinity or affinity.
Slightly; in a small degree; as, to be remotely affected by an event.
remoteness: State of being distant in space or time; distance; as the remoteness of a kingdom or of a star; the remoteness of the deluge from our age; the remoteness of a future event, of an evil or of success.
Distance in operation or efficiency; as the remoteness of causes.
Slightness; smallness; as remoteness of resemblance.
remotion: The act of removing; the state of being removed to a distance.
remount: To mount again; as, to remount a horse.
removability: The capacity of being removable from an office or station; capacity of being displaced.
removable: That may be removed from an office or station. Such curate is removable at the pleasure of the rector of the mother church.
That may be removed from one place to another.
removal: The act of moving from one place to another for residence; as the removal of a family.
The act of displacing from an office or post.
The act of curing or putting away; as the removal of a disease.
The state of being removed; change of place.
The act of putting an end to; as the removal of a grievance.
remove: To cause to change place; to put from its place in any manner; as, to remove a building. Thou shalt not remove thy neighbor’s landmark. Deuteronomy 19:14.
To take or put away in any manner; to cause to leave a person or thing; to banish or destroy; as, to remove a disease or complaint. Remove sorrow from thine heart. Ecclesiastes 11:10.
To carry from one court to another; as, to remove a cause or suit by appeal.
To take from the present state of being; as, to remove one by death.
removed: Changed in place; carried to a distance; displaced from office; placed far off.
remover: One that removes; as a remover of landmarks.
removing: changing place; carrying or going from one place to another; displacing; banishing.
remunerable: That may be rewarded; fit or proper to be recompensed.
remunerate: To reward; to recompense; to requite; in a good sense; to pay an equivalent to for any service, loss, expense or other sacrifice; as, to remunerate the troops of an army for their services and sufferings; to remunerate men for labor. the pious usfferer in this life will be remunerated in the life to come.
remuneration: Reward; recompense; the act of paying an equivalent for services, loss or sacrifices.
The equivalent given for services, loss or sufferings.
remunerative: Exercised in rewarding; that bestows rewards; as remunerative justice.
remurmur: To utter back in murmurs; to return in murmurs; to repeat in low hoarse sounds. The trembling trees in every plain and wood, her fate remurmur to the silver flood.
to murmur back; to return or echo in low rumbling sounds. The realms of Mars remurmur’d all around.
renal: Pertaining to the kidneys or reins; as the renal arteries.
renard: a fox; a name used in fables, but not in common discourse.
renascency: The state of springing or being produced again.
renascent: Springing or rising into being again; reproduced.
renascible: That may be reproduced; that may spring again into being.
renavigate: To navigate again; as, to renavigate the Pacific ocean.
rencounter: Literally, a meeting of two bodies. Hence,
A meeting in opposition or contest. The jostling chiefs in rude rencounter join.
A casual combat; a sudden contest or fight without premeditation; as between individuals or small parties.
A casual action; an engagement between armies or fleets. The confederates should - outnumber the enemy in all rencounters and engagements.
rend: To separate any substance into parts with force or sudden violence; to tear asunder; to split; as, powder rends a rock in blasting; lightning rends an oak. An empire from its old foundation rent. I rend my tresses, and by breast I wound. Neither rend your clothes, lest ye die. Leviticus 10:6.

To separate or part with violence. I will surely rend the kingdom from thee. 7 Kings 11:11. To rend the heart, in Scripture, to have bitter sorrow for sin. Joel 2:13. To rend the heavens, to appear in majesty. Isaiah 64:7. Rend differs somewhat from lacerate. We never say, to lacerate a rock or a kingdom, when we mean to express splitting or division. Lacerate is properly applicable to the tearing off of small pieces of a thing, as to lacerate the body with a whip or scourge; or to the tearing of the flesh or other thing without entire separation.
render: To return; to pay back. See that none render evil for evil to any man. 7 Thessalonians 5:15.
To inflict, as a retribution. I will render vengeance to my enemies. Deuteronomy 32:471.
To give on demand; to give; to assign. The sluggard is wiser in his own conceit, than seven men that can render a reason. Proverbs 26:16.

To make or cause to be, by some influence upon a thing, or by some change; as, to render a person more safe or more unsafe; to render him solicitous or cautious; to render a fortress more secure or impregnable; to render a ferocious animal more mild and tractable: .
rendered: Returned; paid back; given; assigned; made; translated; surrendered; afforded.
rendering: Returning; giving back; assigning; making; translating; surrendering; affording.
rendezvous: A place appointed for the assembling of troops, or the place where they assemble; or the port or place where ships are ordered to join company.
A place of meeting, or a sign that draws men together.
To assemble at a particular place, as troops. The place where the Gauls and Bruti had rendezvoused .
To assemble or bring together at a certain place.
rendible: That may be yielded or surrendered.
rendition: The act of yielding possession; surrender.
renegade: One who deserts to an enemy; a deserter.
renegado: One who deserts to an enemy; a deserter.
renerve: renerv’. To nerve again; to give new vigor to.
renew: To renovate; to restore to a former state, or to a good state, after decay or depravation; to rebuild; to repair. Asa renewed the altar of the Lord.2 Chronicles 15:8.
To re-establish; to confirm. Let us go to Gilgal and renew the kingdom there.7 Samuel 11:14.
To make again; as, to renew a treaty or covenant.
To repeat; as, to renew expressions of friendship; to renew a promise; to renew an attempt.
To revive; as, to renew the glories of an ancestor or of a former age.
renewable: That may be renewed; as a lease renewable at pleasure.
renewal: The act of renewing; the act of forming anew; as the renewal of a treaty: .
Revival; restoration to a former or to a good state.
renewed: Made new again; repaired; re-established; repeated; revived; renovated; regenerated.
renewing: Making new again; repairing; re-establishing; repeating; reviving; renovating.
a. Tending or adapted to renovate.
The act of making new; renewal.
reniform: Having the form or shape of the kidneys.
renitence: The resistance of a body to pressure; the effort of matter to resume the place or form from which it has been driven by the impulse of other matter; the effect of elasticity.
Moral resistance; reluctance. We find a renitency in ourselves to ascribe life and irritability to the cold and motionless fibers of plants.
renitency: The resistance of a body to pressure; the effort of matter to resume the place or form from which it has been driven by the impulse of other matter; the effect of elasticity.
Moral resistance; reluctance. We find a renitency in ourselves to ascribe life and irritability to the cold and motionless fibers of plants.
renitent: Resisting pressure or the effect of it; acting against impulse by elastic force.
rennet: The concreted milk found in the stomach of a sucking quadruped, particularly of the calf. It is also written runnet, and this is the preferable orthography.
renounce: To disown; to disclaim; to reject; as a title or claim; to refuse to own or acknowledge as belonging to; as, to renounce a title to land or a claim to reward; to renounce all pretensions to applause.
To deny; to cast off; to reject; to disclaim; as an obligation or duty; as, to renounce allegiance.
To cast off or reject, as a connection or possession; to forsake; as, to renounce the world and all its cares. We have renounced the hidden things of dishonesty. 2 Corinthians 4:2.
renouns’. To declare a renunciation. He of my sons who falls to make it good, by one rebellious act renounces to my blood.
renouns’. The declining to follow suit, when it can be done.
renouncement: renouns’ment. The act of disclaiming or rejecting; renunciation.
renouncing: The act of disowning, disclaiming, denying or rejecting.
renovate: To renew; to restore to the first state, or to a good state, after decay, destruction or depravation. It is synonymous with renew, except in its fourth definition.
renovated: Renewed; made new, fresh or vigorous.
renovation: The act of renewing; a making new after decay, destruction or depravation; renewal; as the renovation of the heart by grace. There is something inexpressibly pleasing in the annual renovation of the world.
renown: Fame; celebrity; exalted reputation derived from the extensive praise of great achievements or accomplishments. Giants of old, men of renown. Genesis 6:4; Numbers 16:2.
To make famous. Soft elocution does thy style renown. A bard whom pilfer’d pastorals renown.
renowned: Famous; celebrated for great and heroic achievements, for distinguished qualities or for grandeur; eminent; as renowned men; a renowned king; a renowned city.
rent: of rend. Torn asunder; split or burst by violence; torn.
A fissure; a break or breach made by force; as a rent made in the earth, in a rock or in a garment.
A schism; a separation; as a rent in the church.
rental: A schedule or account of rents.
renter: One who leases an estate; more generally, the lessee or tenant who takes an estate or tenement on rent.
To fine-draw; to sew together the edges of two pieces of cloth without doubling them, so that the seam is scarcely visible.
In tapestry, to work new warp into a piece of damaged tapestry, and on this to restore the original pattern or design.
To sew up artfully, as a rent.
renting: Leasing on rent; taking on rent.
rentroll: A rental; a list or account of rents or income.
renunciation: The act of renouncing; a disowning; rejection.
renverse: renvers’. In heraldry, inverted; set with the head downward or contrary to the natural posture.
reoppose: s as z. To oppose again.
reordain: To ordain again, as when the first ordination is defective.
reorganization: The act of organizing anew; as repeated reorganization of the troops.
reorganize: To organize anew; to reduce again to a regular body, or to a system; as, to reorganize a society or an army.
repack: To pack a second time; as, to repack beef or pork.
repair: To restore to a sound or good state after decay, injury, dilapidation or partial destruction; as, to repair a house, a wall or a ship; to repair roads and bridges. Temperance and diet may repair a broken or enfeebled constitution. Food repairs the daily waste of the body.
To rebuild a part decayed or destroyed; to fill up; as, to repair a breach.
To make amends, as for an injury, by an equivalent; to indemnify for; as, to repair a loss or damage.
Restoration to a sound or good state after decay, waste, injury or partial destruction; supply of loss; reparation; as, materials are collected for the repair of a church or a city.
To go to; to betake one’s self; to resort; as, to repair to a sanctuary for safety. Go, mount the winds and to the shades repair.
repaired: Restored to a good or sound state; rebuilt; made good.
repairer: One who repairs, restores or makes amends; as the repairer of decay.
repairing: Restoring to a sound state; rebuilding; making amends for loss or injury.
repand: In botany, a repand leaf is one, the rim of which is terminated by angles having sinuses between them, inscribed in the segment of a circle; or which has a bending or waved margin, without any angles; of which is bordered with numerous minute angles and small segments of circles alternately.
reparable: That may be repaired or restored to a sound or good state; as, a house or wall is not reparable.
That may be retrieved or made good; as, the loss is reparable.
That may be supplied by an equivalent; as a reparable injury.
reparably: In a manner admitting of restoration to a good state, or of amends, supply or indemnification.
reparation: That act of repairing; restoration to soundness or a good state; as the reparation of a bridge or of a highway.
Supply of what is wasted; as the reparation of decaying health or strength after disease or exhaustion.
Amends; indemnification for loss or damage. A loss may be too great for reparation.
Amends; satisfaction for injury. I am sensible of the scandal I have given by my loose writings, and make what reparation I am able.
reparative: That repairs; restoring to a sound or good state; that amends defect or makes good.
That which restores to a good state; that which makes amends.
repartee: A smart, ready and witty reply. Cupid was as bad as he; hear but the youngster’s repartee.
To make smart and witty replies.
repass: To pass again; to pass or travel back; as, to repass a bridge or a river; to repass the sea.
To pass or go back; to move back; as troops passing and repassing before our eyes.
repast: The act of taking food; or the food taken; a meal. From dance to sweet repast they turn. A repast without luxury.
Good; victuals. Go, and get me some repast.
repay: To pay back; to refund; as, to repay money borrowed or advanced.
To make return or requital; in a good or bad sense; as, to repay kindness; to repay an injury. Benefits which cannot be repaid - are not commonly found to increase affection.
To recompense, as for a loss.
To compensate; as false honor repaid in contempt.
repayable: That is to be repaid or refunded; as money lent, repayable at the end of sixty days.
repayment: The act of paying back; reimbursement.
The money or other thing repaid.
repeal: To recall, as a deed, will, law or statute; to revoke; to abrogate by an authoritative act, or by the same power that made or enacted; as, the legislature may repeal at one session, a law enacted at a preceding one.
Revocation; abrogation; as the repeal of a statute.
repealable: Capable of being repealed; revocable by the same power that enacted. It is held as a sound principle, that charters or grants which vest rights in individuals or corporations, are not repealable without the consent of the grantees, unless a clause reserving the right is inserted in the act.
repeat: To do, make, attempt or utter again; to iterate; as, to repeat an action; to repeat an attempt or exertion; to repeat a word or discourse; to repeat a song; to repeat an argument.
To try again. I the danger will repeat.
to recite; to rehearse. He repeated some lines of Virgil. To repeat signals, in the navy, is to make the same signal which the admiral or commander has made, or to make a signal again.
In music, a mark directing a part to be repeated in performance.
repeated: done, attempted or spoken again; recited.
repeatedly: More than once; again and againHe has been repeatedly warned of his danger.
repeater: One that repeats; one that recites or rehearses.
A watch that strikes the hours at will, by the compression of a spring.
repel: to drive back; to force to return; to check advance; as, to repel an enemy or an assailant. Hippomedon repell’d the hostile tide. And virtue may repel, though not invade.
To resist; to oppose; as, to repel an argument.
To act with force in opposition to force impressed. Electricity sometimes attracts and sometimes repels.
In medicine, to check an afflux to a part of the body.
repellency: The principle of repulsion; the quality of a substance which expands or separates particles and enlarges the volume; as the repellency of heat.
The quality that repels, drives back or resists approach; as the repellency of the electric fluid.
repellent: Driving back; able or tending to repel.

In medicine, a medicine which drives back morbid humors into the mass of the blood, from which they were unduly secreted; or which prevents such an afflux of fluid to a part, as would raise it to a tumor; a discutient.
repelling: Driving back; resisting advance or approach effectually.
repent: To feel pain, sorrow or regret for something done or spoken; as, to repent that we have lost much time in idleness or sensual pleasure; to repent that we have injured or wounded the feelings of a friend. A person repents only of what he himself has done or said.
To express sorrow for something past. Enobarbus did before thy face repent.
To change the mind in consequence of the inconvenience or injury done by past conduct. Lest peradventure the people repent when they see war, and they return. Exodus 13:17.
Applied to the Supreme Being, to change the course of providential dealings. Genesis 6:6; Psalm 106:45.
repentance: Sorrow for any thing done or said; the pain or grief which a person experiences in consequence of the injury or inconvenience produced by his own conduct.

In theology, the pain, regret or affliction which a person feels on account of his past conduct, because it exposes him to punishment. This sorrow proceeding merely from the fear of punishment, is called legal repentance, as being excited by the terrors of legal penalties, and it may exist without an amendment of life.

Real penitence; sorrow or deep contrition for sin, as an offense and dishonor to God, a violation of his holy law, and the basest ingratitude towards a Being of infinite benevolence. This is called evangelical repentance, and is accompanied and followed by amendment of life. Repentance is a change of mind, or a conversion from sin to God. Godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation. 2 Corinthians 7:10; Matthew 3:8. Repentance is the relinquishment of any practice, from conviction that it has offended God.
repentant: Sorrowful for past conduct or words.
Expressing or showing sorrow for sin; as repentant tears; repentant ashes; repentant sighs.
One that expresses sorrow for sin.
repenting: Grieving for what is past; feeling pain or contrition for sin.
repeople: To people anew; to furnish again with a stock of people. The world after the flood was repeopled by the descendants of one family.
repeopling: Furnishing again with a stock of inhabitants.
The act of furnishing again with inhabitants.
repercussion: The act of driving back; reverberation; as the repercussion of sound.
In music, frequent repetition of the same sound.
repercussive: Driving back; having the power of sending back; causing to reverberate; as repercussive rocks.
repertory: A place in which things are disposed in an orderly manner, so that they can be easily found, as the index of a book, a common- place book.
repetend: The parts of decimals continually repeated.
repetition: The act of doing or uttering a second time; iteration of the same act, or of the same words or sounds.
The act of reciting or rehearsing; the act of reading over.
Recital from memory, as distinct from reading.
In music, the art of repeating, singing or playing the same part a second time.
repine: To fret one’s self; to be discontented; to feel inward discontent which preys on the spirits; with at or against. It is our duty never to repine at the allotments of Providence.
To complain discontentedly; to murmur. Multitudes repine at the want of that which nothing but idleness hinders them from enjoying.
repining: Fretting one’s self; feeling discontent that preys on the spirits; complaining; murmuring.
a. Disposed to murmur or complain; as a repining temper.
The act of fretting or feeling discontent or of murmuring.
replace: To put again in the former place; as, to replace a book. The earl - was replaced in his government.
To put in a new place.
To repay; to refund; as, to replace a sum of money borrowed .
To put a competent substitute in the place of another displaced or of something lost. The paper is lost and cannot be replaced.
replaced: Put again in a former place; supplied by a substitute. Thus in petrification, the animal or vegetable substance gradually wastes away, and is replaced by silex.
replacing: Putting again in a former place; supplying the place of with a substitute.
replait: To plait or fold again; to fold one part over another again and again.
repleader: In law, a second pleading or course of pleadings; or the power of pleading again. Whenever a repleader is granted, the pleadings must begin de novo.
replenish: To fill; to stock with numbers or abundance. The magazines are replenished with corn. The springs are replenished with water. Multiply and replenish the earth. Genesis = 1:28.
replete: Completely filled; full. His words replete with guile.
repletion: The state of being completely filled; or superabundant fullness.
In medicine, fullness of blood; plethorn.
repleviable: In law, that may be replevied.
replevied: Taken by a writ of replevin.
replevin: An action or remedy granted on a distress, by which a person whose cattle or goods are distrained, has them returned to his own possession upon giving security to try the right of taking in a suit at law, and if that should be determined against him, to return the cattle or goods into the possession of the distrainor.
The writ by which a distress is replevied.
replevisable: That may be replevied; but little used, being superseded by repleviable.
replevy: To take back, by a writ for that purpose cattle or goods that have been distrained, upon giving security to try the right of distraining in a suit at law, and if that should be determined against the plaintiff, to return the cattle or goods into the hands of the distrainor. In this case, the person whose goods are distrained becomes the plaintiff, and the person distraining the defendant or avowant.
replication: In law pleadings, the reply of the plaintiff to the defendant’s plea.
replier: One who answers; he that speaks or writes in return to something spoken or written.
reply: To answer; to make a return in words or writing to something said or written by another. O man, who art thou that repliest against God? Romans 9:20.
In law, to answer a defendant’s plea. The defendant pleads in bar to the plaintiffs declaration; the plaintiff replies to the defendant's plea in bar.
To return for an answer. He knows not what to reply.
An answer; that which is said or written in answer to what is said or written by another.
A book or pamphlet written in answer to another.
replying: Answering either in words or writing.
report: To make a statement of facts. The committee will report at twelve o'clock.

To bear or bring back an answer, or to relate what has been discovered by a person sent to examine, explore or investigate; as, a messenger reports to his employer what he has seen or ascertained. The committee reported the whole number of votes.
To give an account of; to relate; to tell. They reported his good deeds before me. Nehemiah 6:19; Acts 4:23.

To tell or relate from one to another; to circulate publicly, as a story; as in the common phrase, it is reported. It is reported among the heathen, and Gashmu saith it, that thou and the Jews think to rebel. Nehemiah 6:6. In this form of expression, it refers to the subsequent clause of the sentence; “that thou and the Jews think to rebel, is reported.” .
To give an official account or statement; as, the secretary of the treasury reports to congress annually the amount of revenue and expenditure.
reported: Told, related or stated in answer to inquiry or direction; circulated in popular rumors; reputed; stated officially.
reporter: One that gives an account, verbal or written, official or unofficial.
An officer or person who makes statements of law proceedings and decisions, or of legislative debates.
reporting: Giving account; relating; presenting statements of facts or of adjudged cases in law.
reposal: s as z. The act of reposing or resting.
repose: To lay at rest. - After the toil of battle, to repose your wearied virtue.
To lay; to rest, as the mind, in confidence or trust; as, to repose trust or confidence in a person’s veracity.
To lay up; to deposit; to lodge; as pebbles reposed in cliffs.
reposed: Laid at rest; placed in confidence.
reposing: Laying at rest; placing in confidence; lying at rest; sleeping.
reposit: To lay up; to lodge, as for safety or preservation. Others reposit their young in holes.
reposited: Laid up; deposited for safety or preservation.
repositing: Laying up or lodging for safety or preservation.
reposition: The act of replacing; as the reposition of a bone.
repository: A place where things are or may be deposited for safety or preservation. A granary is a repository for corn, an arsenal for arms. The mind or memory is called the repository of ideas.
repossess: To possess again. Nor shall my father repossess the land. To repossess one’s self, to obtain possession again.
repossession: The act of possessing again; the state of possessing again.
reprehend: To chide; to reprove. Pardon me for reprehending thee.
To blame; to censure. I nor advise, nor reprehend the choice.
To detect of fallacy . This color will be reprehended or encountered, by imputing to all excellencies in compositions a kind of poverty.
To accuse; to charge with a fault; with of; as Aristippus, being reprehended of luxury.
reprehender: One that reprehends; one that blames or reproves.
reprehensible: Blamable; culpable; censurable; deserving reproof; applied to persons or things; as a reprehensible person; reprehensible conduct.
reprehensibly: Culpably; in a manner to deserve censure or reproof.
reprehension: Reproof; censure; open blame. Faults not punishable, may deserve reprehension.
represent: To show or exhibit by resemblance. Before him burn seven lamps, as in a zodiac, representing the heavenly fires.
To describe; to exhibit to the mind in words. The managers of the bank at Genoa have been represented as a second kind of senate.
To exhibit; to show by action; as a tragedy well represented.
To personate; to act the character or to fill the place of another in a play; as, to represent the character of king Richard.
representation: The act of representing, describing or showing.
That which exhibits by resemblance; image, likeness, picture or statue; as representations of God.

Any exhibition of the form or operations of a thing by something resembling it. A map is a representation of the world or a part of it. The terrestrial globe is a representation of the earth. An orrery is a representation of the planets and their revolutions.
Exhibition, as of a play’ on the stage.
Exhibition of a character in theatrical performance.
representative: Exhibiting a similitude. They own the legal sacrifices, though representative, to be proper and real.
Bearing the character or power of another; as a council representative of the people.
One that exhibits the likeness of another. A statue of Rumor, whispering an idiot in the ear, who was the representative of credulity .

In legislative or other business, an agent, deputy or substitute who supplies the place of another or others, being invested with his or their authority. An attorney is the representative of his client or employer. A member of the house of commons is the representative of his constituents and of the nation. In matters concerning his constituents only, he is supposed to be bound by their instructions, but in the enacting of laws for the nation, he is supposed not to be bound by their instructions, as he acts for the whole nation.
In law, one that stands in the place of another as heir, or in the right of succeeding to an estate of inheritance, or to a crown.
representatively: In the character of another; by a representative.
By — substitution; by delegation of power.
representativeness: The state or quality of being representative. Dr. Burnet observes that every thought is attended with consciousness and representativeness.
represented: Shown; exhibited; personated; described; stated; having substitutes.
representer: One who shows, exhibits or describes.::
A representative; one that acts by deputation.
representing: Showing; exhibiting; describing; acting in another’s character; acting in the place of another.
representment: Representation; image; an idea proposed as exhibiting the likeness of something.
repress: To crush; to quell; to put down; to subdue; to suppress; as, to repress sedition or rebellion; to repress the first risings of discontent.
To check; to restrain. Such kings favor the innocent, repress the bold.
repression: The act of subduing; as the repression of tumults.
repressive: Having power to crush; tending to subdue or restrain.
reprieve: To respit after sentence of death; to suspend or delay the execution of for a time; as, to reprieve a criminal for thirty days. He reprieves the sinner from time to time.
To grant a respit to; to relieve for a time for any suffering. Company, though it may reprieve a man from his melancholy, yet cannot secure a man from his conscience.
The temporary suspension of the execution of sentence of death on acriminal.
Respit; interval of ease or relief. All that I ask is but a short reprieve, till I forgot to love, and learn to grieve.
reprieved: Respited; allowed a longer time to live than the sentence of death permits.
reprieving: Respiting; suspending the execution of for a time.
reprimand: To reprove severely; to reprehend; to chide for a fault. Germanicus was severely reprimanded by Tiberius, for traveling into Egypt without his permission.
To reprove publicly and officially, in execution of a sentence. The court ordered the officer to be reprimanded.
Severe reproof for a fault; reprehension, private or public.
reprint: To print again; to print a second or any new edition.
To renew the impression of any thing. The business of redemption is - to reprint God’s image on the soul.
reprisal: The seizure or taking of any thing from an enemy by way of retaliation or indemnification for something taken or detained by him.

That which is taken from an enemy to indemnify an owner for something of his which the enemy has seized. Reprisals may consist of persons or of goods. Letters of marque and reprisal may be obtained in order to seize the bodies or goods of the subjects of an offending state, until satisfaction shall be made.

Recaption; a retaking of a man’s own goods or any of his family, wife, child or servant, wrongfully taken from him or detained by another. In this case, the owner may retake the goods or persons wherever he finds them. Letters of marque and reprisal, a commission granted by the supreme authority of a state to a subject, empowering him to pass the frontiers that is, enter an enemy’s territories and capture the goods and persons of the enemy, in return for goods or persons taken by him.
The act of retorting on an enemy by inflicting suffering or death on a prisoner taken from him, in retaliation of an act of inhumanity.
reprise: s as z. A taking by way of retaliation. Obs.
reprizes: In law, yearly deductions out of a manor, as rent-charge, rent-seek
reproach: To censure in terms of opprobrium or contempt. Mezentius with his ardor warm’d his fainting friends, reproach’d their shameful flight, repell’d the victors.
To charge with a fault in severe language. That shame there sit not, and reproach us as unclean.
To upbraid; to suggest blame for any thing. A man’s conscience will reproach him for a criminal, mean or unworthy action.
To treat with scorn or contempt. Luke 6:22.
Censure mingled with contempt or derision; contumelious or opprobrious language towards any person; abusive reflections; as foul-mouthed reproach.
reproached: Censured in terms of contempt; upbraided.
reproachful: Expressing censure with contempt; scurrilous; opprobrious; as reproachful words.
Shameful; bringing or casting reproach; infamous; base; vile; as reproachful conduct; a reproachful life. WW~V~AD .
reproachfully: In terms of reproach; opprobriously; scurrilously 7 Timothy 5:14.
reprobate: Not enduring proof or trial; not of standard purity or fineness; disallowed; rejected. Reprobate silver shall men call them, because the Lord hath rejected them. Jeremiah 6:30.
Abandoned in sin; lost to virtue or grace. They profess that they know God, but in works deny him, being abominable and disobedient, and to every good work reprobate. Titus 1:16.
Abandoned to error, or in apostasy. 2 Timothy 3:8.
A person abandoned to sin; one lost to virtue and religion. I acknowledge myself a reprobate, a villain, a traitor to the king.

To disapprove with detestation or marks of extreme dislike; to disallow; to reject. It expresses more than disapprove or disallow. We disapprove of slight faults and improprieties; we reprobate what is mean or criminal.
reprobated: Disapproved with abhorrence; rejected; abandoned to wickedness or to destruction.
reprobating: Disapproving with extreme dislike; rejecting; abandoning to wickedness or to destruction.
reprobation: The act of disallowing with detestation, or of expressing extreme dislike.
The act of abandoning or state of being abandoned to eternal destruction. When a sinner is so hardened as to feel no remorse or misgiving of conscience, it is considered as a sign of reprobation.
A condemnatory sentence; rejection. Set a brand of reprobation on clipped poetry and false coin.
reprobationer: One who abandons others to eternal destruction.
reproduce: To produce again; to renew the production of a thing destroyed. Trees are reproduced by new shoots from the roots or stump; and certain animals, as the polype, are reproduced from cuttings.
reproduction: The act or process of reproducing that which has been destroyed; as the reproduction of plants or animals from cuttings or slips. The reproduction of several parts of lobsters and crabs is one of the greatest curiosities in natural history.
reproof: Blame expressed to the face; censure for a fault; reprehension. Those best can bear reproof, who merit praise. He that hateth reproof is brutish. Proverbs 12:1.
Blame cast; censure directed to a person.
reprovable: Worthy of reproof; deserving censure; blamable.
reprove: To blame; to censure. I will not reprove thee for thy sacrifices - Psalm 50:8.
To charge with a fault to the face; to chide; to reprehend. Luke 3:19.
To blame for; with of; as, to reprove one of laziness.
To convince of a fault, or to make it manifest. John 16:8.
reproved: Blamed; reprehended; convinced of a fault.
reprover: One that reproves; he or that which blames. Conscience is a bold reprover.
reptile: Creeping; moving on the belly, or with many small feet.
Groveling; low; vulgar; as a reptile race or crew; reptile vices.

An animal that moves on its belly, or by means of small short legs, as earth-worms, caterpillars, snakes and the like. In zoology, the reptiles constitute an order of the class Amphibian, including all such as are furnished with limbs or articulated extremities, as tortoises, lizards and frogs.
A groveling or very mean person; a term of contempt.
republic: A commonwealth; a state in which the exercise of the sovereign power is lodged in representatives elected by the people. In modern usage, it differs from a democracy or democratic state, in which the people exercise the powers of sovereignty in person. Yet the democracies of Greece are often called republics.
Common interest; the public. Republic of letters, the collective body of learned men.
republican: Pertaining to a republic; consisting of a commonwealth; as a republican constitution or government.
Consonant to the principles of a republic; as republican sentiments or opinions; republican manners.
One who favors or prefers a republican form of government.
republicanism: A republican form or system of government.
Attachment to a republican form of government.
republicanize: To convert to republican principles; as, to republicanize the rising generation.
republication: A second publication, or a new publication of something before published.

A second publication, as of a former will. If there be many testaments, the last overthrows all the former; but the republication of a former will, revokes one of a later date, and establishes the first.
republish: To publish a second time, or to publish a new edition of a work before published.
To publish anew. Unless, subsequent to the purchase or contract, the devisor republishes his will.
repudiable: That may be rejected; fit or proper to be put away.
repudiate: To cast away; to reject; to discard. Atheists - repudiate all title to the kingdom of heaven.
Appropriately, to put away; to divorce; as a wife.
repudiation: Divorce; as the repudiation of a wife.
repugnance: Opposition or struggle of passions; resistance.
Opposition of principles or qualities; inconsistency; contrariety. But where difference is without repugnancy, that which hath been can be no prejudice to that which is.
repugnancy: Opposition or struggle of passions; resistance.
Opposition of principles or qualities; inconsistency; contrariety. But where difference is without repugnancy, that which hath been can be no prejudice to that which is.
repugnant: Opposite; contrary; inconsistent; properly followed by to. Every sin is repugnant to the will of God. Every thing morally wrong, is repugnant both to the honor, as well as to the interest of the offender.
repulse: A being checked in advancing, or driven back by force. The enemy met with a repulse and retreated.
repuls’. To repel; to beat or drive back as, to repulse an assailant or advancing enemy.
repulser: One that repulses or drives back.
repulsion: In physics, the power of repelling or driving off; that property of bodies which causes them to recede from each other or avoid coming in contact.
repulsive: Repelling; driving off, or keeping from approach. The repulsive power of the electric fluid is remarkable.
Cold; reserved; forbidding; as repulsive manners.
repulsiveness: The quality of being repulsive or forbidding.
repurchase: To buy again; to buy back; to regain by purchase or expense.
The act of buying again; the purchase again of what has been sold.
repurchased: Bought back or again; regained by expense; as a throne repurchased with the blood of enemies.
repurchasing: Buying back or again; regaining by the payment of a price.
reputable: Being in good repute; held in esteem; as a reputable man or character; reputable conduct. It expresses less than respectable and honorable, denoting the good opinion of men, without distinction or great qualities.

Consistent with reputation; not mean or disgraceful. It is evidence of extreme depravity that vice is in any case reputable. In the article of danger, it is as reputable to elude an enemy as to defeat one.
reputably: With reputation; without disgrace or discredit; as, to fill an office reputably.
reputation: Good name; the credit, honor or character which is derived from a favorable public opinion or esteem. Reputation is a valuable species of property or right, which should never be violated. With the loss of reputation, a man and especially a woman, loses most of the enjoyments of life. The best evidence of reputation is a man’s whole life.
Character by report; in a good or bad sense; as, a man has the reputation of being rich or poor, or of being a thief.
repute: To think; to account; to hold; to reckon. The king was reputed a prince most prudent. Wherefore are we counted as beasts, and reputed vile in your sight. Job 18:3.
Reputation; good character; the credit or honor derived from common or public opinion; as men of repute.
Character; in a bad sense; as a man held in bad repute.
Established opinion; as upheld by old repute.
request: The expression of desire to some person for something to be granted or done; an asking; a petition. Haman stood up to make request for his life to Esther the queen. Esther 7:7.
Prayer; the expression of desire to a superior or to the Almighty. Philippians 4:6.
The thing asked for or requested. I will both hear and grant you your requests. He gave them their request; but sent leanness into their soul. PsaIm 106:15.

A state of being desired or held in such estimation as to be sought after or pursued. Knowledge and fame were in as great request as wealth among us now. In request, in demand; in credit or reputation. Coriolanus being now in no request. Request expresses less earnestness than entreaty and supplication, and supposes a right in the person requested to deny or refuse to grant. In this it differs from demand.
To ask; to solicit; to express desire for. The weight of the golden ear-rings which he requested, was a thousand and seven hundred shekels of gold. Judges 8:26.
requicken: To reanimate; to give new life to.
requiem: In the Romish church, a hymn or mass sung for the dead, for the rest of his soul; so called from the first word.
requirable: That may be required; fit or proper to be demanded.
require: To demand; to ask, as of right and by authority. We require a person to do a thing, and we require a thing to be done. Why then doth my lord require this thing? 7 Chronicles 21:3.
To claim; to render necessary; as a duty or any thing indispensable; as, the law of God requires strict obedience.
To ask as a favor; to request. I was ashamed to require of the king a band of soldiers and horsemen to help us against the enemy in the way. Ezra 8:22.
To call to account for. I will require my flock at their hand. Ezekiel 34:10.
To make necessary; to need; to demand. The king’s business required haste.7 Samuel 21:8.
requirement: Demand; requisition. This ruler was one of those who believe that they can fill us every requirement contained in the rule of righteousness. The Bristol water is of service where the secretions exceed the requirements of health.
requisite: s as z. Required by the nature of things or by circumstances; necessary; so needful that it cannot be dispensed with. Repentance and faith are requisite to salvation. Air is requisite to support life. Heat is requisite to vegetation.

That which is necessary; something indispensable. Contentment is a requisite to a happy life. God on his part has declared the requisites on ours; what we must do to obtain blessings, is the great business of us all to know.
requisiteness: The state of being requisite or necessary; necessity.
