requisition: Demand; application made as of right. Under the old confederation of the American states, congress often made requisitions on the states for money to supply the treasury; but they had no power to enforce their requisitions, and the states neglected or partially complied with them.
requital: Return for any office, good or bad; in a good sense, compensation; recompense; as the requital of services; in a bad sense, retaliation or punishment, as the requital of evil deeds.
Return; reciprocal action. No merit their aversion can remove, nor ill requital can efface their love.
requite: To repay either good or evil; in a good sense, to recompense; to return an equivalent in good; to reward. I also will requite you this kindness. 2 Samuel 2:6; 1 Timothy 5:4. In a bad sense, to retaliate; to return evil for evil; to punish. Joseph will certainly requite us all the evil which we did to him. Genesis 50:15.
To do or give in return. He hath requited me evil for good.7 Samuel 25:21.
re-resolve: re-rezolv’. To resolve a second time.
rere-ward: The part of an army that marches in the rear, as the guard; the rear guard. Numbers 10:25; Isaiah 52:12.
rescind: To abrogate; to revoke; to annul; to vacate an act by the enacting authority or by superior authority; as, to rescind a law, a resolution or a vote; to rescind an edict or decree; to rescind a judgment.
rescission: The act of abrogating, annulling or vacating; as the rescission of a law, decree or judgment.
rescissory: Having power to cut off or to abrogate.
rescript: The answer of an emperor, when consulted by particular persons on some difficult question. This answer serves as a decision of the question, and is therefore equivalent to an edict or decree.
rescue: res’cu. To free or deliver from any confinement, violence, danger or evil; to liberate from actual restraint, or to remove or withdraw from a state of exposure to evil; as, to rescue a prisoner from an officer; to rescue seamen from destruction by shipwreck. So the people rescued Jonathan that he died not. 7 Samuel! 14:45; 1 Samuel 30:18; Psalm 35:17. Cattle taken by distress contrary to law, may be rescued by the owner, while on their way to the pound. Estimate the value of one soul rescued from eternal guilt and agony, and destined to grow forever in the knowledge and likeness of God.
Deliverance from restraint, violence or danger, by force or by the interference of an agent.

In law, rescue or rescous, the forcible retaking of a lawful distress from the distrainor, or from the custody of the law; also, the forcible liberation of a defendant from the custody of the officer, in which cases, the remedy is by writ of rescous. But when the distress is unlawfully taken, the owner may lawfully make rescue. The rescue of a prisoner from the court, is punished with perpetual imprisonment and forfeiture of goods.
rescued: Delivered from confinement or danger; or forcibly taken from the custody of the law.
rescuing: Liberating from restraint or danger; forcibly taking from the custody of the law.
research: reserch’. Diligent inquiry or examination in seeking facts or principles; laborious or continued search after truth; as researches of human wisdom.

To search or examine with continued care; to seek diligently for the truth. It is not easy to research with due distinction, in the actions of eminent personages, both how much may have been blemished by the envy of others, and what was corrupted by their own felicity.
To search again; to examine anew.' .
researcher: reserch’er. One who diligently inquires or examines.
resection: The act of cutting or paring off.
reseek: pret. and pp. resought. To seek again.
reseize: To seize again; to seize a second time.

In law, to take possession of lands and tenements which have been disseized. Whereupon the sheriff is commanded to reseize the land and all the chattels thereon, and keep the same in his custody till the arrival of the justices of assize.
reseizure: resc’zhur. A second seizure; the act of seizing again.
resell: To sell again; to sell what has been bought or sold.
resemblance: Likeness; similitude, either of external form or of qualities. We observe a resemblance between persons, a resemblance in shape, a resemblance in manners, a resemblance in dispositions. Painting and poetry bear a great resemblance to each other, as one object of both is to please.
Something similar; similitude; representation. These sensible things which religion hath allowed, are resemblances formed according to things spiritual. Fairest resemblance of thy Maker fair - .
resemble: To have the likeness of; to bear the similitude of something, either in form, figure or qualities. One man may resemble another in features; he may resemble a third person in temper or deportment. Each one resembled the children of a king. Judges 8:18.

To liken; to compare; to represent as like something else. The torrid parts of Africa are resembled to a libbard’s skin, the distance of whose spots represents the dispersed situation of the habitations.
resembling: Having the likeness of; likening; comparing.
resend: pret. and pp. resent. To send again; to send back.
resent: To take well; to receive with satisfaction. Obs.
To take ill; to consider as an injury or affront; to be in some degree angry or provoked at. Thou with scorn and anger would’st resent the offer’d wrong.
resented: Taken ill; being in some measure angry at.
resenter: One who resents; one that feels an injury deeply.
In the sense of one that takes a thing well. Obs.
resentful: Easily provoked to anger; of an irritable temper.
resentingly: With a sense of wrong or affront; with a degree of anger.
With deep sense or strong perception. Obs.
resentive: Easily provoked or irritated; quick to feel an injury or affront.
resentment: The excitement of passion which proceeds from a sense of wrong offered to ourselves, or to those who are connected with us; anger. This word usually expresses less excitement than anger, though it is often synonymous with it. It expresses much less than wrath, exasperation, and indignation. In this use, resentment is not the sense or perception of injury, but the excitement which is the effect of it. Can heavenly minds such high resentment show?
reservation: The act of reserving or keeping back or in the mind; reserve; concealment or withholding from disclosure; as mental reservation.

Something withheld, either not expressed or disclosed, or not given up or brought forward. With reservation of a hundred knights. In the United States, a tract of land not sold with the rest, is called a reservation.
Custody; state of being treasured up or kept in store.

In law, a clause or part of an instrument by which something is reserved, not conceded or granted; also, a proviso. Mental reservation is the withholding of expression or disclosure of something that affects a proposition or statement, and which if disclosed, would materially vary its import.& Mental reservations are the refuge of hypocrites.
reservatory: A place in which things are reserved or kept.
reserve: To keep in store for future or other use; to withhold from present use for another purpose. The farmer sells his corn, reserving only what is necessary for his family. Hast thou seen the treasures of hail, which I have reserved against the day of trouble? Job 38:23.
To keep; to hold; to retain. Will he reserve his anger for ever? Jeremiah 3:5.
To lay up and keep for a future time. 2 Peter 2:9. Reserve your kind looks and language for private hours.

That which is kept for other or future use; that which is retained from present use or disposal. The virgins, besides the oil in their lamps, carried likewise a reserve in some other vessel for a continual supply.
reserved: Kept for another or future use; retained. 2.a. Restrained from freedom in words or actions; backward in conversation; not free or frank. To all obliging, yet reserv’d to all. Nothing reserv’d or sullen was to see.
reservedly: With reserve; with backwardness; not with openness or frankness.
reservedness: Closeness; want of frankness, openness or freedom. A man may guard himself by that silence and reservedness which every one may innocently practice.
reserving: Keeping back; keeping for other use or for use at a future time; retaining.
reservoir: A place where any thing is kept in store, particularly a place where water is collected and kept for use when wanted, as to supply a fountain, a canal or a city by means of aqueducts, or to drive a mill-wheel and the like; a cistern; a mill-pond; a bason.
reset: In Scots law, the receiving and harboring of an outlaw or a criminal.
resettle: To install, as a minister of the gospel.
to settle in the ministry a second time; to be installed.
resettlement: The act of settling or composing again. The resettlement of my discomposed soul.
The state of settling or subsiding again; as the resettlement of lees.
A second settlement in the ministry.
reship: To ship again; to ship what has been conveyed by water or imported; as coffee and sugar imported into New York, and reshipped for Hamburg.
reshipment: the act of shipping or loading on board of a ship a second time; the shipping for exportation what has been imported.
resiant: Resident; dwelling; present in a place. Obs.
reside: to dwell permanently or for a length of time; to have a settled abode for a time. the peculiar uses of this word are to be noticed. When the word is appliced to the natives of a state, or others who dwell in it as permanent citizens, we use it only with reference to the part of a city or country in which a man dwells. We do not say generally that Englishmen reside in England, but a particular citizen resides in London or York, or at such a house in such a street, in the Strand. When the word is applied to strangers or travelers, we do not say, a man resides in an inn for a night, but he resided in London or Oxford a month, or a year; or part of his life. A man lodges, stays, remains, abides, for a day or very short time, but reside implies a longer time, though not definite.
To sink to the bottom of liquors; to settle. Obs.
residence: The act of abiding or dwelling in a place for some continuance of time; as the residence of an American in France or Italy for a year. The confessor had often made considerable residences in Normandy.
The place of abode; a dwelling; a habitation. Caprea had been - the residence of Tiberius for several years.
That which falls to the bottom of liquors. Obs.
In the canon and common law, the abode of a person or incumbent on his benefice; opposed to non-residence.
resident: Dwelling or having an abode in a place for a continuance of time, but not definite; as a minister resident at the court of St. James. AB is now resident in South America.
One who resides or dwells in a place for some time. A B is now a resident in London.
A public minister who resides at a foreign court. It is usually applied to ministers of a rank inferior to that of embassadors.
residentiary: An ecclesiastic who keeps a certain residence.
resider: One who resides in a particular place.
residing: Dwelling in a place for some continuance of time.
residual: Remaining after a part is taken.
residuary: Pertaining to the residue or part remaining; as the residuary advantage of an estate. Residuary legatee, in law, the legatee to whom is bequeathed the part of goods and estate which remains after deducting all the debts and specific legacies.
residue: That which remains after a part is taken, separated, removed or designated. The locusts shall eat the residue of that which has escaped. Exodus 10:5. The residue of them will I deliver to the sword. Jeremiah 15:9.
The balance or remainder of a debt or account.
residuum: Residue; that which is left after any process of separation or purification.
In law, the part of an estate or of goods and chattels remaining after the payment of debts and legacies.
resiege: To seat again; to reinstate. Obs.
resign: To give up; to give back, as an office or commission, to the person or authority that conferred it; hence, to surrender an office or charge in a formal manner; as, a military officer resigns his commission; a prince resigns his crown. Phoebus resigns his darts, and Jove his thunder, to the god of love.
To withdraw, as a claim. He resigns all pretensions to skill.
To yield; as, to resign the judgment to the direction of others.
To yield or give up in confidence. What more reasonable, than that we should in all things resign ourselves to the will of God?
resignation: The act of resigning or giving up, as a claim or possession; as the resignation of a crown or commission.
Submission; unresisting acquiescence; as a blind resignation to the authority of other men’s opinions.
Quiet submission to the will of Providence; submission without discontent, and with entire acquiescence in the divine dispensations. This is christian resignation.
resigned: a. Submissive to the will of God.
resilience: s as z. The act of leaping or springing back, or the act of rebounding; as the resilience of a ball or of sound.
resiliency: s as z. The act of leaping or springing back, or the act of rebounding; as the resilience of a ball or of sound.
resilition: The act of springing back; resilience.
resin: s as z. An inflammable substance, hard when cool, but viscid when heated, exuding in a fluid state from certain kinds of trees, as pine, either spontaneously or by incision. Resins are soluble in oils and alcohol, and are said to be nothing but oils concreted by combination with oxygen. Resins differ from gums, which are vegetable mucilage; and they are less sweet and odorous than balsams.
resiniferous: Yielding resin; as a resiniferous tree or vessels.
resino-electric: Containing or exhibiting negative electricity, or that kind which is produced by the friction of resinous substances.
resino-extractive: Designating extractive matter in which resin predominates.
resinous: Partaking of the qualities of resin; like resin. Resinous substances are combustible. Resinous electricity, is that electricity which is excited by rubbing bodies of the resinous kind. This is generally negative.
resinously: By means of resin; as resinously electrified.
resipiscence: Properly, wisdom derived from severe experience; hence.
resist: Literally, to stand against; to withstand; hence, to act in opposition, or to oppose. a dam or mound resists a current of water passively, by standing unmoved and interrupting its progress. An army resists the progress of an enemy actively, by encountering and defeating it. We resist measures by argument or remonstrance. Why doth he yet find fault? for who hath resisted his will? Romans 9:19.
To strive against; to endeavor to counteract, defeat or frustrate. Ye do always resist the Holy Spirit. Acts 7:57.
To baffle; to disappoint. God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace to the humble. James 4:6.
resistance: The act of resisting; opposition. Resistance is passive, as that of a fixed body which interrupts the passage of a moving body; or active, as in the exertion of force to stop, repel or defeat progress or designs.

The quality of not yielding to force or external impression; that power of a body which acts in opposition to the impulse or pressure of another, or which prevents the effect of another power; as the resistance of a ball which receives the force of another; the resistance of wood to a cutting instrument; the resistance of air to the motion of a cannon ball, or of water to the motion of a ship.
resistibility: The quality of resisting. The name body, being the complex idea of extension and resistibility together in the same subject - .
Quality of being resistible; as the resistibility of grace.
resistible: That may be resisted; as a resistible force; resistible grace.
resisting: withstanding; opposing. Resisting medium, a substance which opposes the passage of a body through it.
resistless: That cannot be effectually opposed or withstood; irresistible. Resistless in her love as in her hate.
resistlessly: So as not to be opposed or denied.
resold: of resell. Sold a second time, or sold after being bought.
resoluble: s as z. That may be melted or dissolved; as bodies resoluble by fire.
resolute: Having a fixed purpose; determined; hence, bold; firm; steady; constant in pursuing a purpose. Edward is at hand, ready to fight; therefore be resolute.
resolutely: With fixed purpose; firmly; steadily; with steady perseverance. Persist resolutely in a course of virtue. WWV”AD .
Boldly; firmly: . Some of these facts he examines, some be resolutely denies.
resoluteness: Fixed purpose; firm determination; unshaken firmness.
resolution: The act, operation or process of separating the parts which compose a complex idea or a mixed body; the act of reducing any compound or combination to its component parts; analysis; as the resolution of complex ideas; the resolution of any material substance by chimical operations.
The act or process of unraveling or disentangling perplexities, or of dissipating obscurity in moral subjects; as the resolution of difficult questions in moral science.
Dissolution; the natural process of separating the component parts of bodies.
In music, the resolution of a dissonance, is the carrying of it, according to rule, into a consonance in the subsequent chord.
In medicine, the disappearing of any tumor without coming to suppuration; the dispersing of inflammation.t
resolutioner: One who joins in the declaration of others.
resolutive: Having the power to dissolve or relax.
resolvable: That may be resolved or reduced to first principles.
resolve: To separate the component parts of a compound substance; to reduce to first principles; as, to resolve a body into its component or constituent parts; to resolve a body into its elements.
To separate the parts of a complex idea; to reduce to simple parts; to analyze.

To separate the parts of a complicated question; to unravel; to disentangle of perplexities; to remove obscurity by analysis; to clear of difficulties; to explain; as, to resolve questions in moral science; to resolve doubts; to resolve a riddle.
To inform to free from doubt or perplexity; as, to resolve the conscience. Resolve me, stranger, whence and what you are?\ .
To settle in an opinion; to make certain. Long since we were resolv’d of your truth, your faithful service and your toil in war.
resolved: Separated into its component parts; analyzed.

Determined in purpose; as, I am resolved not to keep company with gamesters. This phrase is properly, “I have resolved;” as we say, a person is deceased, or has deceased; he is retired, or has retired. In these phrases, the participle is rather an adjective.
resolvent: That which has the power of causing solution. In medicine, that which has power to disperse inflammation and prevent the suppuration of tumors; a discutient.
resolver: One that resolves or forms a firm purpose.
resolving: Separating into component parts; analyzing; removing perplexities or obscurity; discussing, as tumors; determining.
The act of determining or forming a fixed purpose; a resolution.
resonance: A resounding; a sound returned from the sides of a hollow instrument of music; reverberated sound or sounds.
resort: To have recourse; to apply; to betake. The king thought it time to resort to other counsels.
To go; to repair. The people resort to him again. Mark 10:1; John 18:20.
To fall back. The inheritance of the son never resorted to the mother. Obs.
resound: To send back sound; to echo. And Albion’s cliffs resound the rural lay.
To sound; to praise or celebrate with the voice or the sound of instruments.
To praise; to extol with sounds; to spread the fame of. The man for wisdom’s various arts renown’d, long exercis’d in woes, O muse.
To be echoed; to be sent back, as sound; as, common fame resounds back to them.
To be much and loudly mentioned.
resource: Any source of aid or support; an expedient to which a person may resort for assistance, safety or supply; means yet untried; resort. An enterprising man finds resources in his own mind. Pallas view’d his foes pursuing and his friends pursu’d, used threat’nings mix’d with prayers, his last resource.

Resources, in the plural, pecuniary means; funds; money or any property that can be converted into supplies; means of raising money or supplies. Our national resources for carrying on war are abundant. Commerce and manufactures’ furnish ample resources.
resow: pret. resowed; pp. resowed or resown. To sow again.
respeak: To answer; to speak in return; to reply.
respect: To regard; to have regard to in design or purpose. In orchards and gardens, we do not so much respect beauty, as variety of ground for fruits, trees and herbs.
To have regard to, in relation or connection; to relate to. The treaty particularly respects our commerce.
To view or consider with some degree of reverence; to esteem as possessed of real worth. I always loved and respected Sir William.

To look towards. Palladius adviseth the front of his house should so respect the south. To respect the person, to suffer the opinion or judgment to be influenced or biased by a regard to the outward circumstances of a person, to the prejudice of right and equity. Thou shalt not respect the person of the poor. Leviticus 19:15. Neither doth God respect any person. 2 Samuel 14:14.
respectability: State or quality of being respectable; the state or qualities which deserve or command respect.
respectable: Possessing the worth or qualities which deserve or command respect; worth of esteem and honor; as a respectable citizen; respectable company. No government, any more than an individual, will long be respected, without being truly respectable.

In popular language, this word is much used to express what is moderate in degree of excellence on in number, but not despicable. We say, a respectable discourse or performance, a respectable audience, a respectable number of citizens convened.
respectably: With respect; more generally, in a manner to merit respect.
Moderately, but in a manner not to be despised.
respecter: One that respects; chiefly used in the phrase, respecter of persons, which signifies a person who regards the external circumstances of others in his judgment, and suffers his opinion to be biased by them, to the prejudice of candor, justice and equity. I perceive that God is no respecter of persons. Acis 10:34.
respectful: Marked or characterized by respect; as respectful deportment. With humble joy and with respectful fear.
respectfully: With respect; in a manner comporting with due estimation.
respecting: Regarding; having regard to; relating to. This word, like concerning, has reference to a single word or to a sentence. In the sentence, “his conduct respecting us is commendable,” respecting has reference to conduct. But when we say, “respecting a further appropriation of money, it is to be observed, that the resources of the country are inadequate,” respecting has reference to the whole subsequent clause or sentence.
respective: Relative; having relation to something else; not absolute; as the respective connections of society.

Particular; relating to a particular person or thing. Let each man retire to his respective place of abode. The officers were found in their respective quarters; they appeared at the head of their respective regiments. Let each give according to his respective proportion.
Careful; circumspect; cautious; attentive to consequences; as respective and wary men.
respectively: As relating to each; particularly; as each belongs to each. Let each man respectively perform his duty. The impressions from the objects of the senses do mingle respectively every one with its kind.
Partially; with respect to private views. Obs.
respectless: Having no respect; without regard; without reference.
respectlessness: The state of having no respect or regard; regardlessness.
respirable: That may be breathed; fit for respiration or for the support of animal life; as respirable air. Azotic gas is not respirable.
respiration: The act of breathing; the act of inhaling air into the lungs and again exhaling or expelling it, by which animal life is supported. The respiration of fishes, appears to be performed by the air contained in the water acting on the gills.
respiratory: Serving for respiration; as respiratory organs.
respire: To breathe; to inhale air into the lungs and exhale it, for the purpose of maintaining animal life.
To rest; to take rest from toil.
To exhale; to breathe out; to send out in exhalations.
respit: Pause; temporary intermission of labor, or of any process or operation; interval of rest. Some pause and respit only I require.
In law, reprieve; temporary suspension of the execution of a capital offender.
Delay; forbearance; prolongation of time for the payment of a debt beyond the legal time.
The delay of appearance at court granted to a jury, beyond the proper term.
To relieve by a pause or interval of rest. To respit his day-labor with repast.
respited: Relieved from labor; allowed a temporary suspension of execution.
respiting: Relieving from labor; suspending the execution of a capital offender.
resplendence: Brilliant luster; vivid brightness; splendor. Son! thou in whom my glory I behold in full resplendence, heir of all my might.
resplendency: Brilliant luster; vivid brightness; splendor. Son! thou in whom my glory I behold in full resplendence, heir of all my might.
resplendent: Very bright; shining with brilliant luster. With royal arras and resplendent gold.
resplendently: With brilliant luster; with great brightness.
respond: To answer; to reply. A new affliction strings a new chord in the heart, which responds to some new note of complaint within the wide scale of human woe.
To correspond; to suit. To every theme responds thy various lay.
To be answerable; to be liable to make payment; as, the defendant is held to respond in damages.
To answer; to satisfy by payment. The surety was held to respond the judgment of court. The goods attached shall be held to respond the judgment.
A short anthem interrupting the middle of a chapter, which is not to proceed till the anthem is ended.
respondent: Answering; that answers to demand or expectation. - Wealth respondent to payment and contributions.
One that answers in a suit, particularly a chancery suit.
In the schools, one who maintains a thesis in reply, and whose province is to refute objections or overthrow arguments.
response: An answer or reply; particularly, an oracular answer.
The answer of the people or congregation to the priest, in the litany and other parts of divine service.
Reply to an objection in a formal disputation.
In the Romish church, a kind of anthem sung after the morning lesson.
responsibility: The state of being accountable or answerable, as for a trust or office, or for a debt. It is used in the plural; as heavy responsibilities.
Ability to answer in payment; means of paying contracts.
responsible: Liable to account; accountable; answerable; as for a trust reposed, or for a debt. We are all responsible for the talents entrusted to us by our Creator. A guardian is responsible for the faithful discharge of his duty to his ward. The surety is responsible for the debt of his principal.
Able to discharge an obligation; or having estate adequate to the payment of a debt. In taking bail, the officer will ascertain whether the proposed surety is a responsible man.
responsibleness: State of being liable to answer, repay or account; responsibility.
Ability to make payment of an obligation or demand.
responsive: Correspondent; suited to something else. The vocal lay responsive to the strings.
responsory: A response; the alternate speaking, in church service.
rest: Cessation of motion or action of any kind, and applicable to any body or being; as rest from labor; rest from mental exertion; rest of body or mind. A body is at rest, when it ceases to move; the mind is at rest, when it ceases to be disturbed or agitated; the sea is never at rest. Hence,
Quiet; repose; a state free from motion or disturbance; a state of reconciliation to God. Learn of me, for I am meek and lowly in heart; and ye shall find rest to your souls. Matthew 11:29.
Peace; national quiet. The land had rest eighty years. Judges 3:30; Deuteronomy 12:10.
restagnant: Stagnant; remaining without a flow or current.
restagnate: To stand or remain without flowing.
restant: In botany, remaining, as footstalks after the fructification has fallen off.
restauration: Restoration to a former good state.
restem: To force back against the current.
restfully: In a state of rest or quiet.
rest-harrow: A plant of the genus Ononis.
restif: Unwilling to go, or only running back; obstinate in refusing to move forward; stubborn; as a restif steed. It seems originally to have been used of horses that would not be driven forward. It is sometimes written restive. All who before him did ascend the throne, labor’d to draw three restive nations on.
Being at rest, or less in action.
restifness: Obstinate reluctance or indisposition to move.
restinction: The act of quenching or extinguishing.
resting: Ceasing to move or act; ceasing to be moved or agitated; lying; leaning; standing; depending or relying.
restitute: To restore to a former state.
restitution: The act of returning or restoring to a person some thing or right of which he has been unjustly deprived; as the restitution of ancient rights to the crown. Restitution is made by restoring a specific thing taken away or lost.
The act of making good, or of giving an equivalent for any loss, damage or injury; indemnification. He restitution to the value makes.
The act of recovering a former state or posture. Restitution of all things, the putting the world in a holy and happy state. Acts 3:21.
restless: Unquiet; uneasy; continually moving; as a restless child.
Being without sleep; uneasy. Restless he pass’d the remnant of the night.
Passed in unquietness; as, the patient has had a restless night.
Uneasy; unquiet; not satisfied to be at rest or in peace; as a restless prince; restless ambition; restless passions.
restlessly: Without rest; unquietly. When the mind casts and turns itself restlessly from one thing to another.
restlessness: Uneasiness; unquietness; a state of disturbance or agitation, either of body or mind.
Want of sleep or rest; uneasiness.
Motion; agitation; as the restlessness of the magnetic needle.
restorable: That may be restored to a former good condition; as restorable land.
restoration: The act of replacing in a former state. Behold the different climes agree, rejoicing in thy restoration. So we speak of the restoration of a man to his office, or to a good standing in society.
Renewal; revival; re-establishment; as the restoration of friendship between enemies; the restoration of peace after war; the restoration of a declining commerce.
Recovery; renewal of health and soundness; as restoration from sickness or from insanity.
Recovery from a lapse or any bad state; as the restoration of man from apostasy.
In theology, universal restoration, the final recovery of all men from sin and alienation from God, to a state of happiness; universal salvation.
restorative: That has power to renew strength and vigor.
A medicine efficacious in restoring strength and vigor, or in recruiting the vital powers.
restore: To return to a person, as a specific thing which he has lost, or which has been taken from him and unjustly detained. We restore lost or stolen goods to the owner. Now therefore restore to the man his wife. Genesis 20:7.
To replace; to return; as a person or thing to a former place. Pharaoh shall restore thee to thy place. Genesis 40:13.
To bring back. The father banish’d virtue shall restore.

To bring back or recover from lapse, degeneracy, declension or ruin to its former state. - Loss of Eden, till one greater man restore it, and regain the blissful seat. - Our fortune restored after the severest afflictions.
To heal; to cure; to recover from disease. His hand was restored whole like as the other. Matthew 12:13.
restored: Returned; brought back; retrieved; recovered; cured; renewed; re- established.
restorer: One that restores; one that returns what is lost or unjustly detained; one who repairs or re-establishes.
restoring: Returning what is lost or taken; bringing back; recovering; curing; renewing; repairing; re-establishing.
restrain: To hold back; to check; to hold from action, proceeding or advancing, either by physical or moral force, or by an interposing obstacle. Thus we restrain a horse by a bridle; we restrain cattle from wandering by fences; we restrain water by dams and dikes; we restrain men from crimes and trespasses by laws; we restrain young people, when we can, by arguments or counsel; we restrain men and their passions; we restrain the elements; we attempt to restrain vice, but not always with success.
To repress; to keep in awe; as, to restrain offenders.
To suppress; to hinder or repress; as, to restrain excess.
To abridge; to hinder from unlimited enjoyment; as, to restrain one of his pleasure or of his liberty: .
To limit; to confine. Not only a metaphysical or natural, but a moral universality is also to be restrained by a part of the predicate.
restrained: Held back from advancing or wandering; withheld; repressed; suppressed; abridged; confined.
restraining: Holding back from proceeding; checking; repressing; hindering from motion or action; suppressing. 2 .a. Abridging; limiting; as a restraining statute.
restraint: The act or operation of holding back or hindering from motion, in any manner; hinderance of the will, or of any action, physical, moral or mental.
Abridgment of liberty; as the restraint of a man by imprisonment or by duress.
Prohibition. The commands of God should be effectual restraints upon our evil passions.
Limitation; restriction. If all were granted, yet it must be maintained, within any bold restraints, far otherwise than it is received.
That which restrains, hinders or represses. The laws are restraints upon injustice.
restrict: To limit; to confine; to restrain within bounds; as, to restrict words to a particular meaning; to restrict a patient to a certain diet.
restriction: Limitation; confinement within bounds. This is to have the same restriction as all other recreations. Restriction of words, is the limitation of their signification in a particular manner or degree.
restrictive: Having the quality of limiting or of expressing limitation; as a restrictive particle.
Imposing restraint; as restrictive laws of trade.
restringe: restrinj. To confine; to contract; to astringe.
restringency: The quality or power of contracting.
restringent: A medicine that operates as an astringent or styptic.
resty: The same as restive or restif, of which it is a contraction.
resublime: To sublime again; as, to resublime mercurial sublimate.
result: to leap back; to rebound. The huge round stone, resulting with a bound - .

To preceed, spring or rise, aS a consequence, from facts, arguments, premises, conbination of circumstances, consultation or meditation. Evidence results from testimony, or from a variety of concurring circumstances; pleasure results from friendship; harmony results from certain accordances of sounds. Pleasure and peace naturally result from a holy and good life.
To come to a conclusion or determination. the council resulted in recommending harmony and peace to the parties.
Resilience; act of flying back. Sound is produced between the string and the air, by the return of the result of the string.
resultant: In mechanics, a force which is the combined effect of two or more forces, acting in different directions.
resulting: Proceeding as a consequence, effect or conclusion of something; coming to a determination.
In law, resulting use, is a use which returns to him who raised it, after its expiration or during the impossibility of vesting in the person intended.
resumable: s as z. That may be taken back, or that may be taken up again.
resume: To take back what has been given. The sun, like this from which our sight we have, gaz’d on too long, resumes the light he gave.
To take back what has been taken away. They resume what has been obtained fraudulently.
To take again after absence; as, to resume a seat. Reason resum’d her place, and passion fled.
To take up again after interruption; to begin again; as, to resume an argument or discourse.
resumed: Taken back; taken again; begun again after interruption.
resuming: Taking back; taking again; beginning again after interruption.
resumption: The act of resuming, taking back or taking again; as the resumption of a grant.
resupinate: In botany, reversed; turned upside down. A resupinate corol is when the upper lip faces the ground, and the lower lip the sky. A resupinate leaf is when the upper surface becomes the lower, and contrary; or when the lower disk looks upward.
resupination: The state of lying on the back; the state of being resupinate or reversed, as a corol.
resurrection: s as z. A rising again; chiefly, the revival of the dead of the human race, or their return from the grave, particularly at the general judgment. By the resurrection of Christ we have assurance of the future resurrection of men. 7 Peter 1:3. In the resurrection, they neither marry, nor are given in marriage. Matthew 22:30.
resurvey: To survey again or anew; to review.
resuscitate: To revivify; to revive; particularly, to recover from apparent death; as, to resuscitate a drowned person; to resuscitate withered plants.
To reproduce, as a mixed body from its ashes.
resuscitation: The act of reviving from a state of apparent death; the state of being revivified.
The reproducing of a mixed body from its ashes.
resuscitative: Reviving; revivifying; raising from apparent death; reproducing.
retail: To sell in small quantities or parcels, from the sense of cutting or dividing; opposed to selling by wholesale; as, to retail cloth or groceries.
To tell in broken parts; to tell to many; as, to retail slander or idle reports.
The sale of commodities in small quantities or parcels, or at second hand.
retailer: One who sells goods by small quantities or parcels.
retain: To hold or keep in possession; not to lose or part with or dismiss. The memory retains ideas which facts or arguments have suggested to the mind. They did not like to retain God in their knowledge. Romans 1:28.
To keep, as an associate; to keep from departure. Whom I would have retained with me.Philemon 13.
To keep back; to hold. An executor may retain a debt due to him from the testator.
To hold from escape. Some substances retain heat much longer than others. Metals readily receive and transmit heat, but do not long retain it. Seek cloths that retain their color.
retained: Held; kept in possession; kept as an associate; kept in pay; kept from escape.
retainer: One who retains; as an executor, who retains a debt due from the testator.
One who is kept in service; an attendant; as the retainers of the ancient princes and nobility.
An adherent; a dependant; a hanger on.
A servant, not a domestic, but occasionally attending and wearing his master’s livery.
Among lawyers, a fee paid to engage a lawyer or counselor to maintain a cause.
retaining: Keeping in possession; keeping as an associate; keeping from escape; hiring; engaging by a fee.
retake: To take from a captor; to recapture; as, to retake a ship or prisoners.
retaker: One who takes again what has been taken; a recaptor.
retaking: Taking again; taking from a captor.
retaliate: To return like for like; to repay or requite by an act of the same kind as has been received. It is now seldom used except in a bad sense, that is, to return evil for evil; as, to retaliate injuries. In war, enemies often retaliate the death or inhuman treatment of prisoners, the burning of towns or the plunder of goods. It is unlucky to be obliged to retaliate the injuries of authors, whose works are so soon forgotten that we are in danger of appearing the first aggressors.
To return like for like; as, to retaliate upon an enemy.
retaliation: The return of like for like; the doing that to another which he has done to us; requital of evil.
In a good sense, return of good for good.S God takes what is done to others as done to himself, and by promise obliges himself to full retaliation.
retaliatory: Returning like for like; as retaliatory measure; retaliatory edicts.
retard: To diminish the velocity of motion; to hinder; to render more slow in progress; as, to retard the march of an army; to retard the motion of a ship. The resistance of air retards the velocity of a cannon ball. It is opposed to accelerate.
To delay; to put off; to render more late; as, to retard the attacks of old age; to retard a rupture between nations. My visit was retarded by business.
retardation: The act of abating the velocity of motion; hinderance; the act of delaying; as the retardation of the motion of a ship; the retardation of hoary hairs.
retarder: One that retards, hinders or delays.
retarding: Abating the velocity of motion; hindering; delaying.
retardment: The act of retarding or delaying.
retch: To make an effort to vomit; to heave; as the stomach; to strain, as in vomiting; properly to reach.
retection: The act of disclosing or producing to view something concealed; as the retection of the native color of the body.
retention: The act of retaining or keeping.
The power of retaining; the faculty of the mind by which it retains ideas.

In medicine, the power of retaining, or that state of contraction in the solid or vascular parts of the body, by which they hold their proper contents and prevent in voluntary evacuations; undue retention of some natural discharge.
retentive: Having the power to retain; as a retentive memory; the retentive faculty; the retentive force of the stomach; a body retentive of heat or moisture.
retentiveness: The quality of retention; a retentiveness of memory.
reticence: Concealment by silence. In rhetoric, aposiopesis or suppression; a figure by which a person really speaks of a thing, while he makes a show as if he would say nothing on the subject.
reticency: Concealment by silence. In rhetoric, aposiopesis or suppression; a figure by which a person really speaks of a thing, while he makes a show as if he would say nothing on the subject.
reticle: A contrivance to measure the quantity of an eclipse; a kind of micrometer.
reticular: Having the form of a net or of net-work; formed with interstices; as a reticular body or membrane.

Having the form of a net or of net- work; formed with interstices; as a reticular body or membrane. In anatomy, the reticular body, or rete mucosum, is the layer of the skin, intermediate between the cutis and the cuticle, the principal seat of color in man; the reticular membrane is the same as the cellular membrane.
reticulate: Netted; resembling net-work; having distinct veins crossing like net-work; as a reticulate corol or petal.
reticulated: Netted; resembling net-work; having distinct veins crossing like net-work; as a reticulate corol or petal.
reticulation: Net-work; organization of substances resembling a net.
retiform: Having the form of a net in texture; composed of crossing lines and interstices; as the retiform coat of the eye.
retina: In anatomy, one of the coats of the eye, being an expansion of the optic nerve over the bottom of the eye, where the sense of vision is first received.
retinasphalt: A bituminous or resinous substance of a yellowish or reddish brown color, found in irregular pieces very light and shining.
retinite: Pitchstone; stone of fusible pitch, of a resinous appearance, compact, brown, reddish, gray, yellowish, blackish or bluish, rarely homogeneous, and often containing crystals of feldspar and scales of mica. It is the pechstein porphyry or obsidian of the Germans. It is called also retinasphalt.
retinue: The attendants of a prince or distinguished personage, chiefly on a journey or an excursion; a train of persons.
retirade: In fortification, a kind of retrenchment in the body of a bastion or other work, which is to be disputed inch by inch, after the defenses are dismantled. It usually consists of two faces, which make a re- entering angle.
retire: To withdraw; to retreat; to go from company or from a public place into privacy; as, to retire from the world; to retire from notice.
To retreat from action or danger; as, to retire from battle.
To withdraw from a public station. General Washington, in 1796, retired to private life.
To break up, as a company or assembly. The company retired at eleven o'clock.

To depart or withdraw for safety or for pleasure. Men retire from the town in summer for health and pleasure. But in South Carolina, the planters retire from their estates to Charleston, or to an isle near the town.
retired: Secluded from much society or from public notice; private. He lives a retired life; he has a retired situation.
retiredness: A state of retirement; solitude; privacy or secrecy.
retirement: The act of withdrawing from company or from public notice or station.
The state of being withdrawn; as the retirement of the mind from the senses.
Private abode; habitation secluded from much society or from public life. Caprea had been the retirement of Augustus. Retirement is as necessary to me as it will be welcome.
Private way of life. Retirement, rural quiet, friendship, books, progressive virtue and approving heaven.
retiring: Withdrawing; retreating; going into seclusion or solitude. 2.a. Reserved; not forward or obtrusive; as retiring modesty; retiring manners.
retold: and pp. of retell; as a story retold.
retort: To throw back; to reverberate. And they retort that beat again to the first giver.
To return an argument, accusation, censure or incivility; as, to retort the charge of vanity. He pass’d through hostile scorn; and with retorted scorn, his back he turn’d.
To bend or curve back; as a retorted line.
To return an argument or charge; to make a severe reply. He retorted upon his adversary with severity.
The return of an argument, charge or incivility in reply; as the retort courteous.
retouch: retuch’. To improve by new touches; as, to retouch a picture or an essay.
retouched: retuch’ed. Touched again; improved by new touches.
retrace: To trace back; to go back in the same path or course; as, to retrace one’s. steps; to retrace one’s’ proceedings.
To trace back, as a line. Then if the line of Tumus you retrace, he springs from Inachus of Argive race.
retract: To recall, as a declaration, words or saying; to disavow; to recant; as, to retract an accusation, charge or assertion. I would as freely have retracted the charge of idolatry, as I ever made it.
To take back; to unsay; to withdraw concession or declaration. She will, and she will not; she grants, denies, consents, retracts, advances, and then flies.
Among horsemen, the prick of a horse’s foot in nailing a shoe.
retractable: That may be retracted or recalled.
retractation: The recalling of what has been said; recantation; change of opinion declared.
retractible: That may be drawn back; retractile.
retractile: Capable of being drawn back. A walrus with fiery eyes - retractile from external injuries.
retraction: The act of withdrawing something advanced, or changing something done.
Recantation; disavowal of the truth of what has been said; declaration of change of opinion.
Act of withdrawing a claim. Other men’s insatiable desire of revenge, hath beguiled church and state of the benefit of my retractions or concessions.
retractive: That which withdraws or takes from.
retrait: A cast of countenance; a picture. Obs.
retraxit: In law, the withdrawing or open renunciation of a suit in court, by which the plaintiff loses his action.
retreat: The act of retiring; a withdrawing of one’s self from any place. But beauty’s triumph is well tim’d retreat.
Retirement; state of privacy or seclusion from noise, bustle or company. Here in the calm still mirror of retreat.
Place of retirement or privacy. He built his son a house of pleasure - and spared no cost to make it a delicious retreat.
Place of safety or security. That pleasing shade they sought, a soft retreat from sudden April show's, a shelter from the heat.

In military affairs, the retiring of an army or body of men from the face of an enemy or from any ground occupied to a greater distance from the enemy, or from an advanced position. A retreat is properly an orderly march, in which circumstance it differs from a flight.
retreated: as a passive participle, though used by Milton, is not good English.
retrench: To cut off; to pare away. And thy exuberant parts retrench.
To lessen; to abridge; to curtail; as, to retrench superfluities or expenses.
To live at less expense. It is more reputable to retrench than to live embarrassed.
retrenchment: The act of lopping off; the act of removing what is superfluous; as the retrenchment of words or lines in a writing.
The act of curtailing, lessening or abridging; diminution; as the retrenchment of expenses.

In military affairs, any work raised to cover a post and fortify it against an enemy; such as fascines, gabions, sandbags and the like. Numerous remains of Roman retrenchments, constructed to cover the country - .
retribute: To pay back; to make payment, compensation or reward in return; as, to retribute one for his kindness; to retribute to a criminal what is proportionate to his offense.
retributed: Paid back; given in return; rewarded.
retribution: Repayment; return accommodated to the action; reward; compensation. In good offices and due retributions, we may not be pinching and niggardly.
A gratuity or present given for services in the place of a salary.

The distribution of rewards and punishments at the general judgment. It is a strong argument for a state of retribution hereafter, that in this world virtuous persons are very often unfortunate, and vicious persons prosperous.
retributive: Repaying; rewarding for good deeds, and punishing for offenses; as retributive justice.
retributory: Repaying; rewarding for good deeds, and punishing for offenses; as retributive justice.
retrievable: That may be retrieved or recovered.
retrieve: To recover; to restore from loss or injury to a former good state; as to retrieve the credit of a nation; to retrieve one’s character; to retrieve a decayed fortune.
To repair. Accept my sorrow, and retrieve my fall.
To regain. With late repentance now they would retrieve the bodies they forsook, and wish to live.
To recall; to bring back; as, to retrieve men from their cold trivial conceits.
retroaction: Operation on something past or preceding.
retroactive: Operating by returned’ action; affecting what is past; retrospective. A retroactive law or statute, is one which operates to affect, make criminal or punishable, acts done prior to the passing of the law.
retroactively: By returned action or operation; by operating on something past.
retrocede: To cede or grant back; as, to retrocede a territory to a former proprietor.
retrocession: A ceding or granting back to a former proprietor.
retroduction: A leading or bringing back. RETROFLEx
retrofract: Reduced to hang down as it were by force so as to appear as if broken; as a retrofract peduncle. Bent back towards its insertion, as if it were broken.
retrofracted: Reduced to hang down as it were by force so as to appear as if broken; as a retrofract peduncle. Bent back towards its insertion, as if it were broken.
retrogradation: The act of moving backwards; applied to the apparent motion of the planets.
A moving backwards; decline in excellence.
retrograde: In astronomy, apparently moving backward and contrary to the succession of the signs, as a planet.
Declining from a better to a worse state.«
retrogressive: Going or moving backward; declining from a more perfect to a less perfect state. Geography is at times retrogressive.
retromingency: The act of quality of discharging the contents of the bladder backwards.
retromingent: In zoology, an animal that discharges its urine backwards. The retromingents are a division of animals whose characteristic is that they discharge their urine backwards, both male and female.
retrorsely: retros’ly. In a backward direction; as a stem retrorsely aculeate.
retrospect: A looking back on things past; view or contemplation of something past. The retrospect of a life well spent affords peace of mind in old age.
retrospection: The act of looking back on things’ past.
The faculty of looking back on past things.
retrospective: Looking back on past events; as a retrospective view.
Having reference to what is past; affecting things past. A penal statute can have no retrospective effect or operation.
retroversion: A turning or falling backwards; as the retroversion of the uterus.
retund: To blunt; to turn; as an edge; to dull; as, to retund the edge of a weapon.
return: To come or go back to the same place. The gentleman goes from the country to London and returns, or the citizen of London rides into the country and returns. The blood propelled from the heart, passes through the arteries to the extremities of the body, and returns through the veins. Some servants are good to go on errands, but not good to return.
To come to the same state; as, to return from bondage to a state of freedom.
To answer. He said, and thus the queen of heaven return’d.
To come again; to revisit. Thou to mankind be good and friendly still, and oft return.
To appear or begin again after a periodical revolution. With the year seasons return, but not to me returns day - .
returnable: That may be returned or restored.

In law, that is legally to be returned, delivered, given or rendered; as a writ or precept returnable at a certain day; a verdict returnable to the court; an attachment returnable to the king’s bench.
return-day: The day when the defendant is to appear in court and the sheriff is to return the writ and his proceedings.
returned: Restored; given or sent back; repaid; brought or rendered to the proper court or officer.
returner: One who returns; one that repays or remits money.
returning: Giving, carrying or sending back; coming or going back; making report.
returning-officer: The officer whose duty it is to make returns of writs, precepts, juries
retuse: In botany, a retuse leaf is one ending in a blunt sinus, or whose apex is blunt. This term is applied also to the seed.
reunion: A second union; union formed anew after separation or discord; as a reunion of parts or particles of matter; a reunion of parties or sects.
In medicine, union of parts separated by wounds or accidents.
reunite: To unite again; to join after separation.
In medicine, union of parts separated by wounds or accidents.
To be united again; to join and cohere again.
reussite: A salt found in the form of a mealy efforescence, or crystallized in flat six sided prisms, and in acicular crystals.
reve: The bailiff of a franchise or manor. It is usually written reve.
reveal: To disclose; to discover; to show; to make known something before unknown or concealed; as, to reveal secrets.

To disclose, discover or make known from heaven. God has been pleased to reveal his will to man. The wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men. Romans 1:18.
revealed: Disclosed; discovered; made known; laid open.
revealer: One that discloses or makes known.
reveille: In military affairs, the beat of drum about break of day, to give notice that it is time for the soldiers to rise and for the sentinels to forbear challenging.
revelly: In military affairs, the beat of drum about break of day, to give notice that it is time for the soldiers to rise and for the sentinels to forbear challenging.
revel: To feast with loose and clamorous merriment; to carouse; to act the bacchanalian. Antony, that revels long o’nights.
To move playfully or without regularity.
A feast with loose and noisy jollity. Some men ruin the fabric of their bodies by incessant revels.
To draw back; to retract; to make a revulsion.
revelation: The act of disclosing or discovering to others what was before unknown to them; appropriately, the disclosure or communication of truth to men by God himself, or by his authorized agents, the prophets and apostles. How that by revelation he made known to me the mystery, as I wrote before in few words. Ephesians 3:3; 2 Corinthians 12:1.
That which is revealed; appropriately, the sacred truths which God has communicated to man for his instruction and direction. The revelations of God are contained in the Old and New Testament.
The Apocalypse; the last book of the sacred canon, containing the prophecies of St. John.
reveler: One who feasts with noisy merriment.
reveling: A feasting with noisy merriment; revelry. Galatians 5:21; 1 Peter 4:3.
revel-rout: A mob; a rabble tumultuously assembled; an unlawful assembly.
revendicate: To reclaim what has been taken away; to claim to have restored what has been seized. Should some subsequent fortunate revolution deliver it from the conqueror’s yoke, it can revendicate them.
revendication: The act of reclaiming or demanding the restoration of any thing taken by an enemy; as by right of postliminium. The endless disputes which would spring from the revendication of them, have introduced a contrary practice.
revenge: To inflict pain or injury in return for an injury received.

According to modern usage, to inflict pain deliberately and maliciously, contrary to the laws of justice and humanity, in return for injury, pain or evil received; to wreak vengeance spitefully on one who injures or offends. We say, to revenge an injury or insult, or with the reciprocal pronoun, to revenge ourselves on an enemy or for an injury, that is, to take vengeance or satisfaction.
To vindicate by punishment of an enemy. The gods are just and will revenge our cause.
Return of an injury; the deliberate infliction of pain or injury or a person in return for an injury received from him.
revenged: Punished in return for an injury; spitefully punished. The injury is revenged.
revengeful: full of revenge or a desire to inflict pain or evil for injury received; spiteful; malicious; wreaking revenge. If thy revengeful heart cannot forgive.
Vindictive; inflicting punishment. May my hands never brandish more revengeful steel.
revengefully: revenj’fully. By way of revenge; vindictively; with the spirit of revenge.
revengement: revenj’ment. Revenge; return of an injury.
revenger: One who revenges; one who inflicts pain on another spitefully in return for an injury.
One who inflicts just punishment for injuries.
revenging: Inflicting pain or evil spitefully for injury or affront received.
revengingly: With revenge; with the spirit of revenge; vindictively.
revenue: In a general sense, the annual rents, profits, interest or issues of any species of property, real or personal, belonging to an individual or to the public. When used of individuals, it is equivalent to income. In modern usage, income is applied more generally to the rents and profits of individuals, and revenue to those of the state. In the latter case, revenue is .
The annual produce of taxes, excise, customs, duties, rentswhich a nation or state collects and receives into the treasury for public use.
Return; reward; as a rich revenue of praise.
A fleshy lump on the head of a deer.
reverberate: To return, as sound; to send back; to echo; as, an arch reverberates the voice.
To send or beat back; to repel; to reflect; as, to reverberate rays of light .
To send or drive back; to repel from side to side; as flame reverberated in a furnace.
To be driven back; to be repelled, as rays of light, or sound.
To resound. And even at hand, a drum is ready brac’d, that shall reverberate all as well as thine.
reverberated: Driven back; sent back; driven from side to side.
reverberating: Driving or sending back; reflecting, as light; echoing, as sound.
reverberation: The act of driving or sending back; particularly, the act of reflecting light and heat or repelling sound. Thus we speak of the reverberation of the rays of light from an object, the reverberation of sound in echoes, or the reverberation of heat or flame in a furnace.
reverberatory: Returning or driving back; as a reverberatory furnace or kiln.
A furnace with a kind of dome that reflects the flame upon a vessel placed within it, so as to surround it.
revere: To regard with fear mingled with respect and affection; to venerate; to reverence; to honor in estimation. Marcus Aurelius, whom he rather revered as his father, than treated as his partner in the empire - .
revered: Regarded with fear mingled with respect and affection.
reverence: Fear mingled with respect and esteem; veneration. When quarrels and factions are carried openly, it is a sign that the reverence of government is lost. The fear acceptable to God, is a filial fear, an awful reverence of the divine nature, proceeding from a just esteem of his perfections, which produces in us an inclination to his service and an unwillingness to offend him. Reverence is nearly equivalent to veneration, but expresses something less of the same emotion. It differs from awe, which is an emotion compounded of fear, dread or terror, with admiration of something great, but not necessarily implying love or affection. We feel reverence for a parent, and for an upright magistrate, but we stand in awe of a tyrant. This distinction may not always be observed.
An act of respect or obeisance; a bow or courtesy. 2 Samuel 9:6.
A poetical title of a father.

To regard with reverence; to regard with fear mingled with respect and affection. We reverence superiors for their age, their authority and their virtues. We ought to reverence parents and upright judges and magistrates. We ought to reverence the Supreme Being, his word and his ordinances. Those that I reverence, those I fear, the wise. They — will reverence my son. Matthew 21:37. Let the wife see that she reverence her husband. Ephesians 5:33.
reverenced: Regarded with fear mingled with respect and affection.
reverencing: Regarding with fear mixed with respect and affection.
reverend: Worthy of reverence; entitled to respect mingled with fear and affection; as reverend and gracious senators. A reverend sire among them came.

A title of respect given to the clergy or ecclesiastics. We style a clergyman reverend; a bishop is styled right reverend; an archbishop most reverend. The religious in catholic countries, are styled reverend fathers; abbesses, prioressesreverend mothers. In Scotland, as in the United States, the clergy are individually styled reverend. A synod is styled very reverend, and the general assembly venerable.
reverent: Expressing reverence, veneration or submission; as reverent words or terms; a reverent posture in prayer; reverent behavior.
Submissive; humble; impressed with reverence. They prostrate fell before him reverent.
reverential: Proceeding from reverence, or expressing it; as reverential fear or awe; reverential gratitude or esteem. Religion - consisting in a reverential esteem of things sacred.
reverentially: With reverence, or show of reverence.
reverently: With reverence; with respectful regard. Chide him for faults, and do it reverently.
With veneration; with fear of what is great or terrifying. So reverently men quit the open air, when thunder speaks the angry Gods abroad.
revering: Regarding with fear mixed with respect and affection; venerating.
reversal: A change or overthrowing; as the reversal of a judgment, which amounts to an official declaration that it is false. So we speak of the reversal of an attainder or of an outlawry, by which the sentence is rendered void.
reverse: To turn upside down; as, to reverse a pyramid or cone.
To overturn; to subvert; as, to reverse the state.
To turn back; as with swift wheel reverse.
To turn to the contrary; as, to reverse the scene. - Or affectations quite reverse the soul.
To put each in the place of the other; as, to reverse the distinctions of good and evil.
reversed: Turned side for side or end for end; changed to the contrary.
In law, overthrown or annulled. 3.a. In botany, resupinate; having the upper lip larger and more expanded than the lower; as a reversed corol.
reverseless: revers’less. Not to be reversed; irreversible.
reversely: revers’ly. On the other hand; on the opposite.
reversible: That may be reversed; as a reversible judgment or sentence.
reversing: Turning upside down; subverting; turning the contrary way; annulling.
reversion: In general sense, a returning; appropriately, in law, the returning of an estate to the grantor or his heirs, after a particular estate is ended. Hence,

The residue of an estate left in the grantor, to commence in possession after the determination of the particular estate granted. Thus when there is a gift in tail, the reversion of the fee is, without any special reservation, vested in the donor by act of law.
Succession; right to future possession or enjoyment.
In algebra, reversion of series, a kind of reversed operation of an infinite series.
reversionary: Pertaining to a reversion, that is, to be enjoyed in succession, or after the determination of a particular estate; as a reversionary interest or right.
reversioner: The person who has a reversion, or who is entitled to lands or tenements, after a particular estate granted is determined.
revert: To turn back; to turn to the contrary; to reverse. Till happy chance revert the cruel scene.
To drive or turn back; to reverberate; as a stream reverted.

In law, to return to the proprietor, after the determination of a particular estate. A feud granted to a man for life, or to him and his issue male, or his death or failure of issue male, reverted to the lord or proprietor.
revertent: A medicine which restores the natural order of the inverted irritative motions in the animal system.
revery: Properly, a raving or delirium; but its sense, as generally used, is a loose or irregular train of thoughts, occurring in musing or meditation; wild, extravagant conceit of the fancy or imagination. There are reveries and extravagancies which pass through the minds of wise men as well as fools.
revest: To reinvest; to vest again with possession or office; as, to revest a magistrate with authority.
to lay out in something less fleeting than money; as, to revest money in stocks.
to take effect again, as a title; to return to a former owner; as, the title or right revest in A, after alienation.
revestiary: The place or apartment in a church or temple where the dresses are deposited; now contracted into vestry.
revetment: In fortification, a strong wall on the outside of a rampart, intended to support the earth.
revibrate: to vibrate back or in return.
revictual: revit’l. to furnish again with provisions.
revie: to accede to the proposal of a stake and to overtop it; an old phrase at cards. Obs.
To return the challenge of a wager at cards; to make a retort.
review: To see again. I shall review Sicilia.
To view and examine again; to reconsider; to revise; as, to review a manuscript. It is said that Virgil was prevented by death from reviewing the AEneis.
To retrace. Shall I the long laborious scene review?
To survey; to inspect; to examine the state of any thing, particularly of troops; as, to review a regiment.
reviewer: One that reviews or re-examines; an inspector; one that critically examines a new publication, and communicates his opinion upon its merits.
reviewing: Looking back on; seeing again; revising; re-examining; inspecting, as an army; critically examining and remarking on.
revile: To reproach; to treat with opprobrious and contemptuous language. She revileth him to his face. Thou shalt not revile the gods. Exodus 22:28. Blessed are ye when men shall revile you. Matthew 5:117.
reviled: Reproached; treated with opprobrious or contemptuous language.
reviler: One who reviles another; one who treats another with contemptuous language.
reviling: Reproaching; treating with language of contempt.
The act of reviling or treating with reproachful words. Isaiah 51:7.
revilingly: With reproachful or contemptuous language; with opprobrium.
revindicate: To vindicate again; to reclaim; to demand and take back what has been lost.
revisal: Revision; the act of reviewing and re-examining for correction and improvement; as the revisal of a manuscript; the revisal of a proof sheet.
revise: To review; to re-examine; to look over with care for correction; as, to revise a writing; to revise a proof sheet.
To review, alter and amend; as, to revise statutes.
Among printers, a second proof sheet; a proof sheet taken after the first correction.
reviser: One that revises or re-examines for correction.
revision: The act of reviewing; review; re-examination for correction; as the revision of a book or writing or of a proof sheet; a revision of statutes.
revisit: s as z. To visit again Let the pale sire revisit Thebes.
revisor: In Russia, one who has taken the number of inhabitants.
revival: Return, recall or recovery to life from death or apparent death; as the revival of a drowned person.
Return or recall to activity from a state of languor; as the revival of spirits.
Recall, return or recovery from a state of neglect, oblivion, obscurity or depression; as the revival of letters or learning.
Renewed and more active attention to religion; an awakening of men to their spiritual concerns.
revive: To return to life; to recover life. The soul of the child came into him again, and he revived. 7 Kings 17:22; Romans 14:9.
To recover new life or vigor; to be reanimated after depression. When he saw the wagons which Joseph had sent to carry him, the spirit of Jacob their father revived. Genesis 45:27.
To recover from a state of neglect, oblivion, obscurity or depression. Learning revived in Europe after the middle ages.
In chimistry, to recover its natural state, as a metal. Sin revives, when the conscience is awakened by a conviction of guilt. Romans 7:9.
To bring again to life; to reanimate.
revived: Brought to life; reanimated; renewed; recovered; quickened; cheered; reduced to a metallic state.
reviver: That which revives; that which invigorates or refreshes; one that redeems from neglect or depression.
revivificate: To revive; to recall or restore to life.
revivification: Renewal of life; restoration of life; or the act of recalling to life.
In chimistry, the reduction of a metal to its metallic state.
revivify: To recall to life; to reanimate.
To give new life or vigor to.
reviving: Bringing to life again; reanimating; renewing; recalling to the memory; recovering from neglect or depression; refreshing with joy or hope; reducing to a metallic state.
reviviscence: Renewal of life; return to life.
reviviscency: Renewal of life; return to life.
reviviscent: Reviving; regaining or restoring life or action.
revivor: In law, the reviving of a suit which is abated by the death of any of the parties. This is done by a bill of revivor.
revocable: That may be recalled or revoked; that may be repealed or annulled; as a revocable edict or grant.
revocation: The act of recalling or calling back; as the revocation of Calvin.
Repeal; reversal; as the revocation of the edict of Nantz. A law may cease to operate without an express revocation. so we speak of the revocation of a will, of a use, of a devise.
revoke: To recall; to repeal; to reverse. A law, decree or sentence is revoked by the same authority which enacted or passed it. A charter or grant which vests rights in a corporation, cannot be legally revoked without the consent of the corporation. A devise may be revoked by the devisor, a use by the grantor, and a will be the testator.
To check; to repress; as, to revoke rage.
To draw back. Seas are troubled when they do revoke their flowing waves into themselves again.
The act of renouncing at cards.
revolt: To fall off or turn from one to another.

To renounce allegiance and subjection to one’s prince or state; to reject the authority of a sovereign; as a province or a number of people. It is not applied to individuals. The Edomites revolted from under the hand of Judah. 2 Chronicles 21:8, 10.
In Scripture, to disclaim allegiance and subjection to God; to reject the government of the King of kings. Isaiah 317:6.
To turn; to put to flight; to overturn.
revolted: Having swerved from allegiance or duty.
revolter: One who changes sides; a deserter.
One who renounces allegiance and subjection to his prince or state.
In Scripture, one who renounces the authority and laws of God. Jeremiah 6:28; Hosea 9:15.
revolting: Disclaiming allegiance and subjection to a prince or state.
a. Doing violence, as to the feelings; exciting abhorrence.
revolute: In botany, rolled back or downwards; as revolute foliation or leafing, when the sides of the leaves in the bud are rolled spirally back or towards the lower surface; a revolute leaf or tendril; a revolute corol or valve.
revolution: In physics, rotation; the circular motion of a body on its axis; a course or motion which brings every point of the surface or periphery of a body back to the place at which it began to move; as the revolution of a wheel; the diurnal revolution of the earth.
The motion of a body round any fixed point or center; as the annual revolution of the earth or other planet in its orbit round the center of the system.
Motion of any thing which brings it to the same point or state; as the revolution of day and night or of the seasons.
Continued course marked by the regular return of years; as the revolution of ages.
Space measured by some regular return of a revolving body or of a state of things; as the revolution of a day.
revolutionary: Pertaining to a revolution in government; as a revolutionary war; revolutionary crimes or disasters.
Tending to produce aé revolution; as revolutionary measures.
revolutioner: One who is engaged in effecting a revolution; a revolutionist.
In England, one who favored the revolution in 1688.
revolutionist: One engaged in effecting a change of government; the favorer of a revolution.
revolutionize: To effect a change in the form of a political constitution; as, to revolutionize a government.
To effect an entire change of principles in. The gospel, if received in truth, has revolutionized his soul.
revolutionized: Charged in constitutional form and principles.
revolutionizing: Changing the form and principles of a constitution.
revolvency: State, act or principle of revolving; revolution. Its own revolvency upholds the world.
revomit: To vomit or pour forth again; to reject from the stomach.
revulsion: In medicine, the act of turning or diverting a flux of humors or any cause of disease, from one part of the body to another.
The act of holding or drawing back.
revulsive: That which has the power of diverting humors from one part to another.
That which has the power of withdrawing.
reward: a as aw. To give in return, either good or evil. Thou hast rewarded me good, whereas I have rewarded thee evil. Samuel 24:17. Hence, when good is returned for good, reward signifies to repay, to recompense, to compensate. When evil or suffering is return for injury or wickedness, reward signifies to punish with just retribution, to take vengeance on, according to the nature of the case. I will render vengeance to my enemies; and will reward them that hate me. Deuteronomy 32:41. The Son of man shall come in the glory of his Father, with his angels, and then he shall reward every man according to his works. Matthew 16:27. In the latter passage, reward signifies to render with good and evil.

Recompense, or equivalent return for good done, for kindness, for services and the like. Rewards may consist of money, goods or any return of kindness or happiness. The laborer is worthy of his reward.7 Timothy 5:18. Great is your reward in heaven. Matthew 5:12. Rewards and punishments presuppose moral agency, and something voluntarily done, well or ill; without which respect, though we may receive good, it is only a benefit and not a reward.
The fruit of men’s labor or works. The dead know not any thing, neither have they any more a reward. Ecclesiastes 9:5.
A bribe; a gift to pervert justice. Deuteronomy 27:25.
A sum of money offered for taking or detecting a criminal, or for recovery of any thing lost.
rewardable: That may be rewarded; worthy of recompense.
rewardableness: The state of being worthy of reward.
rewarder: One who rewards; one that requites or recompenses. Hebrews 11:6.
rewarding: Making an equivalent return for good or evil; requiting; recompensing or punishing.
reword: To repeat in the same words.
reys: The master of an Egyptian bark or ship.
rhabdology: The act or art of computing or numbering by Napier’s rods or Napier’s bones.
rhabdomancy: Divination by a rod or wand.
rhapsodic: Pertaining to or consisting of rhapsody; unconnected.
rhapsodical: Pertaining to or consisting of rhapsody; unconnected.
rhapsodist: One that writes or speaks without regular dependence of one part of his discourse on another.
One who recites or signs rhapsodies for a livelihood; or one who makes and repeats verses extempore.
Anciently, one whose profession was to recite the verses of Homer and other poets.
rhapsody: Originally, a discourse in verse, sung or rehearsed by a rhapsodist; or a collection of verses, particularly those of Homer. In modern usage, a collection of passages thoughts or authorities, composing a new piece, but without necessary dependence or natural connection.
rhenish: Pertaining to the river Rhine, or to Rheims in France; as Rhemish wine; as a noun, the wine produced on the hills about Rheims, which is remarkable as a solvent of iron.
rhetian: Pertaining to the ancient Rhaeti, or to Rhaetia, their country; as the Rhetian Alps, now the country of Tyrol and the Grisons.
rhetoric: The art of speaking with propriety, elegance and force.
The power of persuasion or attraction; that which allures or charms. We speak of the rhetoric of the tongue, and the rhetoric of the heart or eyes. Sweet silent rhetoric of persuading eyes.
rhetorical: Pertaining to rhetoric; as the rhetorical art.
Containing the rules of rhetoric; as a rhetorical treatise.
rhetorically: In the manner of rhetoric; according to the rules of rhetoric; as, to treat a subject rhetorically; a discourse rhetorically delivered.
rhetorician: One who teaches the art of rhetoric, or the principles and rules of correct and elegant speaking. The ancient sophists and rhetoricians, who had young auditors, lived till they were a hundred years old.
One well versed in the rules and principles of rhetoric.
rhetorize: To represent by a figure of oratory.
rheum: An increased and often inflammatory action of the vessels of an organ; but generally applied to the inflammatory action of the mucous glands, attended with increased discharge and an altered state of their excreted fluids.
A thin serous fluid, secreted by the mucous glands; as in catarrh.
rheumatic: Pertaining to rheumatism, or partaking of its nature, as rheumatic pains or affections.
rheumatism: A painful disease affecting muscles and joints of the human body, chiefly the larger joints, as the hips, knees, shoulders.
rheumy: Full of rheum or watery matter; consisting of rheum or partaking of its nature.
Abounding with sharp moisture; causing rheum.
rhino: A cant word for gold and silver, or money.
rhinocerial: Pertaining to the rhinoceros; resembling the rhinoceros.
rhinoceros: A genus of quadrupeds of two species, one of which, the unicorn, as a single horn growing almost erect from the nose. This animal when full grown, is said to be 12 feet in length. There is another species with two horns, the bicornis. They are natives of Asia and Africa.
rhinoceros-bird: A bird of the genus Buceros, having a crooked horn on the forehead, joined to the upper mandible.
