rhodian: Pertaining to Rhodes, an isle of the Mediterranean; as Rhodian laws.
rhodium: A metal recently discovered among grains of crude platinum.
rhodonite: A mineral of a red, reddish, or yellowish white color, and splintery fracture, occurring compact or fibrous in the Hartz at Strahlberg
rhoetizite: A mineral occurring in masses or in radiated concretions, and of a white color.
rhetizite: A mineral occurring in masses or in radiated concretions, and of a white color.
rhombic: Having the figure of a rhomb.
rhomboid: In geometry, a figure having some resemblance to a rhomb; or a quadrilateral figure whose opposite sides and angles are equal, but which is neither equilateral nor equiangular.
a. In anatomy, the rhomboid muscle is a thin, broad and obliquely square fleshy muscle, between the basis of the scapula and the spina dorsi.
rhomboidal: Having the shape of a rhomboid, or a shape approaching it.
rhomb-spar: A mineral of a grayish white, occurring massive disseminated and crystallized in rhomboids, imbedded in chlorite slate, limestoneIt consists chiefly of carbonates of lime and magnesia.
rhubarb: A plant of the genus Rheum, of several species; as the rhapontic, or common rhubarb; the palmated, or true Chinese rhubarb; the compact or Tartarian; the undulated or wave-leafed Chinese rhubarb; and the rubes, or currant rhubarb of mount Libanus. The root is medicinal and much used as a moderate cathartic.
rhubarbarine: A vegetable substance obtained from rhubarb.
rhumb: In navigation, a vertical circle of any given place, or the intersection of such a circle with the horizon; in which last sense, rhumb is the same as a point of the compass.-LINEIn navigation, a line prolonged from any point of the compass on a nautical chart, except from the four cardinal points.
rhumb-line: In navigation, a line prolonged from any point of the compass on a nautical chart, except from the four cardinal points.
rhyme: In poetry, the correspondence of sounds in the terminating words or syllables of two verses, one of which succeeds the other immediately, or at no great distance. For rhyme with reason may dispense, and sound has right to govern sense. To constitute this correspondence in single words or in syllables, it is necessary that the vowel, and the final articulations or consonants, should be the same, or have nearly the same sound. The initial consonants may be different, as in find and mind, new and drew, cause and laws.
A harmonical succession of sounds. The youth with songs and rhymes, some dance, and some haul the rope.
Poetry; a poem. He knew himself to sing, and build the lofty rhyme.
Aword of sound to answer to another word. Rhyme or reason, number or sense. But from that time unto this season, I had neither rhyme nor reason.
To accord in sound. But fagoted his notions as they fell, and if they rhym’d and rattl’d, all was well.
rime: In poetry, the correspondence of sounds in the terminating words or syllables of two verses, one of which succeeds the other immediately, or at no great distance. For rhyme with reason may dispense, and sound has right to govern sense. To constitute this correspondence in single words or in syllables, it is necessary that the vowel, and the final articulations or consonants, should be the same, or have nearly the same sound. The initial consonants may be different, as in find and mind, new and drew, cause and laws.
A harmonical succession of sounds. The youth with songs and rhymes, some dance, and some haul the rope.
Poetry; a poem. He knew himself to sing, and build the lofty rhyme.
Aword of sound to answer to another word. Rhyme or reason, number or sense. But from that time unto this season, I had neither rhyme nor reason.
White or hoar frost; congealed dew or vapor.
rhymeless: Destitute of rhyme; not having consonance of sound.
rhymer: One who makes rhymes; a versifier; a poor poet.
rhymist: One who makes rhymes; a versifier; a poor poet.
rhymster: One who makes rhymes; a versifier; a poor poet.
rhythm: In music, variety in the movement as to quickness or slowness, or length and shortness of the notes; or rather the proportion which the parts of the motion have to each other.
rhythmus: In music, variety in the movement as to quickness or slowness, or length and shortness of the notes; or rather the proportion which the parts of the motion have to each other.
rhythmical: Having proportion of sound, or one sound proportioned to another; harmonical. Duly regulated by cadences, accents and quantities.
rial: A royal; a gold coin of the value of ten shillings sterling, formerly current in Britain.
rib: A bone of animal bodies which forms a part of the frame of the thorax. The ribs in the human body are twelve on each side, proceeding from the spine to the sternum, or towards it, and serving to inclose and protect the heart and lungs.
In ship building, a piece of timber which forms or strengthens the side of a ship. Ribs of a parrel, are short pieces of plank, having holes through which are reeved the two parts of the parrel-rope.
In botany, the continuation of the petiole along the middle of a leaf, and from which the veins take their rise.
In cloth, a prominent line or rising, like a rib.
Something long, thin and narrow; a strip.
ribald: A low, vulgar, brutal wretch; a lewd fellow.
ribaldry: Mean, vulgar language; chiefly, obscene language.
riban: In heraldry, the eighth part of a bend.
ribbed: Furnished with ribs; as ribbed with steel.
Marked or formed with rising lines and channels; as ribbed cloth.
ribin: A filet of silk; a narrow web of silk used for an ornament, as a badge, or for fastening some part of female dress.

In naval architecture, a long narrow flexible piece of timber, nailed upon the outside of the ribs from the stem to the sternpost, so as to encompass the ship lengthwise; the principal are the floor- ribin and the breadth-ribin.
ribroast: To beat soundly; a burlesque word.
ribwort: A plant of the genus Plantago.
ric: as a termination, denotes jurisdiction, or a district over which government is exercised, as in bishopric. L. rego, to rule, and region.
as a termination of names, denotes rich or powerful, as in Alfric, Fredrick, like the Greek Polycrates and Plutarchus. It is the first syllable of Richard.
rick: as a termination, denotes jurisdiction, or a district over which government is exercised, as in bishopric. L. rego, to rule, and region.

A heap or pile of grain or hay in the field or open air, but sheltered with a kind of roof. In America, we usually give this name to a long pile; the round and conical pile being called stack. In the north of England, it is said this name is given to small piles of corn in the field.
rice: A plant of the genus Oryza, and its seed. The calyx is a bivalvular uniflorous glume; the corol bivalvular, nearly equal, and adhering to the seed. There is only one species. This plant is cultivated in all warm climates, and the grain forms a large portion of the food of the inhabitants. In America, it grows chiefly on low moist land, which can be overflowed. It is a light food, and said to be little apt to produce acidity in the stomach. Indeed it seems intended by the wise and benevolent Creator to be the proper food of men in warm climates.
rice-bird: A bird of the United States, the Emberiza oryzivora; so named from its feeding on rice in the southern states. In New England it is called bob- lincoln.
rice-bunting: A bird of the United States, the Emberiza oryzivora; so named from its feeding on rice in the southern states. In New England it is called bob- lincoln.
rich: Wealthy; opulent; possessing a large portion of land, goods or money, or a larger portion than is common to other men or to men of like rank. A farmer may be rich with property which would not make a nobleman rich. An annual income of 500 sterling pounds would make a rich vicar, but not a rich bishop. Men more willingly acknowledge others to be richer, than to be wiser than themselves. Abram was very rich in cattle, in silver and in gold. Genesis 13:2.
Splendid; costly; valuable; precious; sumptuous; as a rich dress; a rich border; a rich silk; rich furniture; a rich present.
Abundant in materials; yielding great quantities of any thing valuable; as a rich mine; rich ore.
Abounding in valuable ingredients or qualities; as a rich odor or flavor; rich spices. So we say, a rich description; a discourse rich in ideas.
Full of valuable achievements or works. Each minute shall be rich in some great action.
riches: Wealth; opulence; affluence; possessions of land, good or money in abundance. Riches do not consist in having more gold and silver, but in having more in proportion than our neighbors.
Splendid sumptuous appearance. The riches of heav’n’s pavement, trodden gold.

In Scripture, an abundance of spiritual blessings. Luke 16:11. The riches of God, his fullness of wisdom, power, mercy, grace and glory, Ephesians 1:7, 18 Ephesians 2:4, 7; or the abundance supplied by his works. Psalm 104:24. The riches of Christ, his abundant fullness of spiritual and eternal blessings for men. Ephesians 3:8. The riches of a state or kingdom, consist less in a full treasury than in the productiveness of its soil and manufactures, and in the industry of its inhabitants.
richly: With riches; with opulence; with abundance of goods or estate; with ample funds; as a hospital richly endowed. In Belmont is a lady richly left.
Gaily; splendidly; magnificently; as richly dressed; richly ornamented.
Plenteously; abundantly; amply as, to be richly paid for services. The reading of ancient authors will richly reward us for the perusal.
Truly; really; abundantly; fully; as a chastisement richly deserved.
richness: Fertility; fecundity; fruitfulness; the qualities which render productive; as the richness of a oil.
Fullness; abundance; as the richness of a treasury.
Quality of abounding with something valuable; as the richness of a mine or an ore; the richness of milk or of cane-juice.
rickets: A disease which affects children, and in which the joints become knotted, and the legs and spine grow crooked. As the child advances in life, the head is enlarged, the thorax is compressed on the sides, and the sternum rises.
rickety: Weak; feeble in the joints; imperfect.
ricochet: In gunnery, the firing of guns, mortars or howitzers with small charges, and elevated a few degrees, so as to carry the balls or shells just over the parapet, and cause them to roll along the opposite rampart. This is called ricochet-firing, and the batteries are called ricochet-batteries.
rid: To free; to deliver; properly, to separate, and thus to deliver or save. That he might rid him out of their hands. Genesis 37:22. I will rid you out of their bondage. Exodus 6:6.
To separate; to drive away. I will rid evil beasts out of the land. Leviticus 26:6.
To free; to clear; to disencumber; as, to rid one of his care. It is not easy to rid the sea of pirates. Resolv’d at once to rid himself of pain.
To dispatch. For willingness rids away.
riddance: Deliverance; a setting free; as riddance from all adversity.
The act of clearing away. Thou shalt not make clean riddance of the corners of thy field. Leviticus 23:22.
riddle: An instrument for cleaning grain, being a large sieve with a perforated button, which permits the grain to pass through it, but retains the chaff.
t To separate, as grain from the chaff with a riddle; as, to riddle wheat.
An enigma; something proposed for conjecture, or that is to be solved by conjecture; a puzzling question; an ambiguous proposition. Judges 14:12-19.
To solve; to explain; but we generally use unriddle, which is more proper. Riddle me this, and guess him if you can.
riddler: One who speaks ambiguously or obscurely.
riddlingly: In the manner of a riddle; secretly.
ride: pret. rode or rid; pp. rid
To be carried on horseback, or on any beast, or in any vehicle. We ride on a horse, on a camel, in a coach, chariot, wagon.
To be borne on or in a fluid. A ship rides at anchor; the ark rode on the flood; a balloon rides in the air. He rode on a cherub and did fly; yea, he did fly on the wings of the wind. Psalm 18:10.
To be supported in motion. Strong as the axle-tree on which heaven rides.
To practice riding. He rides often for his health.
rider: One who is borne on a horse or other beast, or in a vehicle.
One who breaks or manages a horse.
An inserted leaf or an additional clause, as to a bill in parliament.

In ship building, a short of interior rib fixed occasionally in a ships’s hold, opposite to some of the timbers to which they are bolted, and reaching from the keelson to the beams of the lower deck, to strengthen her frame.
ridge: The back or top of the back.
A long or continued range of hills or mountains; or the upper part of such a range. We say, a long ridge of hills, or the highest ridge.
A steep elevation, eminence or protuberance. Part rise in crystal wall, or ridge direct.
A long rising land, or a strip of ground thrown up by a plow or left between furrows. Psalm 65:10.
The top of the roof of a building.
ridgil: The male of any beast half gelt.
ridgling: The male of any beast half gelt.
ridgy: Having a ridge or ridges; rising in a ridge.
ridicule: Contemptuous laughter; laughter with some degree of contempt; derision. It expresses less than scorn. Ridicule is aimed at what is not only laughable, but improper, absurd or despicable. Sacred subjects should never be treated with ridicule. Ridicule is too rough an entertainment for the polished and refined. It is banished from France, and is losing ground in England .

That species of writing which excites contempt with laughter. It differs from burlesque, which may excite laughter without contempt, or it may provoke derision. Ridicule and derision are not exactly the same, as derision is applied to persons only, and ridicule to persons or things. We deride the man, but ridicule the man or his performances.
To laugh at with expressions of contempt; to deride.
To treat with contemptuous merriment; to expose to contempt or derision by writing.
ridiculed: Treated with laughter and contempt; derided.
ridiculing: Laughing at in contempt; exposing to contempt and derision.
ridiculous: That may justly excite laughter with contempt; as a ridiculous dress; ridiculous behavior. A fop and a dandy are ridiculous in their dress.
ridiculously: In a manner worthy of contemptuous merriment; as a man ridiculously vain.
ridiculousness: The quality of being ridiculous; as the ridiculousness of worshiping idols.
riding: Passing or traveling on a beast or in a vehicle; floating.
a. Employed to travel on any occasion. No suffragan bishop shall have more than one riding apparitor.
A road cut in a wood or through a ground, for the diversion of riding therein.
One of the three intermediate jurisdictions between a three and a hundred, into which the county of York, in England, is divided, anciently under the government of a reeve.
riding-clerk: In England, one of the six clerks in chancery.
riding-coat: A coat for riding on a journey.
riding-habit: A garment worn by females when they ride or travel.
riding-hood: A hood used by females when they ride; a kind of cloak with a hood.
riding-school: A school or place where the art of riding is taught. It may in some places be called a riding-house.
ridotto: A musical entertainment consisting of singing and dancing, in the latter of which the whole company join.
rife: Prevailing; prevalent. It is used of epidemic diseases. The plague was then rife in Hungary.
rifely: Prevalently; frequently. It was rifely reported that the Turks were coming in a great fleet.
rifle: To seize and bear away by force; to snatch away. Till time shall rifle ev’ry youthful grace.
To strip; to rob; to pillage; to plunder. You have rifled my master.
A gun about the usual length and size of a musket, the inside of whose barrel is rifled, that is, grooved, or formed with spiral channels.
rifled: Seized and carried away by violence; pillaged; channeled.
rifleman: A man armed with a rifle.
rifler: A robber; one that seizes and bears away by violence.
rifling: Plundering; seizing and carrying away by violence; grooving.
rift: A cleft; a fissure; an opening made by riving or splitting.
to cleave; to rive; to split; as, to rift an oak or a rock.
to burst open; to split. Timber - not apt to rift with ordnance.
rig: to dress; to put on; when applied to persons, not elegant, but rather a ludicrous word, to express the putting on of a gay, flaunting or unusual dress. Jack was rigged out in his gold and silver lace, with a feather in his cap.
To furnish with apparatus or gear; to fit with tackling.
To rig a ship, in seamen’s language, is to fit the shrouds, stays, bracesto their respective masts and yards.
rigadoon: a gay brisk dance performed by one couple, and said to have been borrowed from Provdence in France.
rigation: The act of watering; but irrigation is generally used.
rigged: Dressed; furnished with shrouds, staysas a ship.
rigger: One that rigs or dresses; one whose occupation is to fit the rigging of a ship.
rigging: Dress; tackle; particularly, the ropes which support the masts, extend and contract the sailsof a ship. This is of two kinds, standing rigging, as the shrouds and stays, and running rigging, such as braces, sheets, halliards, clewlines
riggle: To move one way and the other.
right: Properly; strained; stretched to straightness; hence,
Straight. A right line in geometry is the shortest line that can be drawn or imagined between two points. A right line may be horizontal, perpendicular, or inclined to the plane of the horizon.

In morals and religion, just; equitable; accordant to the standard of truth and justice or the will of God. That alone is right in the sight of God, which is consonant to his will or law; this being the only perfect standard of truth and justice. In social and political affairs, that is right which is consonant to the laws and customs of a country, provided these laws and customs are not repugnant to the laws of God. A man’s intentions may be right, though his actions may be wrong in consequence of a defect in judgment.

Fit; suitable; proper; becoming. In things indifferent, or which are regulated by no positive law, that is right which is best suited to the character, occasion or purpose, or which is fitted to produce some good effect. It is right for a rich man to dress himself and his family in expensive clothing, which it would not be right for a poor man to purchase. It is right for every man to choose his own time for eating or exercise. Right is a relative term; what may be right for one end, may be wrong for another.
Lawful; as the right heir of an estate.
righten: é. To do justice to. Obs.
righteous: Just; accordant to the divine law. Applied to persons, it denotes one who is holy in heart, and observant of the divine commands in practice; as a righteous man. Applied to things, it denotes consonant to the divine will or to justice; as a righteous act. It is used chiefly in theology, and applied to God, to his testimonies and to his saints. The righteous, in Scripture, denote the servants of God, the saints.
Just; equitable; merited. And I thy righteous doom will bless.
righteously: ri’chusly. Justly; in accordance with the laws of justice; equitably; as a criminal righteously condemned. Thou shalt judge the people righteously. Psalm 67:4.
righteousness: Purity of heart and rectitude of life; conformity of heart and life to the divine law. Righteousness, as used in Scripture and theology, in which it is chiefly used, is nearly equivalent to holiness, comprehending holy principles and affections of heart, and conformity of life to the divine law. It includes all we call justice, honesty and virtue, with holy affections; in short, it is true religion.
Applied to God, the perfection or holiness of his nature; exact rectitude; faithfulness.
The active and passive obedience of Christ, by which the law of God is fulfilled. Daniel 9:7.
Justice; equity between man and man.Luke 1:75.
righter: One who sets right; one who does justice or redresses wrong.
rightful: Having the right or just claim according to established laws; as the rightful heir to a throne or an estate.
Being by right, or by just claim; as a rightful lord; rightful property; rightful judge.
Just; consonant to justice; as a rightful cause; a rightful war.
rightfully: According to right, law or justice; as a title rightfully vested.
rightfulness: Justice; accordance with the rules of right; as the rightfulness of a claim to lands or tenements.
Moral rectitude. But still although we fail of perfect rightfulness.
right-hand: The hand opposite to the left, usually the strongest, most convenient or dextrous hand, and hence its name in other languages, as well as in our.
rightly: According to justice; according to the divine will or moral rectitude; as duty rightly performed.
Properly; fitly; suitably; as a person rightly named.
According to truth or fact; not erroneously. He has rightly conjectured.
Exactly. Thou didst not rightly see.
rightness: Correctness; conformity to truth or to the divine will, which is the standard of moral rectitude. It is important that a man should have such persuasion of the rightness of his conscience as to exclude rational doubt.
Straightness; as the rightness of a line.
rigid: Stiff; not pliant; not easily bent. It is applied to bodies or substances that are naturally soft or flexible, but not fluid. We never say, a rigid stone or rigid iron, nor do we say, rigid ice; but we say, an animal body or limb, when cold, is rigid. Rigid is then opposed to flexible, but expresses less than inflexible.
Strict in opinion, practice or discipline; severe in temper; opposed to lax or indulgent; as a rigid father or master; a rigid officer.
Strict; exact; as a rigid law or rule; rigid discipline; rigid criticism.
Severely just; as a rigid sentence or judgment.
Exactly according to the sentence or law; as rigid execution.
rigidity: Stiffness; want of pliability; the quality of not being easily bent.
A brittle hardness, as opposed to ductibility, malleability and softness.
Stiffness of appearance or manner; want of ease or airy elegance.
rigidly: Severely; strictly; exactly; without laxity, indulgence or abatement; as, to judge rigidly; to criticize rigidly; to execute a law rigidly.
rigidness: Stiffness of a body; the quality of not being easily bent; as the rigidness of a limb or of flesh.
Severity of temper; strictness in opinion or practice; but expressing less than inflexibility.
riglet: a flat thin piece of wood, used for picture frames; also used in printing; to regulate the margin
rigmarole: a repetition of stories; a succession of stories.
rigoli: a musical instrument consisting of several sticks bound together, but separated by beads.
rigor: In medicine, a sense of chilliness, with contradiction of the skin; a convulsive shuddering or slight tremor, as in the cold fit of a fever.
Stiffness of opinion or temper; severity; sternness. All his rigor is turned to grief and pity.
Severity of life; austerity; voluntary submission to pain, abstinence or mortification.
Strictness; exactness without allowance, latitude or indulgence; as the rigor of criticism; to execute a law with rigor; to enforce moral duties with rigor.
rigorous: Severe; allowing no abatement or mitigation; as a rigorous officer of justice.
Severe; exact; strict; without abatement or relaxation; as a rigorous execution of law; an enforcement of rigorous discipline.
Exact; strict; scrupulously accurate; as a rigorous definition or demonstration.
Severe; very cold; as a rigorous winter.
rigorously: Severely; without relaxation, abatement or mitigation; as a sentence rigorously executed.
Strictly; exactly; with scrupulous nicety; rigidly. The people would examine his works more vigorously than himself.
rigorousness: Severity without relaxation or mitigation; exactness.
rill: A small brook; a rivulet; a streamlet.
to run in a small stream, or in streamlets.
rim: the border, edge or margin of a thing; as the rim of a kettle or bason; usually applied to things circular or curving.
the lower part of the belly or abdomen.
to put on a rim or hoop at the border.
rimose: In botany, chinky; abounding with clefts, cracks or chinks; as the bark of trees.
rimous: In botany, chinky; abounding with clefts, cracks or chinks; as the bark of trees.
rind: The bark of a plant; the skin or coat of fruit that may be pared or peeled off; also, the inner bark of trees.
rindle: A small water course or gutter.
ring: A circle, or a circular line, or any thing in the form of a circular line or hoop. Thus we say of men, they formed themselves into a ring, to see a wrestling match. Rings of gold were made for the ark. Exodus 25:12-15. Rings of gold or other material are worn on the fingers and sometimes in the ears, as ornaments.
Acircular course. Place me, O place me in the dusty ring, where youthful charioteers contend for glory.
A sound; particularly, the sound of metals; as the ring of a bell.
Any loud sound, or the sounds of numerous voices; or sound continued, repeated or reverberated; as the ring’ of acclamations.
A chime, or set of bells harmonically tuned.
ring-bolt: An iron bolt with an eye to which is fitted a ring of iron.
ring-bone: A callus growing in the hollow circle of the little pastern of a horse, just above the coronet.
ringdove: A species of pigeon, the Columba palumbus, the largest of the European species.
ringent: In botany, a ringent or labiate corol is one which is irregular, monopetalous, with the border usually divided into two parts called the upper and lower lip; or irregular and gaping, like the mouth of an animal.
ringing: Causing to sound, as a bell; sounding; fitting with rings.
The act of sounding or of causing to sound.
ringleader: The leader of any association of men engaged in violating of law or an illegal enterprise, as rioters, mutineers and the like. this name is derived from the practice which men associating to oppose law have sometimes adopted, of signing their names to articles of agreement in a ring, that no one of their number might be distinguished as the leader.
ringlet: A curl; particularly, a curl of hair. Her golden tresses in wanton ringlets wav’d .
A circle. To dance our ringlets in the whistling wind.
ring-ousel: A bird of the genus Turdus, inhabiting the hilly and mountainous parts of Great Britain.
ring-streaked: Having circular streaks or lines on the body; as ring-streaked goats. Genesis 30:35.
ring-tail: A kind of kite with a whitish tail.
A small quadrilateral sail, set on a small mast on a ship’s tafferel.
ring-worm: A circular eruption on the skin; a kind of tetter.
rinse: To wash; to cleanse by washing. But in present usage,

To cleanse with a second or repeated application of water, after washing. We distinguish washing from rinsing. Washing is performed by rubbing, or with the use of soap; rinsing is performed with clean water, without much rubbing or the use of soap. Clothes are rinsed by dipping and dashing; and vessels are rinsed by dashing water on them, or by slight rubbing. A close barrel may be rinsed, but cannot well be washed.
rinsed: Cleansed with a second water; cleaned.
riot: In a general sense, tumult; uproar; hence technically, in law, a riotous assembling of twelve persons or more, and not dispersing upon proclamation. The definition of riot must depend on the laws. In Connecticut, the assembling of three persons or more, to do an unlawful act by violence against the person or property of another, and not dispersing upon proclamation, is declared to be a riot. In Massachusetts and New Hampshire, the number necessary to constitute a riot is twelve.
Luxury. The lamb thy riot dooms to bleed today. To run riot, to act or move without control or restraint.
To revel; to run to excess in feasting, drinking or other sensual indulgences.
rioter: One who indulges in loose festivity or excessive feasting.
In law, one guilty of meeting with others to do an unlawful act, and declining to retire upon proclamation.
riotous: Luxurious; wanton or licentious in festive indulgences; as riotous eaters of flesh. Proverbs 23:20.
Consisting of riot; tumultuous; partaking of the nature of an unlawful assembly; seditious.
Guilty of riot; applied to persons.
riotously: In the manner of an unlawful assembly; tumultuously; seditiously.
riotousness: The state or quality of being riotous.
rip: To separate by cutting or tearing; to tear or cut open or off; to tear off or out by violence; as, to rip open a garment by cutting the stitches; to rip off the skin of a beast; to rip open a sack; to rip off the shingles or clapboards of a house; to rip up a floor. We never use lacerate in these senses, but apply it to a partial tearing of the skin and flesh.
To take out or away by cutting or tearing. He'll rip the fatal secret from her heart.

To tear up for search or disclosure or for alteration; to search to the bottom; with up. You rip up the original of Scotland. They ripped up all that had been done from the beginning of the rebellion.
To rip out, as an oath.&
A tearing; a place torn; laceration.
ripe: Brought to perfection in growth or to the best state; mature; fit for use; as ripe fruit; ripe corn.
Advanced to perfection; matured; as ripe judgment, or ripe in judgment.
Finished; consummate; as a ripe scholar.
Brought to the point of taking effect; matured; ready; prepared; as things just ripe for war.
Fully qualified by improvement; prepared; as a student ripe for the university; a saint ripe for heaven.
ripen: To grow ripe; to be matured; as grain or fruit. Grain ripens best in dry weather.
To approach or come to perfection; to be fitted or prepared; as, a project is ripening for execution.
To mature; to make ripe; as grain or fruit.
To mature; to fit or prepare; as, to ripen one for heaven.
ripeness: The state of being ripe or brought to that state of perfection which fits for use; maturity; as the ripeness of grain.
Full growth. Time which made them their fame outlive, to Cowley scarce did ripeness give.
Perfection; completeness; as the ripeness of virtue, wisdom or judgment.
Complete maturation or suppuration, as of an ulcer or abscess.
riphean: An epithet given to certain mountains in the north of Asia, probably signifying snowy mountains.
ripier: In old laws, one who brings fish to market in the inland country.
ripper: In old laws, one who brings fish to market in the inland country.
One who tears or cuts open.
ripped: Torn or cut off or out; torn open.
ripping: Cutting or tearing off or open; tearing up.
ripple: To fret on the surface; as water when agitated or running over a rough bottom, appears rough and broken, or as if ripped or torn.
To agitate the surface of water.
The fretting of the surface of water; little curling waves.
A large comb or hatchel for cleaning flax.
rippling: The ripple dashing on the shore, or the noise of it.
The act or method of cleaning flax; a hatcheling.
riptowell: A gratuity given to tenants after they had reaped their lord’s corn.
rise: rize. pret. rose; pp. risen; pron. rose

To move to pass upward in any manner; to ascend; as, a fog rises from a river or from low ground; a fish rises in water; fowls rise in the air; clouds rise from the horizon towards the meridian; a balloon rises above the clouds.
To get up; to leave the place of sleep or rest; as, to rise from bed.
To get up or move from any recumbent to an erect posture; as, to rise after a fall.
To get up from a seat; to leave a sitting posture; as, to rise from a sofa or chair.
riser: One that rises; as an early riser.
Among joiners, the upright board of a stair.
risibility: The quality of laughing, or of being capable of laughter. Risibility is peculiar to the human species.
risible: Having the faculty or power of laughing. Man is a risible animal.

Laughable; capable of exciting laughter. The description of Falstaff in Shakespeare, exhibits a risible scene. Risible differs from ludicrous, as species from genus; ludicrous expressing that which is playful and sportive; risible, that which may excite laughter. Risible differs from ridiculous, as the latter implies something mean or contemptible, and risible does not.
rising: Getting up; ascending; mounting; springing; proceeding from; advancing; swelling; increasing; appearing above the horizon; reviving from death.
Increasing in wealth, power or distinction; as a rising state; a rising character.
The act of getting up from any recumbent or sitting posture.
The act of ascending; as the rising of vapor.
The act of closing a session, as of a public body; as the rising of the legislature.
risk: Hazard; danger; peril; exposure to harm. He, at the risk of his life, saved a drowning man.

In commerce, the hazard of loss, either of ship, goods or other property. Hence, risk signifies also the degree of hazard or danger; for the premiums of insurance are calculated upon the risk. The underwriters now take risks at a low premium. To run a risk, is to incur hazard; to encounter danger.
To hazard; to endanger; to expose to injury or loss; as, to risk goods on board of a ship; to risk one’s person in battle; to risk one’s fame by a publication; to risk life in defense of rights.
To venture; to dare to undertake; as, to risk a battle or combat.
risked: Hazarded; exposed to injury or loss.
risking: Hazarding; exposing to injury or loss.
rite: The manner of performing divine or solemn service as established by law, precept or custom; formal act of religion, or other solemn duty. The rites of the Israelites were numerous and expensive; the rites of modern churches are more simple. Funeral rites are very different in different countries. The sacrament is a holy rite.
ritornello: In music, a repeat; the burden of a song, or the repetition of a verse or strain,
ritual: Pertaining to rites; consisting of rites; as ritual service or sacrifices.
Prescribing rites; as the ritual law.
A book containing the rites to be observed, or the manner of performing divine service in a particular church, diocese or the like.
ritually: By rites; or by a particular rite.
rival: One who is in pursuit of the same object as another; one striving to reach or obtain something which another is attempting to obtain, and which one only can possess; a competitor; as rivals in love; rivals for a crown. Love will not patiently bear a rival.
One striving to equal or exceed another in excellence; as two rivals in eloquence.
An antagonist; a competitor in any pursuit or strife.
Having the same pretensions or claims; standing in competition for superiority; as rival lovers; rival claims or pretensions. Equal in years and rival in renown.
To stand in competition with; to strive to gain the object which another is contending for; as, to rival one in love.
rivalry: Competition; a strife or effort to obtain an object which another is pursuing; as rivalry in love; or an endeavor to equal or surpass another in some excellence; emulation; as rivalry for superiority at the bar or in the senate.
rivalship: The state or character of a rival.
Strife; contention for superiority; emulation; rivalry.
rive: pret. rived; pp. rived or riven. To split; to cleave; to rend asunder by force; as, to rive timber for rails or shingles with wedges; the riven oak; the riven clouds. The scolding winds have riv’d the knotty oaks.
To be split or rent asunder. Freestone rives, splits and breaks in any direction.
rivel: To contract into wrinkles; to shrink; as riveled fruits; riveled flowers.
riven: of rive. Split; rent or burst asunder.
river: A large stream of water flowing in a channel on land towards the ocean, a lake or another river. It is larger than a rivulet or brook; but is applied to any stream from the size of a mill-stream to that of the Danube, Maranon and Mississippi. We give this name to large streams which admit the tide and mingle salt water with fresh, as the rivers Hudson, Delaware and St. Lawrence.
A large stream; copious flow; abundance; as rivers of blood; rivers of oil .
river-dragon: A crocodile; a name given by Milton to the king of Egypt.
river-god: A deity supposed to preside over a river, as its tutelary divinity; a naiad.
river-horse: The hippopotamus, an animal inhabiting rivers.
river-water: The water of a river, as distinguished from rain-water.
rivet: To fasten with a rivet or with rivets; as, to rivet two pieces of iron.
To clinch; as, to rivet a pin or bolt.
To fasten firmly; to make firm, strong or immovable; as, to rivet friendship or affection.

a pin of iron or other metal with a head, driven through a piece of timber or metal, and the point bent or spread and beat down fast, to prevent its being drawn out; or a pin or bolt clinched at both ends.
rivulet: A small stream or book; a streamlet. By fountain or by shady rivulet, he sought them.
rix-dollar: A silver coin of Germany, Denmark and Sweden, of different value in different places. In Hamburg and some other parts of Germany, its value is the same as the American dollar, or 4-6d sterling. In other parts of Germany, its value is 3-6d sterling, or about cents.
roach: A fish of the genus Cyprinus, found in fresh water, easily caught and tolerably good for food. As sound as a roach, is a phrase supposed to have been originally, as sound as a rock.
road: An open way or public passage; ground appropriated for travel, forming a communication between one city, town or place and another. The word is generally applied to highways, and as a generic term it includes highway, street and lane. The military roads of the Romans were paved with stone, or formed of gravel or pebbles, and some of them remain to this day entire.
A place where ships may ride at anchor at some distance from the shore; sometimes called roadstead, that is, a place for riding, meaning at anchor.
An inroad; incursion of an enemy. On the road, passing; traveling.
roader: Among seamen, a vessel riding at anchor in a road or bay.
roadster: Among seamen, a vessel riding at anchor in a road or bay.
roam: To wander; to ramble; to rove; to walk or move about from place to place without any certain purpose or direction. The wolf and the savage roam in the forest. Daphne roaming through a thorny wood.
To range; to wander over; as, to roam the woods; but the phrase is elliptical.
roamer: A wanderer; a rover; a rambler; a vagrant.
roan: A roan horse is one that is of a bay, sorrel or dark color, with spots of gray or white thickly interspersed.
roan-tree: A tree of the genus Sorbus; the mountain ash.
roar: To cry with a full, loud, continued sound; to bellow, as a beast; as a roaring bull; a roaring lion.
To cry aloud, as in distress. The suffring chief roar’d out for anguish.
To cry aloud; to bawl; as a child.
To cause a loud continued sound. We say, the sea or the wind roars; a company roar in acclamation.
To make a loud noise. The brazen throat of war had ceas’d to roar.
roarer: One that roars, man or beast.
roaring: Crying like a bull or lion; uttering a deep loud sound.
The cry of a lion or other beast; outcry of distress, Job 3:24; loud continued sound of the billows of the sea or of a tempest. Isaiah 5:30.
roast: To cook, dress or prepare meat for the table by exposing it to heat, as on a spit. In a bake-pan, in an oven or the like. We now say, to roast meat on a spit, in a pan, or in a tin oven; to bake meat in an oven; to broil meat on a gridiron.
To prepare for food by exposure to heat; as, to roast apples or potatoes; to roast eggs.
To heat to excess; to heat violently. Roasted in wrath and fire.
To dry and parch by exposure to heat; as, to roast coffee.
In metallurgy, to dissipate the volatile parts of ore by heat.
roasted: Dressed by exposure to heat on a spit.
roaster: One that roasts meat; also, a gridiron.
roasting: Preparing for the table by exposure to heat on a spit; drying and parching.
rob: The inspissated juice of ripe fruit, mixed with honey or sugar to the consistence of a conserve.
In law, to take from the person of another feloniously, forcibly and by putting him in fear; as, to rob a passenger on the road.
To seize and carry from any thing by violence and with felonious intent; as, to rob a coach; to rob the mail.
To plunder; to strip unlawfully; as, to rob an orchard; to rob a man of his just praise.
To take away by oppression or by violence. Rob not the poor because he is poor. Proverbs 22:22.
roballo: A fish found in Mexico, which affords a most delicate food.
robbed: Deprived feloniously and by violence; plundered; seized and carried away by violence.
robber: In law, one that takes goods or money from the person of another by force or menaces, and with a felonious intent.
In a looser sense, one who takes that to which he has no right; one who steals, plunders or strips by violence and wrong.
robbery: In law, the forcible and felonious taking from the person of another any money or goods, putting him in fear, that is, by violence or by menaces of death or personal injury. Robbery differs from theft, as it is a violent felonious taking from the person or presence of another; whereas theft is a felonious taking of goods privately from the person, dwellingof another. These words should not be confounded.
A plundering; a pillaging; a taking away by violence, wrong or oppression.
robbing: Feloniously taking from the person of another; putting him in fear; stripping; plundering; taking from another unlawfully or by wrong or oppression.
robbins: Short flat plaited pieces of rope with an eye in one end, used in pairs to tie the upper edges of square sails to their yards. I
rope-bands: Short flat plaited pieces of rope with an eye in one end, used in pairs to tie the upper edges of square sails to their yards. I
robe: A kind of gown or long loose garment worn over other dress, particularly by persons in elevated stations. The robe is properly a dress of state or dignity, as of princes, judges, priestsSee Samuel 24:4; Matthew 27:28.
A splendid female gown or garment. 2 Samuel 13:18.
In Scripture, the vesture of purity or righteousness, and of happiness. Job 29:14; Luke 15:22.
To put on a robe; or to dress with magnificence; to array.
robed: Dressed with a robe; arrayed with elegance.
robersman: In the old statutes of England, a bold stout robber or night thief, said to be so called from Robinhood, a famous robber.
robertsman: In the old statutes of England, a bold stout robber or night thief, said to be so called from Robinhood, a famous robber.
robert: A plant of the genus Geranium; stork’s bill.
robertine: One of an order of monks, so called from Robert Flower, the founder, A.D. 1187.
robin: A bird of the genus Motacilla, called also redbreast. This is the English application of the word.
In the United States, a bird with a red breast, a species of Turdus.
roborant: A medicine that strengthens; but corroborant is generally used.
robust: Strong; lusty; sinewy; muscular; vigorous; forceful; as a robust body; robust youth. It implies full flesh and sound health.
Violent; rough; rude. Romp loving miss is haul’d about in gallantry robust.
robustness: Strength; vigor, or the condition of the body when it has full firm flesh and sound health.
rocambole: A sort of wild garlic, the Allisum scorodoprasum, growing naturally in Denmark and Sweden. It has a heart-shaped root at the side of the stalk.
rokambole: A sort of wild garlic, the Allisum scorodoprasum, growing naturally in Denmark and Sweden. It has a heart-shaped root at the side of the stalk.
roche-alum: Rock-alum, a purer kind of alum. Rochelle salt, tartrate of potash and soda.
rochet: A surplice; the white upper garment of a priest worn while officiating.
A fish, the roach, which see.
rock: A large mass of stony matter, usually compounded of two or more simple minerals, either bedded in the earth or resting on its surface. Sometimes rocks compose the principal part of huge mountains; sometimes hugh rocks lie on the surface of the earth, in detached blocks or masses. Under this term, mineralogists class all mineral substances, coal, gypsum, salt.
In Scripture, figuratively, defense; means of safety; protection; strength; asylum. The Lord is my rock. 2 Samuel 22:2.
Firmness; a firm or immovable foundation. Psalm 28:1; Matthew 7:24-25;, Matthew 16:18.
A fabulous bird in the Eastern tales.
rock-bason: A cavity or artificial bason cut in a rock for the purpose, as is supposed, of collecting the dew or rain for ablutions and purifications prescribed by the druidical religion.
rock-butter: A subsulphite of alumin, oozing from aluminous rocks.
rock-crystal: The most perfect variety of silicious earth or quartz; limpid quartz. When purest it is white or colorless, but it is found of a grayish or yellowish white, pale yellow or citron. Its most usual form is that of hexagonal prisms, surmounted by hexagonal pyramids.
rocked: Moved one way and the other.
rocker: One who rocks the cradle; also, the curving piece of wood on which a cradle or chair rocks.
rocket: An artificial fire-work, consisting of a cylindrical case of paper, filled with a composition of combustible ingredients, as niter, charcoal and sulphur. This being tied to a stick and fired, ascends into the air and bursts.

A plant of the genus Brassica. There is also the bastard rocket, of the genus Reseda; the corn rocket and the sea rocket, of the genus Bunias; the marsh rocket, the water rocket, and the winter rocket, of the genus Sisymbrium; and the dame’s violet rocket, of the genus Hesperis.
rock-oil: Another name for petrol or petroleum.
rock-pigeon: A pigeon that builds her nest on a rock.
rock-rose: A plant of the genus Cistus.
rock-ruby: A name sometimes given to the garnet, when it is of a strong, but not a deep red, and has a cast of blue.
rock-salt: Fossil or mineral salt; salt dug from the earth; muriate of soda. But in America, this name is sometimes given to salt that comes in large crystals from the West Indies, which salt is formed by evaporation from sea water, in large basons or cavities, on the isles. Hexahedral rock-salt occurs foliated and fibrous.
rock-work: Stones fixed in mortar in imitation of the asperities of rocks, forming a wall.
rocky: Full of rocks; as a rocky mountain; rocky shore.
Resembling a rock; as the rocky orb of a shield.
Very hard; stony; obdurate; insusceptible of impression; as a rocky bosom.
rodomontade: Vain boasting; empty bluster or vaunting; rant. I could show that the rodomontades of Almanzor are neither so irrational nor impossible.
To boast; to brag; to bluster; to rant.
rodomontadist: A blustering boaster; one that brags or vaunts.
rodomontador: A blustering boaster; one that brags or vaunts.
roe: A species of deer, the Cervus capreolus, with erect cylindrical branched horns, forked at the summit. This is one of the smallest of the cervine genus, but of elegant shape and remarkably nimble. It prefers a mountainous country, and herds in families. ( .
Roe, the female of the hart.
The seed or spawn of fishes. The roe of the male is call soft roe or milt; that of the female, hard roe or spawn.
roebuck: A species of deer, the Cervus capreolus, with erect cylindrical branched horns, forked at the summit. This is one of the smallest of the cervine genus, but of elegant shape and remarkably nimble. It prefers a mountainous country, and herds in families. ( .
Roe, the female of the hart.
rogation: Litany; supplication. He perfecteth the rogations or litanies before in use.
In Roman jurisprudence, the demand by the consuls or tribunes, of a law to be passed by the people.
rogation-week: The second week before Whitsunday, thus called from the three fasts observed therein; viz, on Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday, called rogation-days, because of the extraordinary prayers then made for the fruits of the earth, or as a preparation for the devotion of the Holy Thursday.
rogue: In law, a vagrant; a sturdy beggar; a vagabond. Persons of this character were, by the ancient laws of England, to be punished by whipping and having the ear bored with a hot iron.

A knave; a dishonest person; applied now, I believe, exclusively to males. This word comprehends thieves and robbers, but is generally applied to such as cheat and defraud in mutual dealings, or to counterfeiters. The rogue and fool by fits is fair and wise.
Aname of slight tenderness and endearment. Alas, poor rogue, I think indeed she loves.
roguery: Knavish tricks; cheating; fraud; dishonest practices. Tis no scandal grown, for debt and roguery to quit the town.
rogueship: The qualities or personage of a rogue.
roguishness: The qualities of a rogue; knavery; mischievousness.
Archness; sly cunning; as the roguishness of a look.
roil: To render turbid by stirring up the dregs or sediment; as, to roil wine, cider or other liquor in casks or bottles.
To excite some degree of anger; to disturb the passion of resentment.
roiled: Rendered turbid or foul by disturbing the lees or sediment; angered slightly; disturbed in mind by an offense.
roiling: Rendering turbid; or exciting the passion of anger.
roist: To bluster; to swagger; to bully; to be bold, noisy, vaunting or turbulent.
roister: To bluster; to swagger; to bully; to be bold, noisy, vaunting or turbulent.
roll: To move by turning on the surface, or with a circular motion in which all parts of the surface are successively applied to a plane; as, to roll a barrel or puncheon; to roll a stone or ball. Sisyphus was condemned to roll a stone to the top of a hill, which, when he had done so, rolled down again, and thus his punishment was eternal.
To revolve; to turn on its axis; as, to roll a wheel or a planet.
To move in a circular direction. To dress, to troll the tongue and roll the eye.
To wrap round on itself; to form into a circular or cylindrical body; as, to roll a piece of cloth; to roll a sheet of paper; to roll parchment; to roll tobacco.
To enwrap; to bind or involve in a bandage or the like.
rolled: Moved by turning; formed into a round or cylindrical body; leveled with a roller, as land.
roller: That which rolls; that which turns on its own axis; particularly, a cylinder of wood, stone or metal, used in husbandry and the arts. Rollers are of various kinds and used for various purposes.
A bandage; a fillet; properly, a long and broad bandage used in surgery.
A bird of the magpie kind, about the size of a jay. A bird of the genus Coracias, found in Europe; called also the German parrot.
rolling: Turning over; revolving; forming into a cylinder or round mass; leveling, as land.
The motion of a ship from side to side.
rolling-pin: A round piece of wood, tapering at each end, with which paste is molded and reduced to a proper thickness.
rolling-press: An engine consisting of two cylinders, by which cloth is calendared, waved and tabbied; also an engine for taking impressions from copper plates; also, a like engine for drawing plates of metal
rolly-pooly: A game in which a ball, rolling into a certain place.
romal: romaul’. A species of silk handkerchief.
roman: Pertaining to Rome, or to the Roman people.

Romish; popish; professing the religion of the pope. Roman catholic, as an adjective, denoting the religion professed by the people of Rome and of Italy, at the head of which is the pope or bishop of Rome; as a noun, one who adheres to the papal religion.
A citizen of Rome; one enjoying the privileges of a Roman citizen.
One of the christian church at Rome to which Paul addressed an epistle, consisting of converts from Judaism or paganism.
romance: A fabulous relation or story of adventures and incidents, designed for the entertainment of readers; a tale of extraordinary adventures, fictitious and often extravagant, usually a tale of love or war, subjects interesting the sensibilities of the heart, or the passions of wonder and curiosity. Romance differs from the novel, as it treats of great actions and extraordinary adventures; that is, according to the Welch signification, it vaults or soars beyond the limits of fact and real life, and often of probability. The first romances were a monstrous assemblage of histories, in which truth and fiction were blended without probability; a composition of amorous adventures and the extravagant ideas of Chivalry.
romans’, ro’mans. To forge and tell fictitious stories; to deal in extravagant stories.
romancing: Inventing and telling fictitious tales; building castles in the air.
romanism: The tenets of the church of Rome.
romanist: An adherent to the papal religion; a Roman catholic.
romanize: To latinize; to fill with Latin words or modes of speech.
To convert to the Roman catholic religion, or to papistical opinions.
To conform to Romish opinions, customs or modes of speech.
romansh: The language of the Grisons in Switzerland, a corruption of the Latin.
romantic: Pertaining to romance, or resembling it; wild; fanciful; extravagant; as a romantic taste; romantic notions; romantic expectations; romantic zeal.
Improbably or chimerical; fictitious; as a romantic tale.
Fanciful; wild; full of wild or fantastic scenery; as a romantic prospect or landscape; a romantic situation.
romanzovite: A recently discovered mineral of the garnet kind, of a brown or brownish yellow color; named from count Romanzoff.
romepenny: A tax of a penny on a house, formerly paid by the people of England to the church of Rome.
romescot: A tax of a penny on a house, formerly paid by the people of England to the church of Rome.
romish: Belonging or relating to Rome, or to the religion professed by the people of Rome and of the western empire, of which Rome was the metropolis; catholic; popish; as the Romish church; the Romish religion, ritual or ceremonies.
romp: A rude girl who indulges in boisterous play.
Rude play or frolic. Romp loving miss is haul’d about in gallantry robust.
To play rudely and boisterously; to leap and frisk about in play.
romping: Playing rudely; as a noun, rude boisterous play.
rompish: Given to rude play; inclined to romp.
rompishness: Disposition to rude boisterous play; or the practice of romping.
rompu: In heraldry, an ordinary that is broken, or a chevron, a bend or the like, whose upper points are cut off.
rompee: In heraldry, an ordinary that is broken, or a chevron, a bend or the like, whose upper points are cut off.
rondeau: Akind of poetry, commonly consisting of thirteen verses, of which eight have one rhyme, and five another. It is divided into three couplets, and at the end of the second and third, the beginning of the rondeau is repeated in an equivocal sense, if possible.

In music, the rondo, vocal or instrumental, generally consists of three strains, the first of which closes in the original key, while each of the others is so constructed in modulation as to reconduct the ear in an easy and natural manner to the first strain.
A kind of jig or lively tune that ends with the first strain repeated.
rondo: Akind of poetry, commonly consisting of thirteen verses, of which eight have one rhyme, and five another. It is divided into three couplets, and at the end of the second and third, the beginning of the rondeau is repeated in an equivocal sense, if possible.

In music, the rondo, vocal or instrumental, generally consists of three strains, the first of which closes in the original key, while each of the others is so constructed in modulation as to reconduct the ear in an easy and natural manner to the first strain.
A kind of jig or lively tune that ends with the first strain repeated.
rong: the old pret. and pp. of ring, now rung.
ront: An animal stinted in its growth.
rood: The fourth part of an acre, or forty square rods. [See Acre.I .
A pole; a measure of five yards; a rod or perch.
The cross; or an image of Christ, of the virgin Mary and a saint or St. John, on each side of it.
roodloft: A loft or gallery in a church on which relics and images were set to view.
roof: The cover or upper part of a house or other building, consisting of rafters covered with boards, shingles or tiles, with a side or sides sloping from the ridge, for the purpose of carrying off the water that falls in rain or snow. In Asia, the roofs of houses are flat or horizontal. The same name, roof, is given to the sloping covers of huts, cabins and ricks; to the arches of ovens, furnaces.
A vault; an arch; or the interior of a vault; as the roof of heaven.
The vault of the mouth; the upper part of the mouth; the palate. If I do not remember thee, let my tongue cleave to the roof of my mouth. Psalm 137:6.
To cover with a roof. I have not seen the remains of any Roman buildings, that have not been roofed with vaults or arches.
To inclose in a house; to shelter. Here had we now our country’s honor roof'd.
roofed: Furnished or covered with a roof or arch.
roofing: The materials of which a roof is composed; or materials for a roof.
roofless: Having no roof; as a roofless house.
Having no house or home; unsheltered.
rook: A fowl of the genus Corvus, the fowl mentioned by Virgil under this name. This fowl resembles the crow, but differs from it in not feeding on carrion, but on insects and grain. In crows also the nostrils and root of the bill are clothed with feathers, but in rooks the same parts are naked, or have only a few bristly hairs. The rook is gregarious.
A cheat; a trickish, rapacious fellow.
To cheat; to defraud by cheating.
rooky: Inhabited by rooks; as the rooky wood.
room: To occupy an apartment; to lodge; an academic use of the word. A B rooms at No. 7.
Space; compass; extent of place, great or small. Let the words occupy as little room as possible.
Space or place unoccupied. Lord, it is done as thou hast commanded, and yet there is room. Luke 14:22.
Place for reception or admission of any thing. In this case, there is no room for doubt or for argument.
Place of another; stead; as in succession or substitution. One magistrate or king comes in the room of a former one. We often place one thing in the room of another. 7 Kings 20:24.
roominess: Space; spaciousness; large extent of space. Roomth, space, and roomthy, spacious, are ill formed words and not used in the United States.
roomy: Spacious; wide; large; having ample room; as a roomy mansion; a roomy deck.
roost: The pole or other support on which fowls rest at night. He clapp’d his wings upon his roost. At roost, in a state for rest and sleep.
To sit, rest or sleep, as fowls on a pole, tree or other thing at night.
roosting: Sitting for rest and sleep at night.
root: That part of a plant which enters and fixes itself in the earth, and serves to support the plant in an erect position, while by means of its fibrils it imbibes nutriment for the stem, branches and fruit.
The part of any thing that resembles the roots of a plant in manner of growth; as the roots of a cancer, of teeth.
The bottom or lower part of any thing. Deep to the roots of hell - . Burnet uses root of a mountain, but we now say base, foot or bottom. See Job 28:9.
A plant whose root is esculent or the most useful part; as beets, carrots.
root-bound: a2. Fixed to the earth by roots.
rooted: Having its roots planted or fixed in the earth; hence, fixed; deep; radical; as rooted sorrow; rooted aversion; rooted prejudices.
rooter: One that roots; or one that tears up by the roots.
rooting: Striking or taking root; turning up with the snout.
root-leaf: A leaf growing immediately from the root.
rootlet: A radicle; the fibrous part of a root.
rooty: Full of roots; as rooty ground.
ropalic: Club-formed; increasing or selling towards the end.
rope: A large string or line composed of several strands twisted together. It differs from cord, line and string, only in its size; being the name given to all sorts of cordage above an inch in circumference. Indeed the smaller ropes, when used for certain purposes, are called lines. Ropes are by seamen ranked under two descriptions, cable-laid, and hawser-laid; the former composed of nine strands, or three great strands, each consisting of three small ones; the latter made with three strands, each composed of a certain number of rope- yarns.
A row or string consisting of a number of things united; as a rope of onions.
Ropes, the intestines of birds. Rope of sand, proverbially, feeble union or tie; a band easily broken.
To draw out or extend into a filament or thread, by means of any glutinous or adhesive quality. Any glutinous substance will rope considerably before it will part.
rope-dancer: One that walks on a rope suspended.
rope-maker: One whose occupation is to make ropes or cordage.
rope-making: The art or business of manufacturing ropes or cordage.
ropery: A place where ropes are made.
A trick that deserves the halter.
rope-trick: A trick that deserves the halter.
rope-walk: A long covered walk, or a long building over smooth ground, where ropes are manufactured.
rope-yarn: Yarn for ropes, consisting of a single thread. The threads are twisted into strands, and the strands into ropes.
ropiness: Stringiness, or aptness to draw out in a string or thread without breaking, as of glutinous substances; viscosity; adhesiveness.
ropy: Stringy; adhesive; that may be drawn into a thread; as a glutinous substance; viscous; tenacious; glutinous; as ropy wine; ropy lees.
roral: Pertaining to dew or consisting of dew; dewy.
rosaceous: s as z. Rose-like; composed of several petals, arranged in a circular form; as a rosaceous corol.
rosary: A bed of roses, or place where roses grow.
A string of beads used by Roman catholics, on which they count their prayers.
rosasic: The rosasic acid is obtained from the urine of persons affected with intermitting and nervous fevers.
roscid: Dewy; containing dew, or consisting of dew.
rose: A plant and flower of the genus Rosa, of many species and varieties, as the wild canine or dog-rose, the white rose, the red rose, the cinnamon rose, the eglantine or sweet briarThere are five petals; the calyx is urceolate, quinquefid, and corneous; the seeds are numerous, hispid, and fixed to the inside of the calyx.

A knot of ribbon in the form of a rose, used as an ornamental tie of ashoe. Under the rose, in secret; privately; in a manner that forbids disclosure. Rose of Jericho, a plant growing on the plain of Jericho, the Anastatica hierochuntica.
roseal: Like a rose in smell or color.
roseate: Rosy; full of roses; as roseate bowers.
Blooming; of a rose color; as roseate beauty.
rosebay: A plant, the Nerium oleander. The dwarf rosebay is the rhododendron.
rose-mallow: A plant of the genus Alcea, larger than the common mallow.
rosemary: A verticillate plant of the genus Rosmarinus, growing naturally in the southern part of France, Spain and Italy. It has a fragrant smell and a warm pungent bitterish taste.
rose-noble: A ancient English gold coin, stamped with the figure of a rose, first struck in the reign of Edward III and current at 6s. 8d. or according to Johnson, at shillings.
rose-quartz: A subspecies of quartz, rose red or milk white.
rose-root: A plant of the genus Rhodiola.
roset: A red color used by painters.
rose-water: Water tinctured with roses by distillation.
rose-wood: A plant or tree of the genus Aspalathus, growing in warm climates, from which is obtained the oleum rhodii, an agreeable perfume, used in scenting pomatum and liniments.
rosicrucian: The Rosicrucians were a sect or cabal of hermetical philosophers, or rather fanatics, who sprung up in Germany in the fourteenth century, and made great pretensions to science; and among other things, pretended to be masters of the secret of the philosopher’s stone.
Pertaining to the Rosicrucians, or their arts.
rosin: Inspissated turpentine, a juice of the pine.
Any inspissated matter of vegetables that dissolves in spirit of wine.
rosiness: s as z. The quality of being rosy, or of resembling the color of the rose.
rosiny: Like rosin, or partaking of its qualities.
rosland: Heathy land; land full of ling; moorish or watery land.
rospo: A fish of Mexico, perfectly round, without scales, and good for food.
ross: The rough scaly matter on the surface of the bark of certain trees.
rostel: In botany, the descending plane part of the coracle or heart, in the first vegetation of a seed.
roster: In military affairs, a plan or table by which the duty of officers is regulated. In Massachusetts, a list of the officers of a division, brigade, regiment or battalion, containing under several heads their names, rank, the corps to which they belong, date of commission and place of abode. These are called division rosters, brigade rosters, regimental or battalion rosters. The word is also used frequently instead of register, which comprehends a general list of all the officers of the state, from the commander in chief to the lowest in commission, under the same appropriate heads, with an additional column for noting the alterations which take place.
rostral: Resembling the beak of a ship.
rostrate: In botany, beaked; having a process resembling the beak of a bird.
Furnished or adorned with beaks; as rostrated galleys.
rostrated: In botany, beaked; having a process resembling the beak of a bird.
Furnished or adorned with beaks; as rostrated galleys.
rostrum: The beak or bill of a bird.
The beak or head of a ship.
In ancient Rome, a scaffold or elevated place in the forum, where orations, pleadings funeral harangues, were delivered.
The pipe which conveys the distilling liquor into its receiver, in the common alembic.
A crooked pair of scissors, used by surgeons for dilating wounds.
rosy: Resembling a rose in color or qualities; blooming; red; blushing; charming. While blooming youth and gay delight sit on thy rosy check contest. The rosy morn resigns her light.
Made in the form of a rose.
rot: To lose the natural cohesion and organization of parts, as animal and vegetable substances; to be decomposed and resolved into its original component parts by the natural process, or the gradual operation of heat and air; to putrefy.
To make putrid; to cause to be decomposed by the natural operation of air and heat; to bring to corruption.

A fatal distemper incident to sheep, usually supposed to be owing to wet seasons and moist pastures. The immediate cause of the mortality of sheep, in this disease, is found to be a great number of small animals, called flukes, (Fascida,) found in the liver, and supposed to be produced from eggs swallowed with their food.
Dry rot, in timber, the decay of the wood without the access of water.
rota: An ecclesiastical court of Rome, composed of twelve prelates, of whom one must be a German, another a Frenchman, and two Spaniards; the other eight are Italians. This is one of the most august tribunals in Rome, taking cognizance of all suits in the territory of the church by appeal, and of all matters beneficiary and patrimonial.
In English history, a club of politicians, who, in the time of Charles I. contemplated an equal government by rotation .
rotary: Turning, as a wheel on its axis; as rotary motion.
rotate: In botany, wheel-shaped; monopetalous, spreading flat, without any tube, or expanding into a flat border, with scarcely any tube; as a rotate corol.
rotation: The act of turning, as a wheel or solid body on its axis, as distinguished from the progressive motion of a body revolving round another body or a distant point. Thus the daily turning of the earth on its axis, is a rotation; its annual motion round the sun is a revolution.
Vicissitude of succession; the course by which officers or others leave their places at certain times and are succeeded by others; applied also to a change of crops.
rotato-plane: In botany, wheel-shaped and flat, without a tube; as a rotato-plane corol.
rotator: That which gives a circular or rolling motion; a muscle producing a rolling motion.
rotatory: Turning on an axis, as a wheel; rotary.
Going in a circle; following in succession; as rotatory assemblies.
rote: A kind of violin or harp. Obs.

Properly, a round of words; frequent repetition of words or sounds, without attending to the signification, or to principles and rules; a practice that impresses words in the memory without an effort of the understanding, and without the aid of rules. Thus children learn to speak by rote; they often repeat what they hear, till it becomes familiar to them. So we learn to sing by rote, as we hear notes repeated, and soon learn to repeat them ourselves.

To fix in the memory by means of frequent repetition ourselves, or by hearing the repetition of others, without an effort of the understanding to comprehend what is repeated, and without the aid of rules or principles.
To go out by rotation or succession.
rother-beasts: Cattle of the bovine genus; called in England black cattle. [Not used in America.,
rother-nails: Among shipwrights, nails with very full heads, used for fastening the rudder irons of ships.
rothoffite: A variety of grenate, brown or black, found in Sweden. It has a resemblance to melanite, another variety, but differs from it in having a small portion of alumin.
rotten: Putrid; carious; decomposed by the natural process of decay; as a rotten plank.
Not firm or trusty; unsound; defective in principle; treacherous; deceitful .
Defective in substance; not sound or hard.
rottenness: State of being decayed or putrid; cariousness; putrefaction; unsoundness.
rotten-stone: A soft stone or mineral, called also Tripoli, terra Tripolitana, from the country from which it was formerly brought. It is used in all sorts of finer grinding and polishing in the arts, and for cleaning furniture of metallic substances. The rotten-stone of Derbyshire, in England, is a Tripoli mixed with calcarious earth.
rotund: In botany, circumscribed by one unbroken curve, or without angles; as a rotund leaf.
rotundity: Roundness; sphericity; circularity; as the rotundity of a globe.
rotundo: A round building; any building that is round both on the outside and inside. The most celebrated edifice of this kind is the Pantheon at Rome.
roucou: roo’coo. A substance used in dyeing; the same as anotta.
rouge: Red paint; a substance used for painting the cheeks.
To paint the face, or rather the cheeks.
To paint, or tinge with red paint.
rough: Having inequalities, small ridges or points on the surface; not smooth or plane; as a rough board, a rough stone; rough cloth.
Stony; abounding with stones and stumps; as rough land; or simply with stones; as a rough road.
Not wrought or polished; as a rough diamond.
Thrown into huge waves; violently agitated; as a rough sea.
Tempestuous; stormy; boisterous; as rough weather.
rough-cast: To form in its first rudiments, without revision, correction and polish.
To mold without nicety or elegance, or to form with asperities.
To cover with a mixture of plaster and shells or pebbles; as, to rough-cast a building.
A rude model; the form of a thing in its first rudiments.
rough-draught: ruf’-draft. A draught in its rudiments; a draught not perfected; a sketch.
rough-draw: ruf’-draw. To draw or delineate coarsely.
roughen: rufn. To grow or become rough.
rough-footed: ruf’-footed. Feather-footed; as a rough-footed dove.
rough-hew: To hew coarsely without smoothing; as, to rough-hew timber.
To give the first form or shape to a thing. There’s a divinity that shapes our ends, rough-hew them how we will.
rough-hewn: Rugged; unpolished; of coarse manners; rude. A rough-hewn seaman.
roughings: ruf’ings. Grass after mowing or reaping.
roughly: With uneven surface; with asperities on the surface.
Harshly; uncivilly; rudely; as, to be treated roughly.
Severely; without tenderness; as, to blame too roughly.
roughness: Unevenness of surface, occasioned by small prominences; asperity of surface; as the roughness of a board, of a floor, or of a rock.
Austereness to the taste; as the roughness of sloes.
rove: To wander; to ramble; to range; to go, move or pass without certain direction in any manner, by’ walking, riding, flying or otherwise. For who has power to walk, has power to rove.
To wander over; as roving a field; roving the town. This is an elliptical form of expression, for roving over, through or about the town.
To draw a thread, string or cord through an eye or aperture.
rover: A wanderer; one who rambles about.
A robber or pirate; a freebooter. At rovers, without any particular aim; at random; as shooting at rovers.
roving: Rambling; wandering; passing a cord through an eye.
row: A series of persons or things arranged in a continued line; a line; a rank; a file; as a row of trees; a row of gems or pearls; a row of houses or columns. Where the bright Seraphim in burning row.
To impel, as a boat or vessel along the surface of water by oars; as, to row a boat.
To transport by rowing; as, to row the captain ashore in his barge.
To labor with the oar; as, to row well; to row with oars muffled.
rowable: Capable of being rowed or rowed upon.
rowel: The little wheel of a spur, formed with sharp points.
Among farriers, a roll of hair or silk, used as an issue on horses, answering to a seton in surgery.
A little flat ring or wheel of plate or iron on horses’ bits.
To insert a rowel in; to pierce the skin and keep open the wound by a rowel.
rowen: Rowen is a field kept up till after Michaelmas, that the corn left on the ground may sprout into green. Turn your cows that give milk into your rowens, till snow comes. In New England, the second growth of grass in a season. We never apply the word to a field, nor to a growth of corn, after harvest, nor is the word ever used in the plural. The first growth of grass for mowing is called the first crop, and the second rowen.
rower: One that rows or manages an oar in rowing.
rowing: Impelling, as a boat by oars.
row-lock: That part of a boat’s gunwale on which the oar rests in rowing.
row-port: A little square hole in the side of small vessels of war, near the surface of the water, for the use of an oar for rowing in a calm.
royal: Kingly; pertaining to a king; regal; as royal power or prerogative; a royal garden; royal domains; the royal family.
Becoming a king; magnificent; as royal state.
Noble; illustrious. How doth that royal merchant, good Antonio?
A large kind of paper. It is used as a noun or an adjective.
Among seamen, a small sail spread immediately above the top- gallant-sail; sometimes termed the top-gallant-royal.
royalism: Attachment to the principles or cause of royalty, or to a royal government.
royalist: An adherent to a king, or one attached to a kingly government. Where Candish fought, the royalist prevail’d.
royally: In a kingly manner; like a king; as becomes a king. His body shall be royally interr’d.
royalty: Kingship; the character, state or office of a king. Royalty by birth was the sweetest way of majesty.
roynish: Mean; paltry; as the roynish clown.
rub: To move something along the surface of a body with pressure; as, to rub the face or arms with the hand; to rub the body with flannel. Vessels are scoured or cleaned by rubbing them.
To wipe; to clean; to scour; but rub is a generic term, applicable to friction for every purpose.
To touch so as to leave behind something which touches; to spread over; as to rub any thing with oil.
To polish; to retouch; with over. The whole business of our redemption is to rub over the defaced copy of the creation.

To obstruct by collision. In popular language, rub is used for teasing, fretting, upbraiding, reproaching or vexing with gibes or sarcasms. To rub down, to clean by rubbing; to comb or curry, as a horse. To rub off, to clean any thing by rubbing; to separate by friction; as, to rub off rust.
rubbage: for rubbish, vulgar and not used.
rubbidge: for rubbish, vulgar and not used.
rubble: for rubbish, vulgar and not used.
rubber: The instrument or thing used in rubbing or cleaning.
A coarse file, or the rough part of it.

The gaming, two games out of three; or the game that decides the contest; or a contest consisting of three games. India rubber, elastic resin, or caoutchouc, a substance produced from the syringe tree of south America; a substance remarkably pliable and elastic.
rubbish: Fragments of buildings; broken or imperfect pieces of any structure; ruins. He saw the towns one half in rubbish lie.
Waste or rejected matter; any thing worthless.
rubble-stone: A stone, so called from its being rubbed and worn by water; graywacke.
rubefacient: In medicine, a substance or external application which excites redness of the skin.
rubellite: A silicious mineral of a red color of various shades; the red shorl; siberite. It occurs in accumulated groups of a middle or large size, with straight tubular-like stria. In a red heat, it becomes snow-white and seems to phosphoresce. Rubellite is red tourmaline.
rubescent: Growing or becoming red; tending to a red color.
rubican: Rubican color of a horse, is a bay, sorrel or black, with a light gray or white upon the flanks, but the gray or white not predominant there.
rubicel: A gem or mineral, a variety of ruby of a reddish color, from Brazil.
rubied: Red as a ruby; as a rubied lip; rubied nectar.
rubiform: Having the form of red; as, the rubiform rays of the sun are least refrangible.
ruble: roo’bl. A silver coin of Russia, of the value of about fifty seven cents, or two shillings and seven pence sterling; in Russia, a hundred kopecks; originally, the fourth part of a grivna or pound, which was cut into four equal parts.
rubric: In the canon law, a title or article in certain ancient law books; so called because written in red letters.
Directions printed in prayer books. The rubric and the rules relating to the liturgy are established by royal authority, as well as the liturgy itself.
rubricate: To mark or distinguish with red.
ruby: A precious stone; a mineral of a carmine red color, sometimes verging to violet, or intermediate between carmine and hyacinth red; but its parts vary in color, and hence it is called sapphire ruby or orange red, and by some vermeille or rubicel. There are two kinds of ruby, the oriental or corundum, and the spinelle. The latter is distinguishable from the former by its color and crystallization. The ruby is next in hardness and value to the diamond, and highly esteemed in jewelry.

A blain; a blotch; a carbuncle. Ruby of arsenic or sulphur, is the realgar, or red combination of arsenic and sulphur. Ruby of zink, is the red blend. Rock ruby, the amethystizontes of the ancients, is the most value species of garnet.
ruck: To cower; to bend and set close. J .
To wrinkle; as, to ruck up cloth or a garment.
A wrinkle; a fold; a plait.
ructation: The act of belching wind from the stomach.
rud: to make red, used by Spenser, is a different spelling of red. Obs.
rudd: A fish of the genus Cyprinus, with a deep body like the bream, but thicker, a prominent back, and small head. The back is of an olive color; the sides and belly yellow, marked with red; the ventral and anal fins and tail of a deep red color.
rudder: In navigation, the instrument by which a ship is steered; that part of the helm which consists of a piece of timber, broad at the bottom, which enters the water and is attached to the stern-post by hinges, on which it turns. This timber is managed by means of the tiller or wheel.
That which guides or governs the course. For rhyme the rudder is of verses.

A sieve. Rudder perch, a small fish with the upper part of the body brown, varied with large round spots of yellow, the belly and sides streaked with lines of white and yellow. This fish is said to follow the rudders of ships in the warm parts of the Atlantic.
ruddiness: The state of being ruddy; redness, or rather a lively flesh color; that degree of redness which characterizes high health; applied chiefly to the complexion or color of the human skin; as the ruddiness of the cheeks or lips.
ruddle: The name of a species of chalk or red earth, colored by iron.
